syllabus_desc: Describe the requirements to hold an aviation document, as laid down in sections 55 and 78 of the CA Act 2023
concept: Sections 55 and 78 of the CA Act 2023 are the backbone of the aviation document system. Section 55 sets the core rule: if an activity requires an aviation document, you can only carry it out if the Director has granted you one (or a person who holds one has authorised you). Section 78 deals with issue, renewal, and variation — the Director must be satisfied you meet the prescribed requirements, that you're a fit and proper person, and that granting the document is in the interests of aviation safety and security. In practice, that means you can't self-certify an IFR flight — you need a valid pilot licence with a current IR, issued by the Director under the Act, and you've got to keep it current. If your document's expired or suspended, you're not legally flying IFR, full stop.
exam_tip: Know the two sections by number. Section 55 = you need a document to carry out the activity. Section 78 = how the Director issues it (prescribed requirements + fit and proper person + interests of aviation safety). ASPEQ loves testing the Act references.
key_facts: CA Act 2023 section 55 -- an activity requiring an aviation document may only be carried out by a holder (or person authorised by a holder)
CA Act 2023 section 78 -- Director issues, renews, or varies the document if prescribed requirements are met, fit and proper person test is passed, and it's in the interests of aviation safety
Without a current, valid document you cannot legally conduct the activity
The Director can impose conditions on any aviation document
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CA Act 2023 sections 55 and 78

syllabus_desc: Describe the duties of the pilot-in-command, as laid down in section 14 of the CA Act 2023
concept: Section 14 of the CA Act 2023 is the PIC duty clause. It says the pilot-in-command of an aircraft is responsible for the safe operation of the aircraft in flight, the safety and wellbeing of passengers and crew, and the safety of cargo carried. It also makes clear the PIC has final authority over the operation of the aircraft while in command. That last bit matters — it means no-one (not your chief pilot, not ATC, not the operator's ops manager) can override a PIC's safety decision in the moment. If you decide the flight's unsafe, that's the call. On the IFR side, it gets real: deciding to divert when the destination goes below minima, declaring an emergency when the alternator packs up at night in IMC, refusing a clearance that would take you into icing you can't handle — all of that is section 14 in action.
exam_tip: Section 14 is short but powerful. Remember two things: (1) PIC is responsible for safe operation, passengers, crew, and cargo; (2) PIC has final authority while in command. Don't confuse with section 15 (airworthiness) or section 13 (general duty).
key_facts: Section 14 of the CA Act 2023 places the PIC in charge of the safe operation of the aircraft
PIC is responsible for passengers, crew, and cargo
PIC has final authority over the operation of the aircraft during flight
This authority cannot be overridden by the operator or ATC in the moment
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CA Act 2023 section 14

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: Act. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: The 'Act' means the Civil Aviation Act 2023.
exam_tip: When CARs refer to 'the Act', they mean the Civil Aviation Act 2023 — the primary NZ aviation legislation.
key_facts: The Act = Civil Aviation Act 2023
The Act is the primary NZ aviation legislation
CARs (Civil Aviation Rules) are made under authority of the Act
source: Civil Aviation Act 2023, s 5 (Interpretation); CAR Part 1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: ADS-B system. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: ADS-B system means a GNSS position source and a compatible Mode S Extended Squitter 1090 MHz ADS-B OUT transponder, or any other suitable transponder determined by the Director.
exam_tip: ADS-B = Automatic Dependent Surveillance – Broadcast. The aircraft broadcasts its GPS position automatically. ADS-B OUT transmits; ADS-B IN receives traffic.
key_facts: ADS-B = Automatic Dependent Surveillance – Broadcast
Requires GNSS position source + Mode S Extended Squitter 1090 MHz transponder
ADS-B OUT broadcasts; ADS-B IN receives
Director may approve other suitable transponders
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: aerodrome control service. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Aerodrome control service means an air traffic control service provided for the control of aerodrome traffic.
exam_tip: Aerodrome control service = ATC for aerodrome traffic (aircraft and vehicles on the manoeuvring area, and aircraft in the circuit).
key_facts: Provides ATC service for aerodrome traffic
Covers aircraft in the circuit and on the manoeuvring area
Provided by an aerodrome control tower
Definition from Civil Aviation Act 2023 s 5
source: Civil Aviation Act 2023, s 5; CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: aerodrome traffic circuit. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Aerodrome traffic circuit means the pattern flown by aircraft operating in the vicinity of an aerodrome.
exam_tip: The circuit pattern — upwind, crosswind, downwind, base, final. Standard circuit is left-hand unless published otherwise.
key_facts: Pattern flown by aircraft operating in the vicinity of an aerodrome
Standard circuit is left-hand
Legs: upwind, crosswind, downwind, base, final
Definition from Civil Aviation Act 2023 s 5
source: Civil Aviation Act 2023, s 5; CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: aeronautical information circular. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Aeronautical information circular (AIC) means a notice containing information that does not qualify for the origination of a NOTAM or for inclusion in the AIP but relates to flight safety, air navigation, technical, administrative, or legislative matters.
exam_tip: AICs are advisory — they don't have the regulatory force of rules, but contain important safety information. They supplement the AIP.
key_facts: Advisory notices supplementing the AIP
Cover: flight safety, air navigation, technical, administrative, legislative matters
Not NOTAMs — more permanent than NOTAMs
Published as part of the integrated AIP service
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: aeronautical information publication. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Aeronautical information publication (AIP) means a publication issued by, or with the authority of, a State and containing aeronautical information of a lasting character essential to air navigation.
exam_tip: The AIP is the primary reference for permanent aeronautical information — divided into GEN (General), ENR (En Route), and AD (Aerodromes) sections.
key_facts: Publication containing permanent aeronautical information essential to air navigation
Issued by or with authority of a State
NZ AIP = AIPNZ, divided into GEN, ENR, and AD sections
Updated by AIP Amendments and Supplements
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: AIP supplement. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: AIP supplement means temporary changes to the information contained in the AIPNZ that are published by means of special pages.
exam_tip: AIP Supplements = temporary changes to the AIP. They use yellow pages for easy identification. When the change becomes permanent, it's incorporated into the AIP proper.
key_facts: Temporary changes to AIPNZ information
Published as special pages (traditionally yellow)
More permanent than NOTAMs but less permanent than AIP amendments
Used for significant temporary changes
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: air traffic control service. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Air traffic control service means a service provided for the purpose of: preventing collisions between aircraft, and between aircraft and obstructions on the manoeuvring area; and expediting and maintaining an orderly flow of air traffic.
exam_tip: ATC has two primary purposes: collision prevention and orderly traffic flow. It includes aerodrome, approach, and area control services.
key_facts: Purpose 1: prevent collisions between aircraft, and between aircraft and obstructions
Purpose 2: expedite and maintain orderly air traffic flow
Includes: aerodrome control, approach control, area control services
Definition from Civil Aviation Act 2023 s 5
source: Civil Aviation Act 2023, s 5; CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: airworthiness certificate. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Airworthiness certificate means, for a NZ registered aircraft, an airworthiness certificate issued by the Director under Part 21; and for a foreign registered aircraft, a certificate issued by the competent authority of the State of registry.
exam_tip: Every aircraft operating IFR must hold a current airworthiness certificate. NZ-registered = Director issues under Part 21; foreign = home State issues.
key_facts: NZ-registered aircraft: issued by Director under Part 21
Foreign-registered aircraft: issued by competent authority of State of registry
Required for all aircraft operations
Standard category is the normal category for commercial/IFR operations
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: alternate aerodrome. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Alternate aerodrome means an aerodrome to which an aircraft may proceed when it becomes either impossible or inadvisable to proceed to or land at the aerodrome of intended landing.
exam_tip: Three types of alternate: take-off alternate (if can't return), en-route alternate (if divert during flight), destination alternate (if destination unusable). IFR requires destination alternate when met conditions apply.
key_facts: An aerodrome to divert to when destination is impossible or inadvisable
Three types: take-off alternate, en-route alternate, destination alternate
IFR destination alternate required unless met conditions are above minima
Must be specified in flight plan
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: alternate means of navigation. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Alternate means of navigation means a navigational system that, for a given operation or phase of flight, will enable the continued safe navigation and landing of the aircraft if the primary means of navigation fails.
exam_tip: AMoN = the backup navigation system. If GNSS is the primary means, DME/DME or VOR/DME may be the alternate means. Ensures continued safe operation if primary nav fails.
key_facts: Backup navigation for use if primary means fails
Must enable continued safe navigation and landing
Operation-specific — depends on the navigation specification
Example: VOR/DME as AMoN when GNSS is primary
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: altitude. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Altitude means the vertical distance of a level, a point, or an object considered as a point, measured from mean sea level.
exam_tip: Altitude is measured from MSL. Remember the A-A rule: Altitude above sea level, referenced to QNH. Distinguish from height (above a datum/aerodrome) and flight level (pressure altitude referenced to 1013.2 hPa).
key_facts: Measured from mean sea level (MSL)
Set using QNH (aerodrome or area QNH)
Used below transition altitude (13,000 ft in NZ)
Distinguish: altitude (MSL) vs height (AGL/datum) vs flight level (1013.2 hPa)
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: approach control service. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Approach control service means an air traffic control service for arriving or departing controlled flights.
exam_tip: Approach control handles the transition between en-route and aerodrome phases — arrivals on the approach and departures climbing to en-route. Typically provided by RADAR or procedural means.
key_facts: ATC service for arriving and departing controlled flights
Provided by an approach control office
Handles the approach/departure phase of flight
Definition from Civil Aviation Act 2023 s 5
source: Civil Aviation Act 2023, s 5; CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: area control service. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Area control service means an air traffic control service provided for controlled flights in controlled airspace.
exam_tip: Area control = en-route ATC. In NZ, provided by Auckland Oceanic and associated area control centres for controlled airspace (CTA, OCTA where applicable).
key_facts: ATC service for controlled flights in controlled airspace
Covers the en-route phase of flight
Provided by an area control centre (ACC)
Definition from Civil Aviation Act 2023 s 5
source: Civil Aviation Act 2023, s 5; CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: area navigation. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Area navigation (RNAV) means a method of navigation which permits aircraft operation on any desired flight path within the coverage of ground- or space-based navigation systems, or within the limits of self-contained systems, or a combination of these.
exam_tip: RNAV allows off-track navigation — aircraft are not constrained to fly over ground-based navaid stations. GNSS is the primary enabler. RNP adds performance monitoring and alerting to RNAV.
key_facts: Navigation on any desired flight path (not just radials/airways)
Uses ground-based navaids (VOR, DME), space-based (GNSS), or self-contained systems
RNAV is the basis for performance-based navigation (PBN)
RNP = RNAV + performance monitoring and alerting
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: ATC clearance. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: ATC clearance means authorisation for an aircraft to proceed under conditions specified by an air traffic control unit.
exam_tip: An ATC clearance is authorisation, not an instruction. It authorises the pilot to do something (e.g. enter controlled airspace, conduct an approach). The PIC remains responsible for safety.
key_facts: Authorisation (not an instruction) for an aircraft to proceed
Issued by an ATC unit
Specifies conditions (route, altitude, squawk, etc.)
PIC remains responsible for safety even with a clearance
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: ATC instruction. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: ATC instruction means a directive issued by ATC for the purpose of requiring a pilot to take a specific action.
exam_tip: ATC instruction = a directive requiring specific action (e.g. 'Turn left heading 180'). Distinguish from a clearance (authorisation to proceed). Instructions must be complied with immediately unless safety is at risk.
key_facts: A directive requiring a pilot to take specific action
Issued by ATC
Must be complied with promptly
Distinct from an ATC clearance (which authorises rather than directs)
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: AWIB service. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: AWIB service means an automatic broadcast of aerodrome and weather information provided specifically for the facilitation of aviation. An AWIB service is not an air traffic service.
exam_tip: AWIB = Automated Weather Information Broadcast. Key point: it is NOT an air traffic service — just an information service. Similar to ATIS but automated. Pilots are not required to acknowledge receipt.
key_facts: Automated broadcast of aerodrome and weather information
NOT an air traffic service — information only
Facilitates aviation without ATC intervention
Pilots should monitor AWIB before departing/arriving at uncontrolled aerodromes
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: barometric vertical navigation (baro-VNAV). AIP GEN. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Barometric vertical navigation (Baro-VNAV) means an approach technique using barometric altitude to provide vertical guidance to a decision altitude on an instrument approach procedure.
exam_tip: Baro-VNAV provides vertical guidance (like an ILS glideslope) using the aircraft's barometer. It requires temperature correction in cold weather. Used for LNAV/VNAV approaches — lowers minima compared to LNAV-only.
key_facts: Vertical guidance derived from barometric altitude
Used on LNAV/VNAV and RNP APCH procedures
Requires temperature correction in cold weather (cold temperatures cause aircraft to fly lower than indicated)
Results in lower minima than LNAV-only approaches
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3; AIP GEN

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: Category II precision approach procedure. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Category II precision approach procedure means a precision instrument approach and landing with a decision height lower than 200 ft but not lower than 100 ft, and a runway visual range not less than 300 m.
exam_tip: CAT II: DH 100–199 ft, RVR ≥300 m. CAT I: DH ≥200 ft, RVR ≥550 m. CAT III: DH <100 ft (or no DH), RVR <300 m. CAT II/III requires special aircraft equipment and crew training.
key_facts: Decision height: lower than 200 ft but not lower than 100 ft
RVR: not less than 300 m
Requires approved aircraft systems, training, and aerodrome facilities
CAT I: DH ≥200 ft, RVR ≥550 m; CAT III: DH <100 ft or no DH
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: ceiling. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Ceiling means the height above ground or water of the base of the lowest layer of cloud below 20,000 ft covering more than half the sky.
exam_tip: Ceiling = cloud base height AGL of the lowest layer covering >4 oktas (broken or overcast). Must be below 20,000 ft. Scattered cloud (≤4 oktas) is not a ceiling.
key_facts: Height AGL of lowest cloud layer covering >4 oktas (BKN or OVC)
Must be below 20,000 ft to qualify as ceiling
SCT (3-4 oktas) does not constitute a ceiling
Critical for determining IFR vs VFR conditions
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: change-over point (COP). AIP GEN. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Change-over point (COP) means the point at which an aircraft navigating on an ATS route segment defined by reference to VOR should transfer its primary navigation reference from the facility behind to the one ahead.
exam_tip: The COP is normally the mid-point between two VORs unless published otherwise. At the COP, tune from the behind VOR to the ahead VOR. Marked on enroute charts.
key_facts: Transfer navigation reference from behind VOR to ahead VOR
Normally at the equidistant point between two VORs unless published otherwise
Marked on IFR en-route charts
Relevant to airways navigation using VOR
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3; AIP GEN

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: clearance limit. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Clearance limit means the point to which an aircraft is granted an ATC clearance.
exam_tip: The clearance limit is where your ATC clearance expires. Often the destination aerodrome. If you reach the clearance limit without a further clearance, you must hold and request further clearance.
key_facts: The point to which an ATC clearance is valid
Often the destination aerodrome
If reached without further clearance: hold and request further clearance
Can be a fix, waypoint, or aerodrome
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: command practice. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Command practice means the performance by a co-pilot of the duties and functions of a pilot-in-command during a flight, under the supervision of a pilot-in-command designated for the purpose by the operator.
exam_tip: Command practice allows a co-pilot to act as PIC under supervision, building towards PIC qualification. The supervising PIC retains full responsibility for safety.
key_facts: Co-pilot performs PIC duties under supervision
Supervising PIC must be designated by the operator
Supervising PIC retains legal responsibility for safety
Used to build hours/experience for PIC command
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: continental (en route). AC91-21. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Continental (en route) means, for a SEIFR operation, that the aircraft is being operated at a point from which a landing can be made at a suitable aerodrome within 30 minutes flying time in still air at normal cruise speed.
exam_tip: Continental en-route defines the operating envelope for SEIFR (single-engine IFR) passenger operations — must always be within 30 min flying time of a suitable aerodrome. Outside this is 'oceanic'.
key_facts: Within 30 minutes flying time of a suitable aerodrome (still air, normal cruise)
Relevant to SEIFR passenger operations
Outside this boundary = oceanic (different rules apply)
Source: AC91-21 (not directly in CAR Part 1)
source: AC91-21; CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: controlled airspace. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Controlled airspace means an airspace of defined dimensions within which an air traffic control service is provided in accordance with airspace classification.
exam_tip: NZ controlled airspace classifications: A (IFR only), C (IFR and VFR, all require ATC clearance), D (IFR and VFR, VFR needs clearance), G (uncontrolled). IFR must operate under ATC in controlled airspace.
key_facts: Airspace of defined dimensions with ATC service
Classified A, C, D, or G in NZ
Class A: IFR only; Class C: IFR + VFR (all need clearance); Class D: IFR + VFR
Class G: uncontrolled (no ATC service)
source: Civil Aviation Act 2023, s 5; CAR Part 71

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: controlled flight. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Controlled flight means any flight that is subject to an air traffic control clearance under the rules.
exam_tip: IFR flights in controlled airspace are controlled flights — they require an ATC clearance. VFR flights in Class C and D airspace are also controlled flights.
key_facts: Any flight subject to an ATC clearance
All IFR flights in controlled airspace are controlled flights
VFR in Class C and D airspace are also controlled flights
Uncontrolled (Class G) IFR = not a controlled flight
source: Civil Aviation Act 2023, s 5; CAR Part 1

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: co-pilot. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Co-pilot means a licensed pilot serving in any piloting capacity other than as pilot-in-command, but does not include a pilot receiving flight instruction from a pilot on board the aircraft.
exam_tip: The co-pilot flies alongside the PIC but is not in command. Note: a student receiving instruction is NOT a co-pilot under this definition — they are a student pilot.
key_facts: Licensed pilot in any piloting capacity other than PIC
Excludes: student receiving instruction from a pilot on board
Must hold appropriate licence and ratings for the operation
May act as PIC in command practice under supervision
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: crew member. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Crew member means a person carried by an aircraft who is assigned by the operator to perform duties associated with the operation of the flight or aircraft during flight time, including flight crew members and flight attendants.
exam_tip: Crew members include flight crew (pilots, flight engineers) and cabin crew (flight attendants). A person carried purely as a passenger is not a crew member.
key_facts: Assigned by the operator to perform duties during flight
Includes: flight crew members and flight attendants
Also includes: persons undergoing or giving flight instruction, flight examiners
Excludes: passengers
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: day. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Day means the hours between the beginning of morning civil twilight (centre of rising sun's disc is 6° below the horizon) and the end of evening civil twilight (centre of setting sun's disc is 6° below the horizon).
exam_tip: Day starts at morning civil twilight (sun 6° below horizon on rising) and ends at evening civil twilight (sun 6° below horizon on setting). Night is outside these hours. This determines when night VFR or IFR currency requirements apply.
key_facts: Begins: morning civil twilight — sun's centre 6° below horizon rising
Ends: evening civil twilight — sun's centre 6° below horizon setting
Night is outside day hours
Civil twilight ≠ sunrise/sunset (which is when sun disc appears on horizon)
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: decision altitude (DA). CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Decision altitude (DA) means a specified altitude, referenced to MSL, in a precision approach at which a missed approach must be initiated if the required visual reference has not been established.
exam_tip: DA is used on precision and APV approaches (ILS, LPV, LNAV/VNAV). Referenced to MSL using QNH. At DA, you must either see the runway environment or go around — no loitering.
key_facts: Referenced to MSL (set using QNH)
Used on precision and APV approaches (ILS, LPV, LNAV/VNAV, RNP APCH)
If visual reference not established at DA: initiate missed approach immediately
Distinct from DH (which is referenced to threshold elevation)
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: decision height (DH). CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Decision height (DH) means a specified height, referenced to the runway threshold, in a precision approach at which a missed approach must be initiated if the required visual reference has not been established.
exam_tip: DH is referenced to the runway threshold (like a radio altimeter reading). Same concept as DA but referenced differently. CAT I ILS: DH ≥200 ft above threshold. CAT II: DH 100–199 ft.
key_facts: Referenced to runway threshold elevation (often using radio altimeter)
Used on precision approaches (ILS, MLS)
CAT I: DH ≥200 ft; CAT II: DH 100–199 ft
Missed approach must begin at DH if visual reference not established
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: dual flight time. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Dual flight time means flight time during which a person is receiving flight instruction from an appropriately licensed and rated pilot on board a dual control aircraft.
exam_tip: Dual flight time = time with an instructor on board a dual-control aircraft. Logged in the 'dual' column of the logbook. Does not count as PIC time unless the student is the sole manipulator of controls AND holds appropriate licence/rating.
key_facts: Receiving instruction from appropriately licensed/rated pilot
Must be a dual control aircraft
Instructor must be on board
Logged in dual column — typically does not count as PIC time
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: enhanced visual imaging system. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Enhanced visual imaging system means technology which uses imaging sensors to display an image of the external scene topography to the flight deck, to see in front of and along the flight path of the aircraft.
exam_tip: EVIS uses sensors (e.g. infrared cameras) to display what's ahead — useful in low visibility. Distinct from synthetic vision (SVS), which uses databases rather than sensors.
key_facts: Uses imaging sensors (e.g. infrared, low-light cameras)
Displays image of external scene on flight deck
Provides forward/flight-path view
Distinct from SVS (which uses databases) — EVIS uses real sensor data
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: final reserve fuel. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Final reserve fuel means the minimum fuel required to provide a margin to secure safe completion of a flight in the event of unplanned manoeuvring in the vicinity of the destination or alternate, and ordinarily remains on board until completion of landing.
exam_tip: Final reserve is the untouchable minimum — it should still be on board after landing. For non-turbine: 45 min. For turbine/helicopters: 30 min. It is NOT used in planning — it's the last-resort margin.
key_facts: Minimum fuel margin for unplanned manoeuvring near destination/alternate
Should remain on board until after landing
Non-turbine aeroplane: 45 minutes at normal cruise
Turbine aeroplane/helicopter: 30 minutes at holding speed
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: flight examiner. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Flight examiner means a person who holds a flight examiner rating issued under Part 61, or is approved by the Director to conduct specific flight tests.
exam_tip: Only designated Flight Examiners can conduct flight tests and issue certificates/endorsements. The examiner's rating specifies which tests they can administer.
key_facts: Holds a flight examiner rating issued under Part 61
Or approved by Director for specific flight tests
Authorised to conduct flight tests and assess competency
Rating specifies which licences/ratings the examiner can test for
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: flight level. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Flight level means a surface of constant atmospheric pressure expressed in terms of hundreds of feet, related to the specific datum 1013.2 hPa, and separated from other such surfaces by specific pressure intervals.
exam_tip: Flight levels are set on 1013.2 hPa (standard pressure). In NZ, use flight levels above the transition altitude (13,000 ft). FL150 = 15,000 ft on standard pressure. FL45 = 4,500 ft on standard.
key_facts: Constant pressure surface referenced to 1013.2 hPa (standard pressure)
Expressed in hundreds of feet (FL150 = 15,000 ft on standard pressure)
Used above the transition level (FL150 in NZ)
Set altimeter to 1013 hPa when climbing through transition altitude (13,000 ft NZ)
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: GNSS database. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: GNSS database means an electronic memory containing information on airports, navigation aids, reporting points, SIDs, STARs, instrument approaches, special use airspace, and other items of value to the pilot.
exam_tip: The GNSS database must be current for IFR operations. It contains all the navigation data loaded into the FMS/GPS. Currency is checked against the expiry date on the database cycle (usually 28-day AIRAC cycles).
key_facts: Electronic memory with airports, navaids, waypoints, procedures
Includes SIDs, STARs, instrument approaches
Must be current for IFR use
Updated on AIRAC cycles (every 28 days)
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: height. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Height means the vertical distance of a level, a point, or an object considered as a point, measured from a specified datum, and includes the vertical dimension of an object.
exam_tip: Height is measured from a specified datum (e.g. aerodrome elevation, runway threshold, or terrain). Set using QFE (field elevation) or calculated as AGL. Distinguish from altitude (MSL) and flight level (1013.2 hPa).
key_facts: Measured from a specified datum (not necessarily MSL)
Includes the vertical dimension of an object
Often expressed as AGL (above ground level)
DH and obstacle heights use height referenced to threshold/ground
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: IFR flight. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: IFR flight means a flight conducted in accordance with the instrument flight rules.
exam_tip: IFR flights follow Part 91 Subpart F rules. In IMC or above transition altitude, IFR is mandatory. Even in VMC, a pilot may elect to fly IFR. IFR flights require ATC clearance in controlled airspace.
key_facts: A flight conducted under the instrument flight rules (Part 91 Subpart F)
Required when in IMC or at/above transition altitude
May be elected in VMC ('VFR on top' not permitted — must be IFR in Class A)
Requires ATC clearance in controlled airspace
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: instrument approach procedure. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Instrument approach procedure means a series of predetermined manoeuvres by reference to flight instruments, with specified protection from obstacles, from the initial approach fix to a point from which a landing can be completed and, if a landing is not completed, to a holding or en-route position.
exam_tip: An IAP is the published procedure from the IAF through the intermediate and final approach segments to the MDA/DA, then either land or missed approach. All segments have obstacle clearance criteria.
key_facts: Series of predetermined manoeuvres by reference to instruments
Has specified obstacle protection
Starts at the Initial Approach Fix (IAF)
Ends at landing point or missed approach to holding/en-route position
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: instrument flight. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Instrument flight means flight during which an aircraft is piloted solely by reference to instruments and without external reference points.
exam_tip: Instrument flight = piloting without visual reference outside. The key criterion is 'solely by reference to instruments'. Contrast with instrument flight time, which covers both actual IMC and simulated instrument flight.
key_facts: Piloted solely by reference to flight instruments
No external (visual) reference points used
Occurs in IMC or when deliberately excluding external references
Distinct from instrument flight time (which includes simulated)
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: instrument flight time. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Instrument flight time means time during which an aircraft is piloted solely by reference to instruments and without external reference points.
exam_tip: Instrument flight time = actual IMC time logged during flight. Required for IR currency (Part 61 rule 61.807 requires 3 instrument approaches and 3 hours instrument flight time in 12 months).
key_facts: Time piloted solely by instruments, no external references
Logged in the 'instrument' column of the logbook
Required for IR currency maintenance
Distinct from instrument time (which includes simulated device time)
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: instrument meteorological conditions. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Instrument meteorological conditions (IMC) means meteorological conditions expressed in terms of visibility, distance from cloud, and ceiling, less than the minima specified for visual meteorological conditions.
exam_tip: IMC = conditions BELOW VMC minima. When in IMC, IFR flight rules apply. VMC minima vary by airspace class. In Class C/D: 5 km vis, 1500 m horizontal/300 ft vertical from cloud, ceiling 1000 ft.
key_facts: Conditions below the VMC minima thresholds
Defined by: visibility, distance from cloud, and ceiling
When in IMC: IFR rules apply (must be IFR rated)
VMC minima vary by airspace class (Class C/D: 5 km, 1500 m/300 ft)
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: instrument time. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Instrument time includes instrument flight time and time during which a pilot is practising simulated instrument flight on an approved flight simulation training device.
exam_tip: Instrument time is broader than instrument flight time — it includes FSTD/simulator time. Can be used for IR currency. Up to half of the required instrument approach currency may be done in an FSTD.
key_facts: Includes actual instrument flight time (in IMC)
Also includes simulated instrument flight on an approved FSTD
Logged in logbook as 'instrument time'
FSTD time can count towards IR currency requirements
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: Mach number. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Mach number means the ratio of true airspeed to the speed of sound.
exam_tip: Mach number = TAS ÷ speed of sound. Speed of sound varies with temperature (decreases with altitude in troposphere, constant in lower stratosphere). M0.83 is the upper holding speed above FL340.
key_facts: Mach = TAS ÷ local speed of sound
Speed of sound: ~340 m/s (661 kt) at ISA sea level; ~295 m/s (573 kt) at FL360
Used for high-altitude operations where IAS becomes unreliable
Upper holding speed limit: M0.83 above FL340
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: minimum descent altitude (MDA). CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Minimum descent altitude (MDA) means a specified altitude, referenced to MSL, in a non-precision or circling approach below which descent may not be made without visual reference.
exam_tip: MDA applies to non-precision approaches (NDB, VOR, LNAV). Unlike DA, you can fly level at MDA to look for the runway. Must go around if visual reference not established before the missed approach point.
key_facts: Referenced to MSL (set using QNH)
Used on non-precision approaches (NDB, VOR, LNAV) and circling approaches
May not descend below MDA without visual reference
Can fly level at MDA; must go around if not visual before the MAP
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: minimum descent height (MDH). CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Minimum descent height (MDH) means a specified height, referenced to the runway threshold, in a non-precision or circling approach below which descent may not be made without visual reference.
exam_tip: MDH = same as MDA but referenced to the runway threshold. Same concept — used in NDB/VOR approaches. MDH is the height equivalent of MDA.
key_facts: Referenced to runway threshold elevation
Used on non-precision approaches and circling approaches
Height equivalent of MDA (MDA is referenced to MSL)
Cannot descend below MDH without visual reference
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: minimum safe altitude (MSA). AIP GEN. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Minimum safe altitude (MSA) means, for an area surrounding an aerodrome, an altitude below which flight is not safe due to terrain or obstacles, usually providing 300 m (1000 ft) clearance within a defined radius.
exam_tip: MSA is shown on approach charts as sectors with minimum safe altitudes within 25 NM (or 46 km) of the reference point. It guarantees terrain clearance but NOT navigation signal coverage or ATC separation.
key_facts: Minimum altitude ensuring terrain/obstacle clearance in a defined area
Usually 300 m (1000 ft) clearance over highest obstacle in sector
Shown on approach charts in sectors around the aerodrome reference point
Does NOT guarantee navaid coverage or ATC separation
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3; AIP GEN

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: minimum sector altitude (MSA 25M). AIP GEN. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Minimum sector altitude (MSA 25M) means the lowest altitude which may be used, within a specified sector around the aerodrome, providing 300 m (1000 ft) of clearance above all objects in that sector within a radius of 25 NM (46 km).
exam_tip: MSA 25M appears on approach charts as pie-chart sectors. The '25M' means 25 nautical miles radius. Each sector has its own MSA. Use the sector your aircraft is in for emergency terrain clearance.
key_facts: Lowest safe altitude in a defined sector within 25 NM (46 km) of reference
Provides 300 m (1000 ft) clearance above highest obstacle in that sector
Published on approach charts as pie sectors
Emergency reference only — not for normal approach use
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3; AIP GEN

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: navigation specification. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Navigation specification means a set of aircraft and operator requirements needed to support performance-based navigation operations within a defined airspace, including RNP specifications (with monitoring/alerting) and RNAV specifications (without monitoring/alerting).
exam_tip: Navigation specifications define the PBN requirements for a procedure or airspace. RNP specs = RNAV + performance monitoring and alerting. RNAV specs = area navigation without the monitoring requirement.
key_facts: Defines PBN requirements for a procedure/airspace
RNP specification: RNAV + performance monitoring and alerting
RNAV specification: area navigation without monitoring/alerting requirement
Examples: RNP APCH, RNP AR APCH, RNAV 1, RNAV 2
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: night. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Night means the hours between the end of evening civil twilight and the beginning of morning civil twilight — i.e., when the centre of the sun's disc is more than 6° below the horizon.
exam_tip: Night is the complement of 'day'. Starts when evening civil twilight ends (sun >6° below horizon). Important for determining night currency, lighting requirements, and passenger carrying privileges.
key_facts: Begins: end of evening civil twilight (sun's centre >6° below horizon on setting)
Ends: beginning of morning civil twilight (sun's centre 6° below horizon on rising)
Night currency required for IFR operations at night
Lighting requirements (nav lights, anti-collision) apply at night
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: NOTAM. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: NOTAM means a notice distributed by means of telecommunication containing information concerning the establishment, condition, or change in any aeronautical facility, service, procedure, or hazard, the timely knowledge of which is essential to personnel concerned with flight operations.
exam_tip: NOTAMs are time-critical notices — check before every flight. They cover temporary changes to nav facilities, airspace, aerodromes, hazards. NOTAMs expire when the condition ends or at the stated end time.
key_facts: Notice distributed by telecommunication
Covers: facilities, services, procedures, hazards
Timely knowledge is essential for flight operations
Check NOTAMs before every flight via AIS (Aeropath in NZ)
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: pilot-in-command (PIC). CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Pilot-in-command (PIC) means the pilot on board the aircraft who is responsible for the operation and safety of the aircraft.
exam_tip: The PIC has final authority and responsibility. Even in multi-crew operations, one pilot is designated PIC. The PIC cannot delegate their responsibility to ATC — they may breach rules in a safety emergency.
key_facts: Pilot on board responsible for operation and safety of the aircraft
Has final authority over the aircraft
Cannot transfer PIC responsibility to another party
In emergencies, may deviate from rules as necessary for safety (CA Act s 15)
source: Civil Aviation Act 2023, s 5; CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: performance-based navigation. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Performance-based navigation (PBN) means area navigation based on performance requirements for aircraft operating along an ATS route, on an instrument approach procedure, or in a designated airspace.
exam_tip: PBN = umbrella term for modern RNAV/RNP navigation. It specifies performance in terms of accuracy, integrity, continuity, and availability — rather than specifying the sensor (GPS, DME, etc.).
key_facts: RNAV-based navigation with defined performance requirements
Covers: ATS routes, instrument approaches, and designated airspace
Performance criteria: accuracy, integrity, continuity, availability
Two families: RNAV specs (no alerting) and RNP specs (with alerting)
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: precision approach procedure. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Precision approach procedure means an instrument approach procedure utilising azimuth and glide path information.
exam_tip: Precision = both lateral (azimuth) AND vertical (glidepath) guidance. Examples: ILS, MLS, GLS. Non-precision = lateral guidance only (NDB, VOR, LNAV). APV = vertical guidance but doesn't meet precision criteria (LPV, LNAV/VNAV).
key_facts: Uses both azimuth (lateral) and glidepath (vertical) guidance
Examples: ILS (most common), MLS, GLS
Has a DH (not MDA) and a DA (not MDA)
Distinct from APV (vertical guidance but not classified as precision)
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: pressure altitude. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Pressure altitude means an atmospheric pressure, expressed in terms of altitude, which corresponds to that pressure in the standard atmosphere.
exam_tip: Pressure altitude is the altitude indicated when the altimeter is set to 1013.2 hPa. Used for flight levels. At MSL in ISA, pressure altitude = 0. Used for performance calculations and above transition altitude.
key_facts: Altitude corresponding to a given pressure in the standard atmosphere
Read when altimeter is set to 1013.2 hPa
Used for flight levels above transition altitude
ISA standard: 1013.25 hPa at MSL, decreasing ~1 hPa per 28 ft
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: primary-means navigation system. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Primary-means navigation system means a navigation system approved for a given navigation specification, or for a phase of flight, that is used to navigate safely on the planned route or procedure.
exam_tip: The primary means is the main navigation system — e.g. GNSS for RNP APCH. If the primary fails, you revert to the alternate means of navigation. The primary system must meet the navigation specification requirements.
key_facts: Main navigation system approved for the navigation specification or phase of flight
Must meet the accuracy/integrity requirements of the operation
If primary fails: revert to alternate means of navigation
Example: GNSS as primary for RNP APCH
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: procedure altitude. AIP GEN. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Procedure altitude means an altitude specified in an instrument approach procedure that is used as a step-down fix or intermediate crossing altitude, above which the aircraft must be at that point during the approach.
exam_tip: Procedure altitudes appear as 'AT OR ABOVE' constraints on approach charts. They ensure terrain clearance during segment transitions. Fly at or above the procedure altitude when crossing that fix.
key_facts: Altitude specified at a fix during an instrument approach
Aircraft must be AT OR ABOVE this altitude when crossing the fix
Ensures terrain/obstacle clearance during approach segments
Shown on approach charts at each fix crossing
source: AIP GEN; CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: rated coverage. AIP GEN. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Rated coverage means the published service volume of a navigation aid within which the facility meets its specified performance criteria for accuracy, integrity, and signal strength.
exam_tip: Navaids have a rated coverage area — outside this, the signal may not meet the required accuracy. For IFR, you must be within the rated coverage of the navaid you are using.
key_facts: Service volume of a navaid meeting specified performance criteria
Defines the usable range of accuracy and signal strength
IFR use requires being within rated coverage
VOR rated coverage varies: terminal, low, and high altitude classes
source: AIP GEN; CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: RAIM warning. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: RAIM warning means a warning that the integrity of the navigation position solution from GNSS satellites may be unreliable.
exam_tip: RAIM = Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring. A RAIM warning means you cannot rely on the GPS position for navigation — you must revert to alternate means. Critical for IFR: check RAIM availability before flight.
key_facts: Warning that GNSS integrity may be unreliable
Generated by the receiver when it detects satellite geometry is inadequate
If RAIM warning: revert to alternate means of navigation
RAIM must be predicted available for the entire IFR operation
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: rating. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Rating means an authorisation entered on or associated with a licence or certificate, stating special conditions, privileges, or limitations relating to that licence or certificate.
exam_tip: A rating extends a licence's privileges — e.g. an Instrument Rating on a PPL or CPL. Ratings can be added to or removed from a licence. The rating must be current to exercise its privileges.
key_facts: Authorisation associated with a licence or certificate
States special conditions, privileges, or limitations
Examples: Instrument Rating, Type Rating, Night Rating
Must be current (valid and met recency requirements) to use its privileges
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: reporting point. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Reporting point means a specified, named geographical location in relation to which the position of an aircraft can be reported.
exam_tip: Reporting points are named waypoints (usually 5-letter codes) at which pilots report their position. Compulsory reporting points must be reported without request; non-compulsory are reported only on request.
key_facts: Named geographical location for position reporting
Two types: compulsory (triangle symbol) and non-compulsory (open triangle)
Compulsory: must report when reaching without ATC request
Non-compulsory: report only when requested by ATC
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: RNP performance. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: RNP performance means a containment value, expressed as a distance in nautical miles from the intended position, within which flights would be for at least 95% of the total flying time.
exam_tip: RNP X means the aircraft stays within X NM of the intended track for ≥95% of flight time. RNP 0.3 = within 0.3 NM for ≥95% of time. The system also monitors this and alerts if it cannot maintain performance.
key_facts: Containment value in NM from intended position
Aircraft within this value for ≥95% of flying time
The RNP number defines the containment distance (e.g. RNP 0.3 = 0.3 NM)
Includes monitoring and alerting of performance degradation
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: runway visual range. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Runway visual range (RVR) means the range over which the pilot of an aircraft on the centreline of a runway can see the runway surface markings or the lights delineating the runway or identifying its centreline.
exam_tip: RVR is measured by instruments (transmissometers) and reported in metres. It is not the same as visibility — RVR is specific to the runway environment. CAT I minima may quote RVR ≥550 m.
key_facts: Measured from the runway centreline looking along the runway
Based on seeing runway markings or lights
Measured instrumentally in metres (not the same as met visibility)
CAT I: RVR ≥550 m; CAT II: RVR ≥300 m; CAT IIIA: RVR ≥175 m
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: segment OCA. AIP GEN. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Segment OCA means the obstacle clearance altitude for a specific segment of an instrument approach procedure, below which an aircraft may not descend in that segment.
exam_tip: Each segment of an approach (initial, intermediate, final, missed approach) has its own OCA. The published MDA/DA is derived from the highest segment OCA. Understanding segment OCAs is important for obstacle clearance analysis.
key_facts: Obstacle clearance altitude for a specific approach segment
Each segment has its own OCA (initial, intermediate, final, missed approach)
The MDA/DA is based on the most critical segment OCA
Pilots use the published MDA/DA — segment OCAs are for procedure designers
source: AIP GEN; CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: SEIFR passenger operation. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: SEIFR passenger operation means an air transport operation carrying passengers in a single-engine aeroplane under IFR.
exam_tip: SEIFR = Single Engine IFR. To carry passengers under IFR in a single-engine aeroplane, the operation must meet SEIFR requirements (Part 91). Continental operations must always be within 30 min of a suitable aerodrome.
key_facts: Air transport carrying passengers in a single-engine aeroplane under IFR
Must comply with SEIFR requirements in Part 91
Continental SEIFR: within 30 min flying time of a suitable aerodrome
Specific equipment and operator requirements apply
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: synthetic vision system. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Synthetic vision system (SVS) means a system that uses computer-generated images of the external scene topography, derived from aircraft attitude, high-precision navigation, and terrain/obstacle databases, to display a synthetic image of the external scene on the flight deck.
exam_tip: SVS uses databases (terrain, obstacles, cultural features) to generate a 3D synthetic image of what should be outside — even in cloud. Distinct from EVIS (which uses sensors to show what IS outside). SVS does not replace instrument flying but aids situational awareness.
key_facts: Computer-generated image from attitude, nav solution, and databases
Uses terrain, obstacle, and cultural feature databases
Displays from the flight deck perspective
Distinct from EVIS (sensor-based real image vs. database-derived synthetic image)
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: transition altitude. AIP GEN. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Transition altitude means the altitude at or below which the vertical position of an aircraft is controlled by reference to altitudes set on QNH. In NZ, the transition altitude is 13,000 ft.
exam_tip: At or below 13,000 ft in NZ = use QNH (altitudes). Climbing through 13,000 ft: switch to 1013.2 hPa and report in flight levels. The transition altitude is the lower boundary of the transition layer.
key_facts: NZ transition altitude = 13,000 ft
At or below: use QNH (altitudes)
Climbing through 13,000 ft: set 1013.2 hPa, switch to flight levels
Defined in AIP ENR 1.07
source: AIP ENR 1.07; CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: transition layer. AIP GEN. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Transition layer means the airspace between the transition altitude and the transition level. In NZ, the transition layer is between 13,000 ft (QNH) and FL150 (1013.2 hPa).
exam_tip: Aircraft may be in the transition layer when climbing or descending. When climbing: use FL. When descending: use altitude (switch to QNH at FL150). Aircraft must not cruise level in the transition layer.
key_facts: NZ transition layer = 13,000 ft to FL150
Climbing aircraft: use flight levels in the transition layer
Descending aircraft: switch to QNH altitude at or passing FL150
No cruising permitted in the transition layer
source: AIP ENR 1.07; CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: transition level. AIP GEN. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Transition level means the lowest flight level available for use above the transition altitude. In NZ, the transition level is normally FL150.
exam_tip: FL150 is the normal transition level in NZ. However, if area QNH is ≤980 hPa, the transition level is raised to FL160. Always check area QNH to confirm the applicable transition level.
key_facts: Lowest flight level available above the transition altitude
NZ normal transition level = FL150
If area QNH ≤980 hPa: transition level raised to FL160
Defined in AIP ENR 1.07
source: AIP ENR 1.07; CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: VFR flight. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: VFR flight means a flight conducted in accordance with the visual flight rules.
exam_tip: VFR flights follow Part 91 Subpart D rules. VFR requires VMC. Above transition altitude, IFR is required (Class A airspace). In Class G uncontrolled airspace, VFR is the default rule set.
key_facts: A flight conducted under the visual flight rules (Part 91 Subpart D)
Requires VMC conditions
Class A airspace = IFR only (VFR prohibited)
Above transition altitude (13,000 ft) = must be IFR in NZ
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: visibility. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Visibility means the ability, as determined by atmospheric conditions and expressed in units of measurement, to see and identify prominent unlighted objects by day and prominent lighted objects by night.
exam_tip: Visibility is a met element reported in km or m (SM in some contexts). Reported visibility is the prevailing meteorological visibility. For IFR, check the reported visibility against the approach minima RVR/Vis requirements.
key_facts: Ability to see and identify prominent objects by day and lit objects at night
Expressed in km or metres (flight visibility in km)
Prevailing visibility = reported in METAR/TAF
Distinct from RVR (RVR is specific to the runway, measured instrumentally)
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: visual meteorological conditions. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Visual meteorological conditions (VMC) means meteorological conditions expressed in terms of visibility, distance from cloud, and ceiling, equal to or better than specified minima.
exam_tip: VMC minima vary by airspace class. Class C/D: 5 km vis, 1500 m horizontal / 300 ft vertical from cloud, ceiling 1000 ft. Below VMC = IMC. Above transition altitude all airspace is Class A — IFR required.
key_facts: Conditions at or above the VMC minima thresholds
Three elements: visibility, distance from cloud, ceiling
Class C/D VMC: 5 km vis, 1500 m horizontal / 300 ft vertical from cloud
When conditions fall below VMC minima = IMC — IFR rules apply
source: CAR Part 1, rule 1.3 definitions

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: visual reference. AIP GEN. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Visual reference means the visual contact with the ground or water, or with the runway environment (including approach lighting), sufficient to allow the pilot to assess and maintain a safe landing.
exam_tip: Visual reference is what the pilot must establish at or before MDA/DA to continue below the IFR minima. It includes the approach lighting system, runway threshold, or markings — not just any ground contact.
key_facts: Visual contact with ground/water OR runway environment
Must be sufficient to assess and maintain a safe landing
Runway environment includes: approach lights, threshold, markings, touchdown zone
If visual reference not established at MDA/DA: go around is mandatory
source: AIP GEN; CAR Part 1, rule 1.3

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: ABAS. AC91-21. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: ABAS = Aircraft-Based Augmentation System. A GNSS augmentation method that uses information obtained on board the aircraft to improve GNSS integrity or accuracy.
exam_tip: ABAS includes RAIM (Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring). The receiver uses redundant satellite signals to self-monitor integrity. No ground infrastructure required.
key_facts: ABAS = Aircraft-Based Augmentation System
Improves GNSS integrity/accuracy using onboard processing
RAIM is the most common form of ABAS
No external ground infrastructure required
source: AC91-21; AIP GEN; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: ACAS. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: ACAS = Airborne Collision Avoidance System. An aircraft system that monitors the airspace around an aircraft for other transponder-equipped aircraft and provides Traffic Advisories (TAs) and Resolution Advisories (RAs).
exam_tip: ACAS is the generic ICAO term; TCAS II is the specific implementation. RAs must be followed immediately — they take priority over ATC instructions. Notify ATC if you follow an RA.
key_facts: ACAS = Airborne Collision Avoidance System
Provides Traffic Advisories (TAs) and Resolution Advisories (RAs)
TCAS II is the common ACAS implementation
RA instructions override ATC — notify ATC immediately if following an RA
source: CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: ADF. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: ADF = Automatic Direction Finder. A radio receiver that displays the bearing to an NDB (Non-Directional Beacon), used for non-precision approaches and en-route navigation.
exam_tip: ADF points to the NDB station. The relative bearing shown is relative to the aircraft heading. ADF is subject to interference (night effect, coastal refraction, thunderstorms). NDB/ADF approaches are non-precision (MDA, not DA).
key_facts: ADF = Automatic Direction Finder
Receives NDB (Non-Directional Beacon) signals and displays bearing
Bearing is relative to aircraft heading
Subject to errors: night effect, coastal refraction, thunderstorm interference
source: CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: ADS-B. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: ADS-B = Automatic Dependent Surveillance – Broadcast. A surveillance technology where aircraft automatically broadcast their GPS position, altitude, speed, and identification.
exam_tip: ADS-B = Automatic (no pilot input), Dependent (depends on GNSS), Surveillance (for ATC), Broadcast (one-way, like a beacon). ADS-B OUT transmits; ADS-B IN receives. Required in Class A/C airspace in NZ.
key_facts: ADS-B = Automatic Dependent Surveillance – Broadcast
Aircraft broadcasts GPS position, altitude, speed, identity
OUT = transmit; IN = receive traffic information
Required equipment in designated NZ airspace (Class A/C)
source: CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: ADS-C. AIP GEN. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: ADS-C = Automatic Dependent Surveillance – Contract. A surveillance system where the aircraft automatically transmits data to ATC based on a contract (agreement) between the aircraft and a ground system.
exam_tip: ADS-C is used in oceanic/remote airspace where radar coverage is unavailable. Unlike ADS-B (broadcast to all), ADS-C is a point-to-point data link between the aircraft and a specific ATC centre. Used in FANS-equipped aircraft.
key_facts: ADS-C = Automatic Dependent Surveillance – Contract
Point-to-point link between aircraft and specific ATC centre
Used in oceanic/remote airspace without radar
Requires FANS (Future Air Navigation System) equipment
source: AIP GEN; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: AMoN. AC91-21. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: AMoN = Alternate Means of Navigation. The backup navigation system for use if the primary means of navigation fails.
exam_tip: AMoN is the fallback if primary navigation (e.g. GNSS) fails. For RNP APCH, AMoN might be VOR/DME or ILS if available. Having an AMoN is a requirement for many IFR operations.
key_facts: AMoN = Alternate Means of Navigation
Backup navigation if primary (e.g. GNSS) fails
Must enable continued safe navigation and landing
Required for many PBN operations
source: AC91-21; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: ANP. AC91-21. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: ANP = Actual Navigation Performance. The current navigation performance actually being achieved, expressed as a circular error in nautical miles (95% containment).
exam_tip: ANP is displayed on some FMS as an actual position error estimate. If ANP exceeds the RNP value, the system will alert. ANP must be ≤ RNP to continue the procedure.
key_facts: ANP = Actual Navigation Performance
Current position error estimate (95% containment value in NM)
Displayed on some FMS/GPS units
If ANP > RNP value: RAIM alert / cannot continue procedure
source: AC91-21; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: APCH. AIP GEN. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: APCH = Approach. Abbreviation used on charts and in ATC communications to denote the approach phase or approach control.
exam_tip: APCH appears on charts (e.g. 'RNP APCH') and in phraseology (e.g. 'Contact Approach on 119.1'). Also used as a leg type in FMS for RF legs (Radius to Fix) on curved approaches.
key_facts: APCH = Approach
Used on charts and ATC communications
Appears in navigation specifications: e.g. RNP APCH
Common FMS leg type designation
source: AIP GEN; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: A-RNP. AC91-21. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: A-RNP = Advanced RNP. A navigation specification that includes several optional capabilities not in basic RNP APCH, including RF legs, scalable RNP, parallel offset, and fixed-radius transitions.
exam_tip: A-RNP adds capabilities beyond standard RNP — notably RF (Radius to Fix) legs that allow curved flight paths. Requires specific FMS approval. Used for complex approach procedures at difficult aerodromes.
key_facts: A-RNP = Advanced Required Navigation Performance
Includes RF (Radius to Fix) legs — curved approach paths
Also: scalable RNP, parallel offset, fixed-radius transitions
Requires specific aircraft and FMS approval
source: AC91-21; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: Baro-VNAV. AIP GEN. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: Baro-VNAV = Barometric Vertical Navigation. An approach technique that uses the aircraft's barometric altimeter to provide vertical guidance, used on LNAV/VNAV approach procedures.
exam_tip: Baro-VNAV provides a virtual glidepath using the altimeter. Temperature compensation is required below +5°C (cold temperatures cause the aircraft to fly lower than indicated altitude). Results in lower minima than LNAV-only.
key_facts: Baro-VNAV = Barometric Vertical Navigation
Vertical guidance derived from barometric altimeter
Used on LNAV/VNAV approach procedures
Temperature correction required in cold weather
source: AIP GEN; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: DA. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: DA = Decision Altitude. The altitude, referenced to MSL and set using QNH, at which a missed approach must be initiated if the required visual reference has not been established during a precision or APV approach.
exam_tip: DA is used on precision and APV approaches (ILS, LPV, LNAV/VNAV). At DA: visual reference = continue; no visual reference = immediate go-around. DA is referenced to MSL (unlike DH which is referenced to threshold).
key_facts: DA = Decision Altitude
Referenced to MSL (set using QNH)
Used on precision and APV approaches
Missed approach mandatory if visual reference not established at DA
source: CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: DF. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: DF = Direction Finding. A radio navigation technique where ground-based equipment determines the bearing of an aircraft's transmission and provides homing or bearing information.
exam_tip: DF is an emergency service — ATC uses it to locate aircraft in distress or with navigation failure. The pilot transmits on the guard frequency and the DF station takes a bearing. Not used for normal navigation.
key_facts: DF = Direction Finding
Ground-based bearing determination from aircraft transmission
Used for emergency location and homing
Pilot transmits; ground station determines bearing to aircraft
source: CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: DME. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: DME = Distance Measuring Equipment. A radio navigation system that provides slant-range distance from an aircraft to a ground-based DME transponder.
exam_tip: DME gives slant range — not horizontal distance. The difference is significant at low altitude near the station. Many NZ VOR stations co-locate a DME. DME/DME is used as an alternate means of navigation when GNSS fails.
key_facts: DME = Distance Measuring Equipment
Provides slant-range distance to ground station
Slant range ≠ horizontal distance (error decreases with range)
Used for en-route navigation, approaches, and as AMoN
source: CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: FAF. AIP GEN. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: FAF = Final Approach Fix. The fix from which the final approach segment of an instrument approach procedure begins, and the point at which descent to MDA or DA is commenced.
exam_tip: The FAF is where you are established on final and begin the final descent. For precision approaches, the FAF is where the glidepath intercept occurs. For non-precision, the FAF triggers the descent to MDA.
key_facts: FAF = Final Approach Fix
Start of the final approach segment
Descent to MDA (non-precision) or DA (precision) begins here
Marked on approach charts — cross at or above the crossing altitude
source: AIP GEN; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: FAP. AIP GEN. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: FAP = Final Approach Point. On a precision approach, the point where the final approach segment begins (where the aircraft intercepts the glidepath/glideslope from above).
exam_tip: FAP is the precision approach equivalent of the FAF. It's where the glidepath intercept occurs. On an ILS, this is where the glideslope needle centres when intercepted from below (ideally).
key_facts: FAP = Final Approach Point
Used on precision approaches
Where the glidepath/glideslope intercept occurs
Equivalent to FAF in non-precision approaches
source: AIP GEN; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: FAS. AC91-21. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: FAS = Final Approach Segment. The segment of an instrument approach procedure in which alignment and descent for landing are accomplished.
exam_tip: The FAS is the last segment of the approach — from the FAF/FAP to the runway. It includes the glidepath/glideslope descent and ends at the MDA/DA or missed approach point.
key_facts: FAS = Final Approach Segment
Segment for final alignment and descent to land
Begins at FAF (non-precision) or FAP (precision)
Ends at MDA/MAP or DA
source: AC91-21; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: FD. AC91-21. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: FD = Fault Detection. A GNSS integrity monitoring function that detects when a satellite or the navigation solution is faulty, but without the ability to exclude the faulty satellite (unlike FDE).
exam_tip: FD detects faults but can't exclude the faulty satellite — the system will alert but cannot continue the procedure. FDE (Fault Detection and Exclusion) goes further and can exclude the faulty satellite, allowing the procedure to continue.
key_facts: FD = Fault Detection
Detects faulty GNSS satellite or navigation solution
Cannot exclude the fault — system alerts and navigation degrades
FDE = Fault Detection and Exclusion (more capable — can exclude and continue)
source: AC91-21; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: FDE. AC91-21. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: FDE = Fault Detection and Exclusion. A GNSS integrity function that both detects and excludes a faulty satellite from the navigation solution, allowing the operation to continue.
exam_tip: FDE is more capable than FD — it not only detects a bad satellite but removes it and continues with the remaining satellites. Required for many precision GNSS approaches where RAIM alone is insufficient.
key_facts: FDE = Fault Detection and Exclusion
Detects AND excludes faulty satellite from navigation solution
Allows operation to continue on remaining satellites
More capable than FD (which only detects)
source: AC91-21; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: FRT. AC91-21. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: FRT = Fixed Radius Transition. A navigation leg type used in RNP procedures where the aircraft flies a curved path at a constant radius (turn), typically used for departure or transition segments.
exam_tip: FRT is a fixed radius turn — the aircraft tracks a curve of specified radius. Requires RNP AR capability. Allows procedures to be designed with precise curved paths around terrain or controlled airspace.
key_facts: FRT = Fixed Radius Transition
Aircraft tracks a curved path at a constant radius
Requires RNP AR APCH capability
Allows complex curved paths in terminal procedures
source: AC91-21; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: GBAS. AC91-21. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: GBAS = Ground-Based Augmentation System. A ground-based system that improves GNSS accuracy and integrity within a local area (typically within 50 km) to support precision approaches — also known as GLS.
exam_tip: GBAS/GLS can support CAT I, II, and III precision approaches. It uses multiple ground reference receivers and broadcasts corrections and integrity data via VHF to aircraft. More flexible than ILS — one system can serve multiple runways.
key_facts: GBAS = Ground-Based Augmentation System
Local area GNSS augmentation (within ~50 km)
Supports CAT I/II/III precision approaches (GLS)
Broadcasts differential corrections via VHF to aircraft
source: AC91-21; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: GBNA. AC91-21. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: GBNA = GNSS-Based Navigation Approval. Approval from the Director for an operator to conduct GNSS-based navigation operations, including IFR operations using GNSS as the primary means of navigation.
exam_tip: GBNA is the NZ-specific approval for GNSS IFR operations. The operator must demonstrate that the GNSS system meets the required navigation performance. Required for RNP operations in NZ.
key_facts: GBNA = GNSS-Based Navigation Approval
NZ Director approval for GNSS IFR operations
Required for operators using GNSS as primary navigation
Operator must demonstrate compliance with navigation performance requirements
source: AC91-21; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: GLS. AC91-21. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: GLS = GNSS Landing System. A precision approach system using GBAS (ground-based augmentation) to provide GNSS-based guidance equivalent to ILS CAT I or better.
exam_tip: GLS uses GBAS to provide precision approach guidance. One GBAS ground station can serve multiple runway ends — unlike ILS (one system per runway end). GLS approaches have LPV minima or better.
key_facts: GLS = GNSS Landing System
Precision approach using GBAS augmentation
One ground station can support multiple runways
CAT I or better minima
source: AC91-21; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: GNSS. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: GNSS = Global Navigation Satellite System. A generic term for satellite-based navigation systems including GPS (USA), GLONASS (Russia), Galileo (Europe), and BeiDou (China).
exam_tip: GNSS is the umbrella term; GPS is the US system. NZ aviation uses GPS-based GNSS for primary navigation. GNSS for IFR requires augmentation (RAIM, WAAS/SBAS) to meet integrity requirements.
key_facts: GNSS = Global Navigation Satellite System
Includes: GPS (USA), GLONASS (Russia), Galileo (EU), BeiDou (China)
GPS is the primary GNSS used in NZ aviation
IFR GNSS requires augmentation for integrity (RAIM, SBAS)
source: CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: GPS. AC91-21. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: GPS = Global Positioning System. The US Department of Defense satellite navigation system that provides positioning, navigation, and timing services globally.
exam_tip: GPS is the specific US GNSS. It uses a constellation of at least 24 satellites in MEO. Standard Positioning Service (SPS) is available to all users. Aviation GPS requires RAIM or other augmentation for integrity.
key_facts: GPS = Global Positioning System (US)
Constellation of ≥24 satellites in Medium Earth Orbit
Standard Positioning Service (SPS) available to all
Requires RAIM or augmentation for aviation integrity requirements
source: AC91-21; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: GPWS. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: GPWS = Ground Proximity Warning System. A system that monitors aircraft altitude, altitude rate, configuration, and terrain to provide warnings of controlled flight into terrain (CFIT).
exam_tip: GPWS uses barometric altitude and terrain algorithms to warn of CFIT. Enhanced GPWS (EGPWS/TAWS) adds a terrain database for look-ahead capability. Required on larger aircraft. Hard warnings require immediate action.
key_facts: GPWS = Ground Proximity Warning System
Monitors altitude, descent rate, and configuration
Warns of imminent CFIT (Controlled Flight Into Terrain)
EGPWS/TAWS adds terrain database for predictive look-ahead
source: CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: IAC. AIP GEN. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: IAC = Instrument Approach Chart. The published chart containing all information needed to fly an instrument approach procedure, including plan view, profile view, minima, and missed approach instructions.
exam_tip: The IAC must be current for IFR operations. It shows: approach plan and profile view, waypoints and altitudes, category minima (A/B/C/D), missed approach procedure, communications frequencies.
key_facts: IAC = Instrument Approach Chart
Contains all information to fly an instrument approach
Shows: plan view, profile view, minima, missed approach
Must be current — check amendment date before flight
source: AIP GEN; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: IAF. AIP GEN. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: IAF = Initial Approach Fix. The fix that defines the beginning of the initial approach segment, where the transition from en-route to the approach commences.
