exam_tip: The IAF is the first fix of the approach procedure — the entry point. From the IAF, the pilot flies the initial segment to the IF (Intermediate Fix) or direct to the FAF. Usually named the same as the procedure or navaid.
key_facts: IAF = Initial Approach Fix
Entry point to the approach procedure
Start of the initial approach segment
From IAF: fly to IF or FAF to commence approach
source: AIP GEN; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: ILS. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: ILS = Instrument Landing System. A precision approach system that provides both lateral (localiser) and vertical (glideslope) guidance to the runway threshold.
exam_tip: ILS = localiser (lateral) + glideslope (vertical). Standard glideslope angle is 3°. CAT I: DH ≥200 ft, RVR ≥550 m. The localiser is on a frequency between 108.10–111.95 MHz. Glideslope is automatically paired.
key_facts: ILS = Instrument Landing System
Localiser: lateral guidance (beam width ±2.5° at threshold)
Glideslope: vertical guidance (typically 3° angle)
CAT I: DH ≥200 ft, RVR ≥550 m
source: CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: LNAV. AIP GEN. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: LNAV = Lateral Navigation. An RNAV approach type that provides lateral guidance only (no vertical guidance), flown to an MDA.
exam_tip: LNAV = lateral only — highest minima of the GNSS approach types. Order of minima (lowest to highest): LPV < LNAV/VNAV < LNAV. If only LNAV is available, use the LNAV minima line on the chart.
key_facts: LNAV = Lateral Navigation
Lateral guidance only — no vertical guidance
Flown to an MDA (not DA)
Highest minima of the RNP APCH options (LPV < LNAV/VNAV < LNAV)
source: AIP GEN; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: LP. AC91-21. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: LP = Localiser Performance. An SBAS-based approach type that provides lateral guidance only (no vertical guidance), with tighter accuracy than LNAV, flown to an MDA.
exam_tip: LP uses SBAS (like WAAS) for higher lateral accuracy than LNAV but still no vertical guidance. Provides lower minima than LNAV but higher than LPV. Not widely used — LPV is preferred where available.
key_facts: LP = Localiser Performance
SBAS-based, lateral guidance only
Tighter accuracy than LNAV
Flown to an MDA (not DA); LPV preferred when available
source: AC91-21; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: LPV. AC91-21. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: LPV = Localiser Performance with Vertical guidance. An SBAS-based approach type providing both lateral and vertical guidance, flown to a DA, with minima approaching CAT I ILS.
exam_tip: LPV = lowest minima on an RNP APCH chart. Requires SBAS (WAAS/SBAS) capability. DA as low as 200 ft possible. Available in NZ at selected aerodromes. Has full vertical and lateral guidance — nearest to ILS in performance.
key_facts: LPV = Localiser Performance with Vertical guidance
SBAS-based, both lateral and vertical guidance
Flown to a DA — lowest minima on RNP APCH chart
DA as low as 200 ft (equivalent to CAT I ILS)
source: AC91-21; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: OPMA. AC91-21. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: OPMA = Obstacle Protection for Missed Approach. The protected airspace defined for the missed approach segment of an instrument approach procedure, ensuring obstacle clearance during the climb after a missed approach.
exam_tip: The OPMA defines where you must fly after the missed approach to guarantee obstacle clearance. Follow the published missed approach procedure precisely — deviating from it may exit the protected area.
key_facts: OPMA = Obstacle Protection for Missed Approach
Protected airspace for the missed approach climb
Guarantees obstacle clearance during missed approach
Published missed approach procedure must be followed exactly
source: AC91-21; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: PAR. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: PAR = Precision Approach Radar. A ground-based radar system that provides the controller with precise azimuth and elevation information about an aircraft on approach, enabling the controller to issue verbal guidance.
exam_tip: PAR is a controller-directed precision approach — the controller watches the radar and calls corrections ('Go left two degrees, go up, on glidepath'). The pilot follows verbal instructions rather than cockpit instruments. Used mainly at military aerodromes.
key_facts: PAR = Precision Approach Radar
Ground radar provides both azimuth and elevation to controller
Controller issues verbal lateral and vertical corrections
Pilot follows ATC instructions — primarily military use
source: CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: PBN. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: PBN = Performance-Based Navigation. An ICAO framework for specifying navigation performance requirements for aircraft operating on routes, approaches, or in airspace, replacing sensor-specific requirements.
exam_tip: PBN is the overarching term for modern navigation. It covers both RNAV (area navigation) and RNP (required navigation performance with monitoring and alerting). The navigation specification defines what's needed for each operation.
key_facts: PBN = Performance-Based Navigation
ICAO framework replacing sensor-specific navigation requirements
Covers both RNAV and RNP operations
Defines performance in terms of accuracy, integrity, continuity, availability
source: CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: PMoN. AC91-21. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: PMoN = Primary Means of Navigation. The principal navigation system approved for use in a given operation or phase of flight.
exam_tip: PMoN = the main nav system you're using for the procedure. For most NZ IFR operations, GNSS is the PMoN. If the PMoN fails, you switch to the AMoN (Alternate Means of Navigation).
key_facts: PMoN = Primary Means of Navigation
Main navigation system for the operation
GNSS is the PMoN for most modern NZ IFR operations
If PMoN fails: use AMoN (Alternate Means of Navigation)
source: AC91-21; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: PRA. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: PRA = Potential Risk Area. In GNSS operations, a defined area where GNSS signal interference or degradation has been identified or is anticipated, requiring pilots to check RAIM availability or use alternate navigation.
exam_tip: PRAs are notified via NOTAM. When operating in or through a PRA, verify RAIM availability and have alternate navigation ready. The NOTAM will specify the affected area and altitude.
key_facts: PRA = Potential Risk Area
Area with identified or anticipated GNSS degradation/interference
Notified via NOTAM
Check RAIM availability and have alternate navigation ready when in a PRA
source: AC91-21; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: P-RNAV. AC91-21. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: P-RNAV = Precision Area Navigation. A navigation specification requiring accuracy of ±1 NM for 95% of flight time, used for terminal area operations (STAR, SID, and holding).
exam_tip: P-RNAV is the predecessor to modern RNP 1. It required ±1 NM accuracy in terminal airspace. It has largely been replaced by RNAV 1 and RNP 1 in the PBN framework.
key_facts: P-RNAV = Precision Area Navigation
Accuracy: ±1 NM for ≥95% of flight time
Used in terminal area (SID, STAR, holding)
Largely replaced by RNAV 1 and RNP 1 under PBN framework
source: AC91-21; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: QFE. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: QFE = Q-code for Field Elevation pressure. The altimeter setting that causes the altimeter to read zero (0 ft) when on the runway at the reference point (usually runway threshold or aerodrome elevation).
exam_tip: QFE = altimeter reads height above the aerodrome reference point. At the threshold, altimeter reads 0 on QFE. Rarely used in NZ IFR operations — QNH is the standard. Some UK/older procedures use QFE.
key_facts: QFE = altimeter reads zero (or runway elevation) at the aerodrome reference
Q-code for the atmospheric pressure at aerodrome level
Altimeter displays height above the aerodrome (not above MSL)
Rarely used in NZ — QNH is standard for IFR operations
source: CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: QNH. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: QNH = Q-code for Nautical Height. The altimeter setting that causes the altimeter to read the aerodrome elevation above MSL when on the ground, and altitude above MSL when airborne.
exam_tip: QNH is the standard altimeter setting below the transition altitude. Set QNH and the altimeter shows altitude above MSL. Use area QNH (NZ 12-zone system) or aerodrome QNH. Above transition altitude: set 1013.2 hPa (standard) and use flight levels.
key_facts: QNH = altimeter reads altitude above MSL
Standard setting for IFR operations below transition altitude
NZ uses area QNH (12 zones) or aerodrome QNH
Above transition altitude (13,000 ft NZ): set 1013.2 hPa, use flight levels
source: CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: RAIM. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: RAIM = Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring. A GNSS integrity technique where the receiver uses redundant satellite signals to detect faulty satellites and warn the pilot when integrity is insufficient.
exam_tip: RAIM requires ≥5 satellites for detection. Without RAIM, you cannot use GNSS for IFR. Check RAIM availability before flight (predict for ETA at destination). A RAIM alert = stop the GPS approach and use alternate navigation.
key_facts: RAIM = Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring
Uses redundant satellites to self-monitor GPS integrity
Requires ≥5 satellites for fault detection
RAIM alert = integrity insufficient; revert to alternate navigation
source: CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: RF. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: RF = Radius to Fix. A navigation leg type where the aircraft flies a constant-radius arc from one waypoint to another, used in RNP AR APCH procedures to achieve curved flight paths.
exam_tip: RF legs create precise curved flight paths. Requires RNP AR capability and specific FMS approval. Used at aerodromes with terrain or airspace constraints that require curved approaches (e.g. Queenstown).
key_facts: RF = Radius to Fix
Constant-radius arc flown between two waypoints
Requires RNP AR APCH aircraft capability
Used for curved approach paths at constrained aerodromes
source: CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: RNP APCH. AC91-21. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: RNP APCH = Required Navigation Performance Approach. A PBN navigation specification for instrument approaches using GNSS, providing LNAV, LNAV/VNAV, and LPV minima.
exam_tip: RNP APCH is the standard GNSS approach specification. It requires RAIM monitoring. The chart shows three minima lines: LPV (lowest), LNAV/VNAV (middle), LNAV (highest). Use the lowest minima available for your equipment.
key_facts: RNP APCH = Required Navigation Performance Approach
Standard GNSS approach specification in NZ
Three minima types: LPV, LNAV/VNAV, LNAV
Requires GNSS with RAIM capability
source: AC91-21; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: RNP AR APCH. AC91-21. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: RNP AR APCH = Required Navigation Performance — Authorisation Required Approach. An advanced RNP approach specification requiring special aircraft and operator approval, allowing very tight minima and curved (RF leg) approaches.
exam_tip: RNP AR = the most demanding approach specification. Requires specific aircraft and operator authorisation (not just equipment). Enables approaches with radii as tight as 0.1 NM. Used at challenging aerodromes like Queenstown.
key_facts: RNP AR APCH = Authorisation Required approach
Requires specific aircraft AND operator approval
Enables RF (curved) legs and very tight approach corridors
Used at Queenstown and other constrained aerodromes
source: AC91-21; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: RVSM. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: RVSM = Reduced Vertical Separation Minimum. A standard that reduces the vertical separation between aircraft from 2000 ft to 1000 ft in the RVSM airspace (FL290–FL410 in NZ).
exam_tip: RVSM requires special aircraft approval (height keeping within ±65 m). In NZ, RVSM applies FL290–FL410. Below FL290: 1000 ft separation. Above FL410: 2000 ft. Non-RVSM aircraft require ATC coordination.
key_facts: RVSM = Reduced Vertical Separation Minimum
1000 ft separation applies FL290–FL410 (NZ)
Requires RVSM-approved aircraft (height keeping ±65 m or ±200 ft)
Non-RVSM aircraft must be identified and special separation applied
source: CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: SBAS. AC91-21. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: SBAS = Satellite-Based Augmentation System. A GNSS augmentation system using geostationary satellites to broadcast integrity information and differential corrections across a wide area.
exam_tip: SBAS includes WAAS (USA), EGNOS (Europe), MSAS (Japan). SBAS enables LPV and LP approach minima. NZ does not have a local SBAS system but may receive WAAS signals — check coverage for IFR use.
key_facts: SBAS = Satellite-Based Augmentation System
Wide-area augmentation via geostationary satellites
Provides differential corrections and integrity data
Examples: WAAS (USA), EGNOS (Europe) — enables LPV minima
source: AC91-21; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: STA. AIP GEN. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: STA = Stabilised Approach. An approach that meets defined stabilisation criteria before a specified gate (typically 1000 ft AAL in IMC), including correct configuration, speed, descent rate, and on-path alignment.
exam_tip: A stabilised approach requires: on speed, on profile, in landing configuration, established on centreline, descent rate not excessive — all by 1000 ft in IMC (500 ft in VMC). If not stable = go around.
key_facts: STA = Stabilised Approach
Gate: 1000 ft AAL in IMC; 500 ft in VMC
Criteria: correct speed, configuration, descent rate, profile
If not stable at gate: mandatory go-around
source: AIP GEN; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: TAWS. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: TAWS = Terrain Awareness and Warning System. An advanced ground proximity warning system that uses a terrain database to provide predictive warnings of terrain conflict.
exam_tip: TAWS = EGPWS with terrain database. Provides 'look-ahead' alerts — warns you of terrain conflict on your current track, not just imminent impact. Mandatory on larger aircraft. Required on turbine aircraft above 9 passengers in NZ.
key_facts: TAWS = Terrain Awareness and Warning System
Enhanced GPWS with terrain database for predictive warnings
Alerts to terrain on current track (look-ahead capability)
Required on turbine aircraft certified for 9+ passengers in NZ
source: CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: TCAS. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: TCAS = Traffic Alert and Collision Avoidance System. The specific implementation of ACAS used in aviation, providing Traffic Advisories (TAs) and Resolution Advisories (RAs) based on transponder interrogation of nearby aircraft.
exam_tip: TCAS II is the standard — it provides both TAs and vertical RAs. TCAS I provides TAs only (no RAs). RAs must be followed immediately — they override ATC instructions. Notify ATC that you are following an RA.
key_facts: TCAS = Traffic Alert and Collision Avoidance System
Implementation of ACAS — interrogates nearby transponders
TCAS II: provides TAs (traffic) and RAs (resolution advisories)
RA override ATC — follow immediately and notify ATC
source: CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: TF. AC91-21. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: TF = Track to Fix. A navigation leg type where the aircraft tracks a straight course from one waypoint to the next, the most common leg type in RNAV/RNP procedures.
exam_tip: TF legs are the standard straight-line RNAV path between waypoints. Used in SIDs, STARs, airways, and approaches. The aircraft tracks along the great circle path from the from-waypoint to the to-waypoint.
key_facts: TF = Track to Fix
Straight-line track between two waypoints
Most common RNAV/RNP leg type
Aircraft flies great circle path to the next fix
source: AC91-21; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: TSE. AC91-21. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: TSE = Total System Error. The difference between the aircraft's true position and the desired flight path, comprising navigation system error (NSE), flight technical error (FTE), and path definition error (PDE).
exam_tip: TSE = the total error that determines whether you're on the required path. TSE must be within the RNP value 95% of the time. NSE (sensor error) + FTE (autopilot/pilot error) + PDE (path calculation error) = TSE.
key_facts: TSE = Total System Error
Total deviation from desired flight path
TSE = NSE (navigation error) + FTE (flight technical error) + PDE
TSE must be within the RNP containment value 95% of flight time
source: AC91-21; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: VNAV. AC91-21. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: VNAV = Vertical Navigation. The automated management of vertical flight path — descents, climbs, and altitude constraints — using the FMS/autopilot to follow a computed vertical profile.
exam_tip: VNAV follows the FMS-computed vertical path. On approaches, VNAV follows the Baro-VNAV glidepath for LNAV/VNAV procedures. VNAV + LNAV = full approach guidance (though not as precise as ILS). Monitor actual altitude against VNAV path.
key_facts: VNAV = Vertical Navigation
FMS/autopilot-managed vertical flight path
On approaches: follows Baro-VNAV glidepath
LNAV/VNAV approach = lateral + vertical RNAV guidance
source: AC91-21; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: VOR. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: VOR = VHF Omnidirectional Radio Range. A ground-based VHF radio navaid that transmits 360 radials (magnetic bearings from the station), allowing aircraft to determine their bearing from the station.
exam_tip: VOR operates on 108.0–117.95 MHz. The cockpit indicator shows the selected radial — TO the station or FROM the station. VOR is used for airways, approaches, and as an AMoN. Subject to line-of-sight limitation.
key_facts: VOR = VHF Omnidirectional Radio Range
VHF ground station broadcasting 360 radials (magnetic)
Frequency: 108.0–117.95 MHz
Line-of-sight limitation — range increases with altitude
source: CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: VORSEC. AIP GEN. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: VORSEC = VOR Sector. A defined sector around a VOR station used for approach procedures, identifying the approach segment and associated obstacle clearance area.
exam_tip: VORSEC defines sectors around a VOR for approach design. The sector determines minimum safe altitudes and obstacle clearance areas for the approach in that bearing range from the VOR. Used in designing VOR approaches.
key_facts: VORSEC = VOR Sector
Defined sector around a VOR for approach procedure design
Determines obstacle clearance area for that bearing range
Used in approach procedure design and charting
source: AIP GEN; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the abbreviation: VPA. AIP GEN. CAR Part 1 (unless otherwise noted)
concept: VPA = Vertical Path Angle. The angle of descent on an instrument approach procedure's final approach segment, typically 3.0° for standard approaches.
exam_tip: VPA is shown on approach charts. Standard is 3.0°. A steeper VPA (e.g. 3.5°, 4.0°) requires higher speed to maintain glidepath but gives better obstacle clearance. VPA drives the required descent rate at a given groundspeed.
key_facts: VPA = Vertical Path Angle
Angle of descent on the final approach segment
Standard VPA = 3.0°
Steeper VPA requires higher descent rate at a given groundspeed
source: AIP GEN; CAR Part 1 abbreviations

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for holding a pilot's licence. CAR Part 61
concept: Under CAR Part 61, to hold a pilot's licence you've got to meet the eligibility requirements for that licence (age, medical, knowledge and flight test, English language proficiency), hold a current class 1 or class 2 medical certificate as applicable, and keep the licence current through the recency and BFR requirements. The licence itself is issued by the Director under section 78 of the CA Act. Once issued, you hold it until it's cancelled, suspended, or surrendered — there's no expiry on the licence itself, but the medical and rating currency on it can expire and that'll stop you from exercising privileges.
exam_tip: The licence doesn't expire, but the medical and the rating recency do. Know the difference between holding a licence and being able to exercise its privileges.
key_facts: Requirements for holding a pilot's licence are set out in CAR Part 61
A licence is issued by the Director under CA Act 2023 section 78
Eligibility requires age, medical, knowledge exams, flight test, and ELP
The licence doesn't expire but privileges can lapse through medical or recency issues
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 61

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for a pilot-in-command to hold a type rating on the type of aircraft being flown. CAR Part 61
concept: CAR Part 61 requires the PIC of any aircraft requiring a type rating to hold that type rating on their licence before acting as PIC of that type. Type ratings are required for aircraft above 5,700 kg MCTOW, turbojet aircraft regardless of weight, and any aircraft specifically identified in the rule. For the IR world, this matters because many IFR-capable aircraft (King Airs, light jets) sit right in the type-rating bracket, and holding a current IR doesn't let you fly them without the separate type endorsement.
exam_tip: IR and type rating are separate qualifications. You need both to fly an IFR flight in a type-rated aircraft. Know the 5,700 kg threshold and the turbojet trigger.
key_facts: Type ratings required for aircraft >5,700 kg MCTOW
Type ratings required for all turbojet aircraft regardless of weight
Type rating is separate from the instrument rating
Type rating must be current and appropriate for the aircraft being flown
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 61

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for entering flight details into a pilot's logbook. CAR Part 61
concept: Part 61 requires you to record in your logbook the date, aircraft type and registration, departure and arrival points, total flight time, and the capacity in which you acted (PIC, dual, P2, etc.). For IFR operations, you also record the instrument flight time (actual IMC) and instrument time (actual + simulated under the hood), plus the number and type of instrument approaches flown. Those IFR entries matter for currency — section 52.14 tests you need to demonstrate, and you can only do that if you've got the logbook entries to prove the approaches and hours.
exam_tip: Know that instrument approaches must be logged by type (ILS, RNP, VOR etc.) to count for currency. The distinction between 'instrument flight time' (actual IMC) and 'instrument time' (actual + simulated) is a common exam question.
key_facts: Logbook entries must include date, type, registration, departure, arrival, total time, capacity
IFR operations must record instrument flight time and instrument time separately
Number and type of instrument approaches must be logged
Entries must be made as soon as practicable after the flight
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 61

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for holding an instrument rating. CAR Part 61
concept: To hold an instrument rating under CAR Part 61 Subpart Q you must hold at least a PPL, hold a current class 1 or class 2 medical, have passed the IR theory exams (Subject 52 IR Law, Subject 54 Flight Nav IFR, Subject 56 Instruments and Nav Aids), met the flight-time experience requirements in rule 61.801, and passed the IR flight test. Once issued, the rating is kept current through the recency provisions in rule 61.807 — you've got to meet the currency requirements every six months, or you lose the privilege to exercise the rating until you requalify.
exam_tip: Know the three theory subjects (52, 54, 56) and the rule number for eligibility (61.801) and recency (61.807). Issue requirements and currency requirements are tested separately.
key_facts: IR eligibility is in CAR rule 61.801
Three theory subjects required: 52 (Law), 54 (Flight Nav IFR), 56 (Instruments & Nav Aids)
Minimum of PPL plus current class 1 or class 2 medical
IR flight test required for initial issue
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 61 Subpart Q, rules 61.801 and 61.807

syllabus_desc: State the licence and rating requirements for acting as a safety pilot during simulated instrument flight. CAR Part 91
concept: CAR Part 91 lets a qualified safety pilot sit in the other seat while the pilot flying is under the hood in simulated instrument conditions. The safety pilot has to hold at least the same category of licence as is required for the flight, have a valid medical, and be current for the operation. The reason the rule exists is practical: when you're under the hood you can't see traffic, so you need someone with legal PIC qualifications scanning outside. The safety pilot is essentially acting as the see-and-avoid eyeballs.
exam_tip: Safety pilot must hold at least the same category of licence as required for the flight and have a current medical. They're there for see-and-avoid, not to act as instructor.
key_facts: Safety pilot requirement is in CAR Part 91
Must hold at least the same category of licence as required for the flight
Must have a current medical appropriate to the role
Purpose is see-and-avoid while the pilot flying is under the hood
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Describe the allowance for a person who does not hold a current pilot's licence to fly dual with an instructor. CAR Part 61
concept: CAR Part 61 allows a person without a current pilot's licence to fly dual with a flight instructor provided the instructor is appropriately rated and acting as PIC. This is what lets student pilots fly during initial training, and it's also the mechanism that allows a lapsed licence holder to take a refresher with an instructor without formally holding the licence. The key is that the instructor is legally PIC for the whole operation and carries the responsibility under section 14 of the Act.
exam_tip: The instructor is PIC, not the student. The student doesn't need a licence because they're not exercising licence privileges -- the instructor is.
key_facts: Dual flight with an instructor is permitted without a current licence
Instructor must hold a current flight instructor rating appropriate to the aircraft
Instructor acts as PIC and is legally responsible for the flight
Allows both ab initio training and refresher flying for lapsed licence holders
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 61

syllabus_desc: State the eligibility requirements for the issue of an instrument rating. CAR Part 61
concept: Rule 61.801 sets the eligibility requirements for IR issue: hold at least a current PPL, hold a current class 1 or class 2 medical, be at least 17 years old, pass the three theory subjects (52, 54, 56), meet the flight-time experience requirements (50 hours cross-country PIC including the specified instrument hours), complete the required training at a Part 141 organisation or under a Part 61 instructor with an IR training endorsement, and pass the IR flight test within the validity period of the theory credits.
exam_tip: Know the 50 hours cross-country PIC figure and that theory credits from exam passes have a validity period -- if you don't use them in time you'll resit.
key_facts: Eligibility in CAR rule 61.801
Minimum age 17, current PPL, current class 1 or 2 medical
50 hours cross-country PIC required
Three theory subjects (52, 54, 56) must be passed
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 61 rule 61.801

syllabus_desc: State the privileges of holding an instrument rating. CAR Part 61
concept: Holding an IR lets you fly under IFR and in IMC, carry out instrument approaches, and operate in airspace and conditions that would otherwise require VMC. The specific privileges depend on what's also on your licence -- an IR on a PPL gives you PPL-level IFR privileges (private operations), an IR on a CPL adds commercial IFR privileges. You can also act as safety pilot for another pilot flying under the hood, and the IR gives you the qualification base for further ratings like multi-engine IR or ATPL studies.
exam_tip: Privileges depend on the underlying licence. PPL + IR ≠ CPL + IR. Know that operating in IMC and flying instrument approaches are the core IR privileges.
key_facts: IR privileges are set out in CAR Part 61
Allows flight under IFR and in IMC
Allows carrying out instrument approaches to published minima
Privileges scale with the underlying licence (PPL, CPL, ATPL)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 61

syllabus_desc: State the limitations on the holder of an instrument rating. CAR Part 61
concept: The IR isn't an all-access pass. Limitations include: you can only exercise IR privileges in the category of aircraft for which the rating was issued (aeroplane or helicopter); you're limited to single-pilot or multi-pilot operations depending on the nature of your training and testing; specific approach types like RNP AR APCH require separate authorisations; PBN privileges depend on the navigation specifications you were trained and tested on; and you must have the correct endorsements for any specific equipment like GNSS. Multi-engine IR is a separate consideration -- if you tested in a single and want to fly twins IFR, you need the ME IR.
exam_tip: The limitation questions often focus on what you DIDN'T train on. Single vs multi-pilot, single vs multi-engine, specific PBN specs -- all tested separately.
key_facts: IR is category-specific (aeroplane or helicopter)
Single-pilot and multi-pilot privileges are distinct
Multi-engine IR is a separate privilege from single-engine IR
Specific PBN specifications (e.g. RNP AR APCH) require separate authorisation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 61

syllabus_desc: State the qualification requirements for carrying out various types of instrument approach. CAR Part 61
concept: Different instrument approach types have different qualification requirements. A 2D approach (non-precision -- LNAV, VOR, NDB) requires the pilot to be trained and current on that approach type. A 3D approach (precision or APV -- ILS, LPV, LNAV/VNAV) requires additional training and a currency check. RNP APCH approaches require PBN training covering the specific navigation specification. RNP AR APCH requires specific operator authorisation and pilot training beyond standard RNP APCH. If the approach uses GNSS, the pilot must be competent on the specific GNSS receiver fitted, and the operator must have the data currency in place.
exam_tip: Know the 2D/3D distinction and that RNP AR needs special authorisation. GNSS competency is type-specific to the receiver installed.
key_facts: 2D approaches (LNAV, VOR, NDB) -- standard IR training covers these
3D approaches (ILS, LPV, LNAV/VNAV) -- require demonstrated competency
RNP APCH requires PBN training per AC91-21
RNP AR APCH requires separate operator and pilot authorisation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 61, AC91-21

syllabus_desc: State the currency requirements of a pilot who is the holder of an instrument rating. CAR Part 61
concept: To keep your IR current under CAR Part 61 you need a competency check (commonly called an IR renewal) every 12 months with an authorised flight examiner. Within that 12-month window you also need to meet recency requirements for instrument approaches -- the rule is you need to have carried out a minimum number of approaches in the preceding period or you lose the privilege to conduct those approaches until you requalify. If the annual competency check lapses, the rating is no longer current and you can't exercise IR privileges until you requalify with a flight test.
exam_tip: 12-month competency check = annual. Within that, there are separate recency rules for approaches. Losing recency doesn't cancel the rating, but losing competency currency does make it unusable.
key_facts: IR competency check required every 12 months with an authorised examiner
Additional approach recency requirements apply within the 12-month period
Failure to renew means the rating is not exercisable until requalification
Requalification after expiry requires a full flight test
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 61

syllabus_desc: State the currency requirements for carrying out an instrument approach. CAR Part 61
concept: For approach recency, Part 61 requires that within the preceding 90 days you have carried out at least three instrument approaches in actual or simulated IMC. If you don't have that, you can't conduct an instrument approach as PIC in IMC until you requalify with an instructor or examiner. The approaches can be in the aircraft or in an approved flight simulator/FSTD. The rule exists because approach flying is perishable -- if you haven't done one in three months, your scan rate, configuration management, and missed approach readiness will be degraded.
exam_tip: Three approaches in 90 days is the headline number. They must be in actual or simulated IMC, and an approved simulator counts.
key_facts: Three instrument approaches required within the preceding 90 days
Must be in actual or simulated IMC (under the hood counts)
Approved FSTD or simulator sessions count towards recency
If lapsed, requalification needed before exercising approach privileges in IMC
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 61

syllabus_desc: State the hearing standard required for the holder of an instrument rating. CAR Part 61
concept: CAR Part 61 specifies the hearing standard for the holder of an instrument rating. The standard is the same as for class 1 medical hearing requirements -- you must be able to hear an average conversational voice at 2 metres with both ears, or demonstrate acceptable audiometric performance if that test can't be met. The reason it's stricter than for VFR is that IFR operations depend on voice communications with ATC at all times. If you can't reliably hear a clearance, instruction, or amendment, you can't safely fly IFR.
exam_tip: The hearing standard is a common exam target. 2 metres conversational voice with both ears is the practical test. Audiometry is the backup if the practical test fails.
key_facts: Hearing standard specified in CAR Part 61 and the Part 67 medical standards
Practical test: hear average conversational voice at 2 metres with both ears
Audiometric test available if practical test not achievable
Required because IFR operations depend on voice ATC communications
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 61, CAR Part 67

syllabus_desc: State the documents which must be carried in aircraft operated in New Zealand. CAR Part 91
concept: CAR Part 91 lists the documents that must be carried in an aircraft operated in New Zealand: the certificate of registration, the certificate of airworthiness, the current flight manual or pilot's operating handbook, the maintenance release, the radio licence (if the aircraft is radio-equipped), the noise certificate (if issued), the certificate of insurance (for commercial ops), the flight crew licences and medicals of those on board exercising privileges, and any weight and balance data required for the flight. For IFR operations you'll also typically need current charts and approach plates for the route and alternates. The mnemonic ARROW or AROW is a reasonable memory aid, but Part 91's list is the legal standard.
exam_tip: Know the core list -- certificate of registration, airworthiness certificate, flight manual, maintenance release, radio licence, flight crew documentation. For IFR, add current charts. Exam questions often ask which item is NOT required.
key_facts: Document requirements listed in CAR Part 91
Certificate of registration and airworthiness certificate must be carried
Current flight manual or POH required
Maintenance release must be on board and current
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the inspection period for radios. CAR Part 91
concept: CAR Part 91 requires radio equipment to be inspected and tested at intervals specified in the maintenance programme. For IFR-required radios (VHF communications and navigation), the standard inspection period is 24 months. The inspection covers transmitter output power, receiver sensitivity, frequency accuracy, and modulation quality. It's done by an appropriately rated avionics engineer and recorded on the maintenance release. If the radio check expires you can't legally fly IFR until the inspection is completed, because the CAR Part 91 IFR equipment requirement says the radios must be in working order and within their inspection period.
exam_tip: 24 months is the number. The inspection is in the maintenance programme under CAR Part 91.
key_facts: Radio inspection period under CAR Part 91 is 24 months
Applies to IFR-required VHF communications and navigation radios
Inspection performed by an appropriately rated avionics engineer
Recorded on the maintenance release
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the inspection period for altimeters. CAR Part 91
concept: Altimeters used for IFR operations must be inspected and tested at intervals specified in CAR Part 91. The standard inspection period for an IFR altimeter is 24 months. The test checks static system integrity (leak check), altimeter scale error against a pressure standard, and the altitude encoding output to the transponder if fitted. A pitot-static leak check is done at the same time. Without a current altimeter inspection, the aircraft cannot legally operate IFR because CAR Part 91 requires the altimeter to be within its inspection period as part of the IFR equipment list.
exam_tip: 24 months again. Altimeter, transponder, and static system all sit in the same biennial inspection cycle for IFR-equipped aircraft.
key_facts: Altimeter inspection period is 24 months under CAR Part 91
Includes pitot-static leak check and altitude encoder verification
Required for IFR operations to be legal
Recorded on the maintenance release
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the inspection period for transponders. CAR Part 91
concept: Transponders require a biennial inspection under CAR Part 91. The standard inspection period is 24 months. The test verifies Mode A code response, Mode C altitude encoding accuracy, reply rate, transmitter output power, and receiver sensitivity. For ADS-B Out installations (which are now mandatory in most NZ controlled airspace), the inspection also verifies the position source (GNSS) integration and the extended squitter output. A transponder with an expired inspection cannot legally be used in transponder-mandatory airspace or for IFR operations.
exam_tip: 24 months. Note that ADS-B Out has been progressively introduced in NZ and the transponder check now covers more than it used to.
key_facts: Transponder inspection period is 24 months under CAR Part 91
Includes Mode A, Mode C, and ADS-B Out verification where fitted
Required for operations in transponder-mandatory airspace
Recorded on the maintenance release
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the inspection period for the ELT. CAR Part 91
concept: The Emergency Locator Transmitter (ELT) has two separate inspection cycles. The annual inspection checks the ELT function, battery condition, antenna integrity, and the crash sensor (g-switch). The battery replacement period depends on the ELT type -- typically every 5 years for 406 MHz ELTs, or at 50% of the manufacturer's rated battery life, or after any activation exceeding 1 cumulative hour, whichever comes first. The 406 MHz registration with RCCNZ must also be kept current (that's a separate administrative requirement, not a maintenance one).
exam_tip: Annual inspection is the headline answer. Battery replacement is separate and type-dependent. Don't confuse the annual function check with the battery replacement cycle.
key_facts: ELT annual inspection required under CAR Part 91
Battery replacement per manufacturer specification (typically 5 years for 406 MHz)
406 MHz ELT registration with RCCNZ is a separate ongoing requirement
Recorded on the maintenance release
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the minimum instrument requirements for an IFR flight. CAR Part 91
concept: CAR Part 91 sets a minimum instrument list for IFR flight. You need an airspeed indicator, a sensitive altimeter (with adjustable subscale), a magnetic compass, a clock showing hours, minutes and seconds, an accurate outside air temperature indicator, a vertical speed indicator, an attitude indicator (artificial horizon), a heading indicator (DG), and a turn and slip indicator (or turn coordinator). For multi-engine aircraft you also need the appropriate engine instruments. The key philosophy is redundancy for attitude and heading information -- the attitude indicator and turn coordinator together let you keep the aircraft upright and on track if one fails.
exam_tip: The classic exam question asks you to identify which instrument is NOT required. Typical distractors include ADF, DME, and angle of attack -- none of which are in the IFR minimum list under Part 91.
key_facts: Minimum IFR instruments listed in CAR Part 91
Must include ASI, sensitive altimeter, magnetic compass, clock, OAT gauge
Must include attitude indicator, heading indicator, turn and slip/turn coordinator
Vertical speed indicator required for IFR
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the communications and navigation equipment requirements for an IFR flight. CAR Part 91
concept: CAR Part 91 requires an IFR-equipped aircraft to carry enough communications and navigation equipment to fly the intended route and the alternate, including any required approach. At minimum that means one VHF comm radio and enough navigation radios (VOR, ILS, DME, GNSS, ADF as applicable) to navigate the planned IFR route and fly the planned approach. For PBN routes, the aircraft must have the navigation specification required for that route (RNAV 2 enroute, RNP 1 terminal, RNP APCH for approaches). If you're flying into controlled airspace, you also need the required transponder and ADS-B Out where mandated.
exam_tip: The phrase to remember is 'sufficient for the route and alternate, including the approach'. PBN-equipped routes and approaches drive specific nav spec requirements -- test-worthy.
key_facts: Comms and nav equipment requirement is in CAR Part 91
Must be sufficient for the intended route, alternates, and approaches
PBN routes require the aircraft to hold the relevant navigation specification
At least one VHF comm radio required
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91, AC91-21

syllabus_desc: State the equipment requirements of aircraft operating in airspace where RVSM is applied by ATC. CAR Part 91
concept: Reduced Vertical Separation Minima (RVSM) airspace requires specific equipment: two independent altitude measurement systems, an altitude alerting system, an automatic altitude control system (autopilot) capable of holding altitude within ±20 ft, and a Mode C transponder with RVSM compliance. The aircraft must also hold an RVSM approval from the operator's state of registry. NZ domestic airspace above FL290 uses RVSM up to FL410. Without the equipment and the approval you can't be cleared above FL290 in RVSM airspace.
exam_tip: Remember: two independent altimeters, altitude alerter, altitude hold autopilot, Mode C transponder, operator approval. All five are typically tested.
key_facts: RVSM equipment listed in CAR Part 91 and AIP ENR
Two independent altitude measurement systems required
Altitude alerting and altitude hold autopilot required
RVSM approval from state of registry required
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State the equipment requirements for an IFR flight. CAR Part 91
concept: Beyond the minimum instruments, CAR Part 91 requires additional equipment for IFR: a serviceable ELT, an alternate source of static air (or an alternate static system), anti-ice/de-ice equipment if operating in known icing, a landing light for night operations, an external power supply or adequate battery capacity for the intended flight, and enough oxygen for the altitudes flown. The principle is that anything the aircraft relies on for IFR navigation, control, or emergency response has to be serviceable before the flight departs.
exam_tip: Pay attention to the alternate static source -- it's a commonly missed item on exam questions about IFR equipment.
key_facts: IFR equipment requirements in CAR Part 91
Serviceable ELT required
Alternate static air source required
Anti-ice equipment required if operating in known icing
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for indicating the time in flight. CAR Part 91
concept: CAR Part 91 requires a clock that shows hours, minutes, and seconds -- and it must be fixed in the aircraft (not just your wristwatch). The reason is timing accuracy matters for holding patterns, missed approach procedures, non-precision approach timed segments, and flight plan compliance. A digital clock with sweep-second display or a traditional analogue clock with a second hand both meet the requirement. On most modern glass cockpit installations, the clock is integrated into the PFD or multifunction display.
exam_tip: It's got to show hours, minutes, AND seconds, and it has to be fixed in the aircraft. A wristwatch doesn't meet the rule.
key_facts: Clock requirement is in CAR Part 91
Must display hours, minutes, and seconds
Must be fixed in the aircraft
Required for IFR timing on approaches and holds
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for night flight. CAR Part 91
concept: Night flight under CAR Part 91 needs additional equipment beyond the day IFR list: position lights (red port, green starboard, white tail), an anti-collision light system (red or white strobes), a landing light, cockpit instrument lighting sufficient for all instruments and controls, a torch available to the pilot, and spare fuses (if the aircraft uses fuses). For commercial night operations there are additional requirements. The equipment exists to keep you visible, let you see your instruments, and give you a backup if something fails.
exam_tip: Don't forget the torch and spare fuses -- small items but frequently tested. Position lights plus anti-collision lights together is the standard answer.
key_facts: Night equipment requirements in CAR Part 91
Position lights and anti-collision light system required
Landing light and cockpit instrument lighting required
Torch and spare fuses (where applicable) required
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain the requirement for altitude alerting/assigned altitude indicating. CAR Part 91
concept: CAR Part 91 requires altitude alerting or assigned altitude indicating equipment for IFR operations in specified aircraft categories. The altitude alerter warns the pilot when approaching an assigned altitude (typically 1000 ft prior) and again if the aircraft deviates from that altitude (typically ±200 ft). The rationale is level bust prevention -- altitude excursions in controlled airspace are a major ATC safety concern, and a simple audible or visual alert dramatically reduces the rate. Most turbine aircraft and larger piston twins require it; simpler singles operating IFR generally don't, though it's increasingly common even on light aircraft.
exam_tip: Know the logic: alert approaching the altitude (≈1000 ft before), alert on deviation (≈200 ft). The rule targets level bust prevention.
key_facts: Altitude alerting requirement in CAR Part 91
Alerts when approaching assigned altitude (typically 1000 ft prior)
Alerts on deviation from assigned altitude (typically ±200 ft)
Required in specified aircraft categories, mandatory for RVSM
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for an ELT. CAR Part 91
concept: CAR Part 91 requires aircraft operated in New Zealand to carry a serviceable ELT. The standard is a 406 MHz ELT (121.5 MHz-only ELTs are no longer accepted, as the satellite system for 121.5 was decommissioned in 2009). The ELT must be properly installed, within its maintenance inspection period, have a current battery, and be registered with the Rescue Coordination Centre NZ (RCCNZ). For some specific operations (e.g. local training flights within a defined area), CAA allows exemptions, but the default for any cross-country or IFR flight is that the 406 MHz ELT must be on board and functional.
exam_tip: 406 MHz is the standard -- 121.5-only ELTs no longer meet the requirement. Registration with RCCNZ is a common distractor.
key_facts: ELT requirement in CAR Part 91
Must be a 406 MHz ELT
Must be registered with RCCNZ
Must be within maintenance inspection period and have serviceable battery
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for operating an aircraft in simulated instrument flight. CAR Part 91
concept: Simulated instrument flight (flying under the hood or with vision-limiting devices) is permitted under CAR Part 91 provided you have a suitably qualified safety pilot in the other control seat, the aircraft has dual controls, the safety pilot has adequate forward and side vision, and both pilots have agreed on the handover procedure. The safety pilot is acting as the see-and-avoid backup and is normally designated as PIC for the operation unless the pilot flying is rated and current. Simulated instrument time is loggable as instrument time but is recorded separately from actual IMC.
exam_tip: Safety pilot + dual controls + adequate vision. Simulated time logs as instrument time but not as instrument flight time.
key_facts: Simulated instrument flight requirements in CAR Part 91
Requires suitably qualified safety pilot
Aircraft must have dual controls
Safety pilot must have adequate forward and side vision
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for carrying appropriate aeronautical publications and charts in flight. CAR Part 91
concept: CAR Part 91 requires the PIC to carry appropriate aeronautical publications and charts for the intended flight. For IFR that means current enroute charts, SIDs, STARs, and approach plates for the departure, destination, and all nominated alternates. Publications can be paper or electronic (EFB) as long as they're current and accessible during the flight. The rule doesn't let you 'fly from memory' on a published procedure -- you must have the relevant chart available. If you're relying on an EFB, you also need a backup plan (paper or a second device) for the case where the primary fails.
exam_tip: Current charts are the key word. EFB is acceptable but needs a backup. Alternates must be charted too, not just destination.
key_facts: Publications and charts requirement in CAR Part 91
Must cover route, alternates, departure, and approach procedures
Must be current and accessible during the flight
EFB acceptable with appropriate backup arrangements
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for the maintenance of an enroute track. CAR Part 91
concept: CAR Part 91 requires pilots of IFR flights to maintain the assigned enroute track. That means flying the cleared route as filed and cleared by ATC, within the tolerances of the navigation specification applicable to the airspace. For conventional navigation (VOR-to-VOR), you're expected to keep the CDI within half-scale deflection. For PBN operations, the tolerance is defined by the navigation specification (e.g. RNAV 2 = ±2 NM 95% of the time). If you can't maintain track -- because of weather avoidance, equipment failure, or anything else -- you must notify ATC as soon as practicable.
exam_tip: Tolerances depend on the nav spec. For RNAV 2 enroute, ±2 NM 95% is the standard answer. Notify ATC of any unavoidable deviation.
key_facts: Enroute track maintenance requirement in CAR Part 91
Tolerance defined by the navigation specification in use
Half-scale CDI deflection for conventional VOR navigation
ATC must be notified of any unavoidable deviation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91, AC91-21

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for IFR cruising altitude or flight level. CAR Part 91
concept: CAR Part 91 requires IFR flights within the NZ FIR to cruise at the IFR cruising levels published in AIP ENR, determined by the magnetic track. For eastbound tracks (magnetic track 000-179), use odd thousands of feet (or odd thousands + 500 ft for VFR-over-the-top). For westbound tracks (180-359), use even thousands. Above FL245 the system transitions to RVSM flight levels. ATC may assign a non-standard level for traffic management, and if so you fly the assigned level and that satisfies the requirement.
exam_tip: Eastbound = odd, westbound = even. Based on magnetic track. ATC assignment overrides the table.
key_facts: IFR cruising levels requirement in CAR Part 91
Eastbound (000-179 magnetic) = odd thousands
Westbound (180-359 magnetic) = even thousands
ATC assignment overrides the table
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State the restrictions on the use of portable electronic devices in flight. CAR Part 91
concept: CAR Part 91 restricts the use of portable electronic devices (PEDs) in flight because they can interfere with aircraft navigation and communication equipment. The rule allows PEDs for which the operator has determined use will not cause interference -- which in practice means most modern phones in flight mode, tablets, and EFBs are permitted. PEDs capable of transmitting (active phones, two-way radios, wireless devices) are restricted during critical phases of flight and in some cases throughout the flight. The PIC has final authority on any PED use onboard.
exam_tip: Operator determines which PEDs are permitted. PIC has final authority in flight. Transmitting PEDs have tighter restrictions than non-transmitting ones.
key_facts: PED restrictions in CAR Part 91
Operator determines which PEDs are permitted
Transmitting PEDs restricted during critical phases of flight
PIC has final authority on PED use
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the speed limitations on aircraft operating under IFR. CAR Part 91
concept: CAR Part 91 imposes speed limits on IFR operations. Below 10,000 ft AMSL, aircraft are limited to 250 KIAS (this is the standard ICAO speed cap designed to keep closing speeds manageable in busier airspace). Within a control zone, aircraft within 4 NM of an aerodrome and below 2500 ft AGL are limited to 185 KIAS unless ATC approves otherwise. At or below circuit height in the vicinity of a non-towered aerodrome, the speed limit is whatever's compatible with the traffic pattern.
exam_tip: 250 KIAS below 10,000 ft is the headline number. 185 KIAS below 2500 ft within 4 NM of a CTR aerodrome is the secondary number. Both are tested regularly.
key_facts: Speed limit of 250 KIAS below 10,000 ft AMSL
185 KIAS below 2500 ft AGL within 4 NM of a CTR aerodrome
Requirements in CAR Part 91
ATC may grant exceptions on request
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the restrictions when operating IFR in icing conditions. CAR Part 91
concept: CAR Part 91 restricts IFR operations in known icing conditions to aircraft certified and equipped for flight in icing (typically labelled 'flight into known icing' or FIKI). If the aircraft isn't certified for FIKI, you cannot intentionally enter known icing conditions, and if you encounter icing unexpectedly you must take immediate action to exit (change altitude, divert, request priority). Ice accretion on a non-FIKI aircraft is a serious threat -- it degrades lift, increases drag, adds weight, and can disable control surfaces. This is why the IR syllabus drums into you that icing avoidance is a flight-planning issue, not an in-flight one.
exam_tip: Non-FIKI aircraft = no intentional flight in known icing. If encountered, immediate action to exit is required. 'Known icing' is the key phrase to understand.
key_facts: Flight in icing restrictions in CAR Part 91
Only FIKI-certified aircraft may intentionally operate in known icing
Non-FIKI aircraft must exit icing immediately if encountered
Known icing is determined from forecast and observed conditions
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the minimum altitudes for IFR flight. CAR Part 91
concept: CAR Part 91 sets minimum altitudes for IFR flight. The general rule is that IFR flights must maintain at least 1000 ft above the highest obstacle within 5 NM of the track (LSALT calculation). In mountainous terrain, the margin increases to 2000 ft. On published IFR procedures (SIDs, STARs, approach segments, airways), the published minimum altitudes apply instead and they've already been protected for obstacle clearance by the procedure designer. In controlled airspace under radar, terrain clearance may be provided by ATC under specific radar vectoring rules.
exam_tip: 1000 ft non-mountainous, 2000 ft mountainous, within 5 NM of track. On published procedures, the chart takes precedence.
key_facts: Minimum IFR altitude rules in CAR Part 91
1000 ft above highest obstacle within 5 NM of track (non-mountainous)
2000 ft above highest obstacle within 5 NM of track (mountainous)
Published minima on SIDs, STARs, and IAPs take precedence
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain the requirements for obtaining and considering relevant information prior to flight. CAR Part 91
concept: Rule 91.217 requires the PIC to obtain and become familiar with all available information relevant to the intended flight before departure. For IFR that covers: current and forecast weather for the route, alternates, and destination; fuel requirements including reserves; alternatives if the planned flight can't be completed (alternate aerodrome, divert options); known or likely ATC delays; status of communication and navigation facilities on the route; current conditions of aerodromes, including runway lengths; take-off and landing distance data from the flight manual; and for multi-engine aircraft, engine-inoperative procedures. The intent is clear: you're required to build a complete picture of the flight before you push the throttle forward, not just grab a TAF and guess.
exam_tip: Rule 91.217 lists the specific items. Know that the requirement is to obtain AND become familiar — just printing the weather isn't enough. ATC delays and nav aid status are commonly overlooked items on exam questions.
key_facts: Pre-flight information requirement in CAR rule 91.217
Must cover weather, fuel, alternatives, ATC delays, nav aid status, aerodrome conditions
Take-off and landing distance data from flight manual required
Multi-engine aircraft: engine-inoperative procedures must be considered
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91 rule 91.217

syllabus_desc: Describe the publications and their content that provide operational route and aerodrome information
concept: The main publications for IFR operations in NZ are: AIP NZ (the master document, containing ENR sections for route info, airspace, procedures; GEN sections for general information; AD sections for individual aerodrome data); AIPNZ Volume 2 & 3 (IFR charts — departure charts, STARs, and instrument approach charts for all published procedures); AIPNZ Volume 4 (aerodrome directory, equivalent of ERSA in Australia); NOTAMs (timely changes to any of the above); and AIP Supplements (temporary changes to AIP content). For flight planning, you'll be drawing from ENR sections for route minima and airspace, the approach charts for destination and alternate procedures, and the aerodrome pages for runway data, lighting, and fuel availability.
exam_tip: Know the structure: AIP NZ (ENR/GEN/AD), AIPNZ Vol 2 & 3 (procedure charts), AIPNZ Vol 4 (aerodrome directory), NOTAMs, and AIP Supplements. These are the five publication types the syllabus expects you to describe.
key_facts: AIP NZ: ENR (route/airspace), GEN (general), AD (aerodromes) sections
AIPNZ Vol 2 & 3: IFR procedure charts (SIDs, STARs, approach charts)
AIPNZ Vol 4: aerodrome directory with operational data
NOTAMs: timely operational changes; AIP Supplements: temporary AIP amendments
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Derive operational information from charts and publications that provide route, approach and aerodrome information
concept: The information you need to extract from charts and publications for an IFR flight falls into three categories. Route information: en route minimum altitudes (track distances, MEAs, MFAs), navigation aid frequencies and identifiers, reporting points, and airspace structure. Approach information: instrument approach charts give you the IAF, procedure track, altitudes for each segment, MDA/DA, missed approach procedure, and circling minima. Aerodrome information: runway dimensions, slope, surface, threshold elevation, lighting types, declared distances, fuel and handling availability. For IFR in NZ you must be able to work all of this from the AIP NZ publications before departure.
exam_tip: This is a 'derive/interpret' item — expect questions where you're given a chart excerpt and asked to read off specific values. Know the structure of an approach chart: plan view, profile view, and minima box.
key_facts: Route info: MEAs, MFAs, nav aid freq/ident, reporting points, airspace
Approach info: IAF, segment altitudes, MDA/DA, missed approach, circling minima
Aerodrome info: runway data, threshold elevations, lighting, declared distances
All sourced from AIPNZ Vol 2/3 (charts) and Vol 4 (aerodrome directory)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: State the meteorological minima at destination which would require an alternate to be nominated. CAR Part 91
concept: CAR rule 91.405(a) tells you when you MUST nominate an alternate. An alternate is not required if the destination has a standard instrument approach procedure published in the AIP AND the meteorological forecast shows that, for the period from one hour before to one hour after the estimated time of arrival, the ceiling will be at least 1000 ft above the minimum for the approach procedure being used, AND the visibility will be at least 5 km or at least 2 km more than the published minimum, whichever is greater. If either condition isn't met, you need an alternate on the flight plan. This is a numbers-based exam question — know the one-hour window and the ceiling/visibility thresholds.
exam_tip: The window is ±1 hour from ETA, not just at ETA. Ceiling must be ≥1000 ft above the approach minimum. Visibility must be ≥5 km OR ≥2 km above the procedure minimum, whichever is greater. All three conditions must be met to avoid nominating an alternate.
key_facts: Alternate required if forecast doesn't meet conditions in CAR rule 91.405(a)
Assessment period: one hour before to one hour after ETA at destination
Ceiling threshold: at least 1000 ft above published approach minimum
Visibility threshold: at least 5 km OR 2 km more than published minimum (greater applies)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91 rule 91.405(a)

syllabus_desc: Determine the meteorological minima required at an aerodrome for it to be nominated as an IFR alternate. CAR Part 91
concept: CAR rule 91.405(b) sets the meteorological minima an aerodrome must be forecast to meet at your ETA in order to qualify as an IFR alternate. For precision approaches: ceiling at least 600 ft or 200 ft above the DA, whichever is higher; visibility at least 3000 m or 1000 m more than the published minimum, whichever is greater. For non-precision approaches: ceiling at least 800 ft or 200 ft above MDA, whichever is higher; visibility at least 4000 m or 1500 m more than published minimum, whichever is greater. If the AIP publishes specific alternate minima for that aerodrome, use those instead. If no instrument approach at all, use VFR descent criteria.
exam_tip: Two tables to remember — precision vs non-precision. For precision: 600/200 ft ceiling and 3000/1000 m vis. For non-precision: 800/200 ft ceiling and 4000/1500 m vis. Always use the higher/greater value.
key_facts: Alternate minima in CAR rule 91.405(b)
Precision approach alternate: ceiling ≥600 ft or DA+200 ft (whichever higher); vis ≥3000 m or min+1000 m (greater)
Non-precision approach alternate: ceiling ≥800 ft or MDA+200 ft (whichever higher); vis ≥4000 m or min+1500 m (greater)
AIP-published alternate minima take precedence if available
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91 rule 91.405(b)

syllabus_desc: State the power supply requirements for the selection of an aerodrome as an alternate on an IFR air operation. CAR Part 91
concept: CAR Part 91 requires that for an aerodrome to be nominated as an alternate on an IFR flight in air operations, the aerodrome must have an adequate power supply for the lighting system serving the instrument approach procedure being planned. In practice, that means if you're nominating an aerodrome that relies on pilot-activated lighting (PAL) only and there's no backup power, it may not meet the requirement for an air operation alternate — though for private IFR flights the rule is somewhat less prescriptive. The intent is that when you arrive at your alternate, the approach lighting and nav aids will actually be working regardless of what the power situation is at the main aerodrome.
exam_tip: Power supply to the approach lighting system is the key requirement. An aerodrome that depends on PAL only or has no backup power for its approach lighting system is a problem as an air operation alternate.
key_facts: Alternate power supply requirement in CAR Part 91
Aerodrome nominated as alternate must have adequate power supply for approach lighting
Requirement ensures lighting and nav aids are available on arrival
More stringent for air operations (commercial) than private IFR
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the reference datum for take-off meteorological minima for IFR operations. CAR Part 91
concept: IFR take-off meteorological minima are referenced to the aerodrome in the NZ AIP Operational Data charts (AD 2 pages for each aerodrome). The datum for take-off minima is the Departure End of Runway (DER) or, more accurately, the aerodrome elevation. If take-off minima are not published for the runway being used, the default is: ceiling at least 300 ft and visibility greater than 1500 m. Reduced take-off minima (zero cloud ceiling, visibility ≥800 m) are permitted under CAR rule 91.413(g) if specific conditions apply — including centreline marking or lighting, the AD 2 permitting reduced minima, and for twin-engine propeller aircraft, auto-feather/auto-coarse.
exam_tip: Take-off reference datum is the aerodrome/DER. Default minima if not published: ceiling 300 ft, vis 1500 m. Reduced minima (0 ceiling/800 m vis) need specific conditions — this combination is tested frequently.
key_facts: Take-off minima referenced to the aerodrome/departure end of runway
Default if not published in AIP AD 2: ceiling 300 ft, visibility more than 1500 m
Reduced minima: 0 ceiling and vis ≥800 m under CAR rule 91.413(g) with specific conditions
Conditions for reduced minima include centreline markings/lights and AD 2 authorisation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91 rule 91.413, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: State the reference datum for landing meteorological minima for IFR operations. CAR Part 91
concept: Landing meteorological minima for IFR operations are referenced to the specific instrument approach procedure being used, as published in the AIP. The flight visibility must not be less than the visibility published in the AIP for that approach procedure. The reference point for the altitude minima (DA or MDA) is: DA is referenced to mean sea level (MSL); DH is referenced to the runway threshold elevation. So a 'DA of 400 ft' means 400 ft AMSL, while a 'DH of 200 ft' means 200 ft above the threshold. You can't descend below DA/DH unless you have the required visual reference and the flight visibility meets the published minimum.
exam_tip: The datum distinction: DA = above MSL, DH = above threshold. For exam questions about landing minima, the reference is the published figure in the AIP approach chart for the specific procedure — not a generic number.
key_facts: Landing minima referenced to published instrument approach procedure in AIP
DA referenced to MSL; DH referenced to runway threshold elevation
Flight visibility must not be less than published procedure minimum
Reference in CAR rule 91.413(d)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91 rule 91.413(d), AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State the reference datum for alternate meteorological minima for IFR operations. AIP ENR
concept: Alternate meteorological minima are referenced to the instrument approach procedure available at the alternate aerodrome, as published in AIP ENR. The reference datum matches the approach type at the alternate: DA (MSL) for precision approaches, MDA (MSL) or MDH (threshold) for non-precision. The figures are assessed against the minima in rule 91.405(b) — or against the AIP-published alternate minima if those exist for that aerodrome. When checking alternates for flight planning purposes, you work from the forecast weather at the alternate for your ETA, and compare against the applicable alternate minima from the table.
exam_tip: Alternate minima reference the approach at the ALTERNATE, not the destination. The datum is the same as for the approach type (DA for precision, MDA/MDH for non-precision). Source: AIP ENR.
key_facts: Alternate minima referenced to instrument approach at the alternate aerodrome
Source: AIP ENR and CAR rule 91.405(b)
Precision alternate: DA referenced to MSL
Non-precision alternate: MDA referenced to MSL, MDH to threshold
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91 rule 91.405(b), AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State the fuel reserve required for an IFR flight in a non-turbine-powered aeroplane. CAR Part 91
concept: CAR rule 91.403 sets the IFR fuel requirements for non-turbine aircraft (piston). If no alternate is required: you need enough fuel to reach the destination AND continue for 45 minutes at holding speed at 1500 ft above the destination aerodrome. If an alternate is required: you need fuel to reach the destination, proceed to the alternate, AND then continue for 45 minutes at holding speed at 1500 ft. The 45-minute final reserve is the non-negotiable buffer — you never plan to arrive at an aerodrome with less than that. In practice, the planning looks like: destination fuel + alternate fuel + 45 min holding reserve + taxi fuel + any additional planning fuel.
exam_tip: Non-turbine piston: 45 minutes final reserve at holding speed, 1500 ft. With alternate: destination + alternate + 45 min. Without alternate: destination + 45 min. The 1500 ft holding speed figure is in the rule.
key_facts: Piston/non-turbine IFR fuel reserve in CAR rule 91.403
No alternate required: fuel to destination + 45 minutes at holding speed/1500 ft
Alternate required: fuel to destination + alternate + 45 minutes at holding speed/1500 ft
45-minute final reserve is the minimum regardless of other planning factors
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91 rule 91.403

syllabus_desc: State the fuel reserve required for an IFR flight in a turbine-powered aeroplane or a helicopter. CAR Part 91
concept: CAR rule 91.403 also covers turbine-powered aeroplanes and helicopters. The final reserve for turbine aircraft is 30 minutes (not 45) at holding speed at 1500 ft. So: if no alternate required — fuel to destination + 30 min holding reserve. If alternate required — fuel to destination + alternate + 30 min holding reserve. The reason turbine gets 30 not 45 is partly that turbine aircraft tend to have more reliable fuel management and known consumption rates, and partly that 30 minutes at a turbine holding speed still represents a substantial fuel reserve. Helicopters use the same 30-minute figure.
exam_tip: Turbine and helicopter: 30 minutes. Piston: 45 minutes. This is one of the most tested numbers in IFR law. Don't mix them up.
key_facts: Turbine/helicopter IFR fuel reserve in CAR rule 91.403
No alternate required: fuel to destination + 30 minutes at holding speed/1500 ft
Alternate required: fuel to destination + alternate + 30 minutes at holding speed/1500 ft
30 minutes (not 45 minutes) applies to all turbine aircraft and helicopters
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91 rule 91.403

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for the filing of a flight plan for flight under IFR. CAR Part 91
concept: CAR rule 91.407 requires the PIC of an IFR flight to submit a flight plan to the appropriate ATS unit before the flight. A flight plan for IFR must include: aircraft identification, aircraft type and wake turbulence category, radio and navigation equipment, departure aerodrome, departure time, cruising speed, cruising altitude, IFR route, destination aerodrome, estimated elapsed time, alternate aerodrome, fuel endurance, number of persons on board, emergency and survival equipment, and PIC contact details. For VFR-to-IFR transitions (e.g. departing VFR and picking up an IFR clearance enroute), the initial plan still needs to cover the IFR portion.
exam_tip: Know the required items. Common exam trap: fuel endurance (not fuel quantity) and number of persons on board (not passenger count — includes crew) must both be in the IFR flight plan.
key_facts: IFR flight plan required under CAR rule 91.407
Must include: aircraft ID, type, wake category, radio/nav equipment, route, levels, timing
Also required: alternate, fuel endurance, persons on board, emergency equipment
Submitted to ATS unit before flight
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91 rule 91.407

syllabus_desc: State the notification lead time for filing an IFR flight plan. CAR Part 91 & AIP ENR
concept: CAR rule 91.407 requires the IFR flight plan to be submitted at least 30 minutes before the intended departure time. AIP ENR 1.10 may have additional local requirements. If there's a delay of more than 30 minutes after the filed departure time, you must advise ATS. The 30-minute lead time exists so that ATC can process the plan, issue the clearance, and have it ready by the time you call start. In practice at busy IFR aerodromes (Auckland, Christchurch, Wellington), you may be expected to file even earlier to allow for airways clearance delivery processing.
exam_tip: 30 minutes is the minimum lead time for filing an IFR flight plan. If departing more than 30 minutes late, you must advise ATS.
key_facts: IFR flight plan must be filed at least 30 minutes before departure
Requirement in CAR rule 91.407
Delays exceeding 30 minutes from filed departure time must be advised to ATS
AIP ENR 1.10 may specify additional local requirements
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91 rule 91.407, AIP ENR 1.10

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for adhering to an IFR flight plan. CAR Part 91
concept: Once you're IFR and have a clearance, CAR rule 91.409 requires you to adhere to the filed flight plan unless you've obtained a new or amended clearance from ATC, or an emergency makes deviation necessary. That means route, levels, and speed profile. You can't just decide to cut a corner or change to a different airway without a new clearance. The rule reflects the fundamental principle of IFR: ATC is building a traffic picture based on what's in the system. If you deviate unannounced, you blow a hole in that picture and create a potential conflict.
exam_tip: Adherence is required unless ATC clearance obtained or emergency. You must notify ATC of any deviation — you can't just do it.
key_facts: Must adhere to flight plan under CAR rule 91.409
Deviation only permitted with ATC clearance, or in emergency
Any deviation must be reported to ATC as soon as practicable
Rule exists to maintain the ATC traffic picture for separation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91 rule 91.409

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for the notification of changes to a filed IFR flight plan. CAR Part 91
concept: CAR rule 91.407 requires you to notify ATS of any significant changes to a filed IFR flight plan — including changes to the destination, route, or cruising level — before making the change where practicable, or as soon as possible after. For in-flight changes, the process is normally to request an amended clearance from ATC, which both changes the clearance and updates the flight plan simultaneously. Changes to the alternate or other non-structural elements can also be notified in flight. The underlying rule is that the ATS system needs to reflect what you're actually doing.
exam_tip: Notify ATS of changes — ideally before you make them, otherwise immediately after. In controlled airspace, the mechanism is an amended clearance request.
key_facts: Changes to filed IFR flight plan must be notified to ATS
Requirement in CAR rule 91.407
Notify before making the change where practicable; otherwise immediately after
In controlled airspace, request amended clearance which updates the plan
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91 rule 91.407

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for an inadvertent departure from an IFR flight plan. CAR Part 91
concept: CAR rule 91.411 covers what happens if you inadvertently depart from the filed plan enroute. You're required to report to ATS as soon as practicable if: you deviate from your cleared track; your average true airspeed between reporting points varies by 5% or more; your Mach number varies by 0.02 or more; or your ETA at the next significant point or aerodrome of first intended landing is in error by more than two minutes. Once you identify the deviation, you must regain the cleared track or altitude as soon as practicable. The intent is to keep ATC informed so separation with other aircraft can be maintained.
exam_tip: The four triggers for mandatory reporting: track deviation, ±5% TAS variation, ±0.02 Mach variation, ETA error >2 minutes. Know all four — they're a common multi-part question.
key_facts: Inadvertent departure from flight plan covered in CAR rule 91.411
Must report to ATS: track deviation, ≥5% TAS variation, ≥0.02 Mach variation, ETA error >2 minutes
