Must regain cleared track/level as soon as practicable
Purpose: maintain ATC traffic picture for separation assurance
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91 rule 91.411

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for the terminating an IFR flight plan at an aerodrome without ATS. CAR Part 91
concept: When you land at an aerodrome that doesn't have an ATS unit (an unattended aerodrome), you're responsible for closing your own IFR flight plan. CAR Part 91 requires you to notify ATS as soon as practicable after landing. The normal procedure is to contact the appropriate ATS unit by radio (or phone if radio isn't available) and advise them of your arrival. If you don't close the plan within a reasonable time after your estimated arrival, ATS will initiate a check on your welfare — which can eventually trigger a search and rescue response. So closing the plan promptly is both a regulatory requirement and good airmanship.
exam_tip: At non-ATS aerodromes you close your own flight plan — it doesn't happen automatically. Contact ATS by radio or phone after landing. Failure to close can trigger SAR.
key_facts: At aerodromes without ATS, PIC must terminate IFR flight plan after landing
Requirement in CAR Part 91
Contact ATS by radio or phone as soon as practicable after landing
Failure to close plan can initiate ATS welfare check and potentially SAR
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Derive from operational publications, the required radio frequency for communicating with specified ATC units
concept: The required frequencies for communicating with ATC units enroute under IFR are published in the AIP NZ, specifically in the ENR sections and on the relevant enroute charts. Each ATS unit (Auckland Centre, Wellington Approach, Christchurch Approach, etc.) has a published primary frequency and often a secondary or area frequency. On the instrument approach charts for each aerodrome, the applicable frequencies for approach, tower, ground, and ATIS are listed. When you depart a frequency area you must contact the next unit on the published frequency before or at the transfer point. The frequencies can also be found in the AIPNZ Volume 4 aerodrome directory.
exam_tip: This is a practical/chart-reading item. Frequencies are derived from the AIP — specifically enroute charts and approach charts. Know where to find them rather than trying to memorise specific frequencies.
key_facts: ATC frequencies published in AIP NZ ENR sections and enroute charts
Approach and aerodrome frequencies on instrument approach charts
Also available in AIPNZ Volume 4 aerodrome directory
Contact next unit before or at the transfer point shown on charts
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for making position reports to an ATS unit. CAR Part 91 & AIP ENR
concept: CAR Part 91 and AIP ENR 1.01 require IFR pilots to make position reports to the ATS unit when: passing compulsory reporting points; reaching or leaving an assigned altitude or level; changing flight level; reaching a holding point; at intervals of not more than 30 minutes when enroute; prior to entering controlled airspace; and prior to commencing an instrument approach at an unattended aerodrome. In a radar environment, ATC may cancel position reporting requirements since they can track you on radar. When position reporting is required, it must be made on the designated frequency for the sector.
exam_tip: Key triggers for mandatory position reports: compulsory reporting points, level changes, holding fixes, 30-minute intervals, controlled airspace entry, commencing instrument approach at unattended aerodrome. Radar cancels most of these.
key_facts: Position report requirements in CAR Part 91 and AIP ENR 1.01
Required at compulsory reporting points, level changes, holding, 30-minute max intervals
Required prior to entering controlled airspace and commencing approach at unattended aerodrome
ATC may cancel requirement when providing radar service
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91, AIP ENR 1.01

syllabus_desc: State the contents of various IFR position reports. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.02 specifies the content of IFR position reports. A standard position report contains in order: (1) aircraft callsign; (2) position (navigation aid or reporting point); (3) time (minutes past the hour, actual time at position, not transmission time); (4) flight level or altitude to nearest 100 ft (if climbing or descending, state 'climbing to' or 'descending to' and the assigned level); (5) next position and estimated time over it; (6) ETA at aerodrome of first intended landing (when outside controlled airspace and not on designated reporting points). The order matters — ATC expects the standard format.
exam_tip: Six elements in order: callsign, position, time, level/altitude, next position+ETA, destination ETA. The time element catches students — it's actual time at the position, not when you transmit the report.
key_facts: IFR position report content specified in AIP ENR 1.02
Six elements: callsign, position, time (actual at position), level, next position+ETA, destination ETA
Time is actual time at the reporting point — not the time of transmission
If climbing or descending, state 'climbing to' or 'descending to' assigned level
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.02

syllabus_desc: State the purpose of Universal Communications Services (UNICOM). AIP GEN
concept: UNICOM is a common communication facility operating on a designated frequency (typically 130.1 MHz in NZ) at certain aerodromes. It's not an ATS unit — it's a non-ATS communication service. In NZ, UNICOM is used primarily for flight plan termination at attended aerodromes where there's no AFIS or ATC, and for communication with ground-based personnel at those aerodromes. Pilots can also use UNICOM to get aerodrome information like runway in use and surface wind. The key point: UNICOM doesn't provide separation, traffic information, or ATC services — it's a communication link only.
exam_tip: UNICOM is NOT an ATS. It's a communication facility — no separation, no traffic service. Common use: flight plan closure at attended but non-controlled aerodromes.
key_facts: UNICOM is a non-ATS communication facility
Used for flight plan termination and general aerodrome communications
Does not provide ATC separation or official traffic information
Found at attended aerodromes without full ATC/AFIS services
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP GEN

syllabus_desc: State the purpose of an Aerodrome Frequency Response Unit (AFRU). AIP GEN
concept: An Aerodrome Frequency Response Unit (AFRU) is an automated device at unattended aerodromes that transmits a short response when a pilot calls on the MBZ or CTAF frequency. The response confirms that the radio is working and gives a brief aerodrome information broadcast (current wind, QNH if available). The AFRU doesn't provide ATS — it's just an automated acknowledgement system. It's particularly useful for pilots arriving at remote aerodromes at night or in IMC to confirm their radio is functional before committing to an approach.
exam_tip: AFRU = automated response unit at unattended aerodrome. It responds to your radio call to confirm radio function and gives basic info. It is NOT an ATS and provides NO separation.
key_facts: AFRU is an automated device at unattended aerodromes
Responds to radio calls on MBZ/CTAF frequency with confirmation and basic aerodrome info
Not an ATS — provides no separation or traffic advisory service
Useful for confirming radio function before committing to approach
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP GEN

syllabus_desc: State the purpose of Aerodrome and Weather Information Broadcasts (AWIB). AIP GEN
concept: An AWIB (Aerodrome and Weather Information Broadcast) is an automatic, continuous broadcast of aerodrome operational information and local weather on a published frequency. CAR Part 1 defines it as: 'an automatic broadcast of aerodrome and weather information provided specifically for the facilitation of aviation, and for the avoidance of doubt, an AWIB service is not an air traffic service.' The broadcast typically includes runway in use, circuit direction, QNH, wind, temperature, and any operational NOTAMs for the aerodrome. Pilots use it to get current aerodrome information before making radio calls or committing to an approach.
exam_tip: AWIB is automatic and continuous — you tune and listen, you don't call. It is explicitly NOT an ATS (this is in the Part 1 definition). ATIS at controlled aerodromes serves a similar function but is more comprehensive.
key_facts: AWIB defined in CAR Part 1 as automatic broadcast of aerodrome and weather information
Explicitly stated in Part 1: an AWIB service is NOT an air traffic service
Continuous broadcast on published frequency — pilot listens passively
Typically contains: runway in use, circuit, QNH, wind, temperature, operational NOTAMs
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 1 rule 1.1, AIP GEN

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the various light signals from a control tower. CAR Part 91 & AIP AD
concept: Light signals from an aerodrome control tower are used when radio communication isn't possible. The signals are given with a signal lamp and have the following meanings. For aircraft in flight: steady green = cleared to land; steady red = give way to other aircraft and continue circling; flashing green = return for landing; flashing red = aerodrome unsafe, do not land; flashing white = land at this aerodrome and proceed to apron; alternating red and green = danger, be on the alert. For aircraft on the ground: steady green = cleared for take-off; steady red = stop; flashing green = cleared to taxi; flashing red = taxi clear of landing area in use; flashing white = return to starting point; alternating red and green = danger, be on the alert.
exam_tip: Learn the table for both in-flight and on-ground signals. The most tested pair: steady green in flight = cleared to land; steady green on ground = cleared for take-off. Alternating red and green always means danger/be alert, in flight or ground.
key_facts: In flight: steady green = cleared to land; steady red = give way and circle
In flight: flashing green = return for landing; flashing red = aerodrome unsafe
On ground: steady green = take-off clearance; steady red = stop
On ground/in flight: alternating red/green = danger, be on alert
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91, AIP AD 1.10

syllabus_desc: State the communications requirements when TIBA procedures are in force. AIP ENR
concept: TIBA (Traffic Information Broadcasts by Aircraft) is a temporary procedure introduced by NOTAM when an ATS unit fails or loses the ability to provide a normal service. Under TIBA, pilots take over the responsibility for broadcasting their position and intentions on the published TIBA frequency (128.95 MHz for Auckland Oceanic FIR; published in AIP ENR for NZ FIR). The procedure: listen for 10 minutes before entering TIBA airspace; broadcast 10 minutes before entering, then at 10-minute intervals enroute, 2-5 minutes before any level change, and at the level change itself. Broadcasts include callsign, position, flight level, route, and intentions. You still maintain your IFR flight — TIBA is not a switch to VFR.
exam_tip: TIBA activates by NOTAM when ATS fails. Key numbers: 10-minute listen before entry, 10-minute broadcast intervals, 2-5 minutes before level changes. Frequency: 128.95 MHz for Auckland Oceanic FIR.
key_facts: TIBA introduced by NOTAM when ATS communication fails
Pilots broadcast position/intentions on published TIBA frequency
128.95 MHz for Auckland Oceanic FIR; check AIP for NZ FIR frequency
Listen 10 min before entry; broadcast every 10 min; broadcast 2-5 min before level changes
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.15

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for complying with ATC clearances and instructions. CAR Part 91 & AIP ENR
concept: CAR rule 91.245 and AIP ENR 1.01 require pilots of IFR flights to comply with ATC clearances and instructions. Once you accept a clearance, you're required to fly it. An ATC clearance authorises you to proceed under the specified conditions — it doesn't guarantee separation from everything (traffic in Class G below, for example), but in controlled airspace, full separation is provided. The readback requirement is part of compliance — you read back route, levels, and key instructions to confirm you've received them correctly. The controller must verify your readback before you proceed. If the clearance is wrong or unsafe, you must advise ATC and request amendment — not just ignore it.
exam_tip: Accept and comply is the core rule. Readback is mandatory for route, levels, and specific instructions. If the clearance contains something unsafe, advise ATC — don't just fly a different route without telling them.
key_facts: Compliance with ATC clearances required under CAR rule 91.245 and AIP ENR 1.01
Accepted clearance must be flown as issued
Readback of route, levels, and key instructions is mandatory
Request amendment if clearance is incorrect or unsafe — don't silently deviate
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91 rule 91.245, AIP ENR 1.01

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for coordinating with an aerodrome flight information service. CAR Part 91
concept: An Aerodrome Flight Information Service (AFIS) is an ATS provided at some attended aerodromes that falls short of full ATC. AFIS operators provide traffic, weather, and aerodrome information, but do NOT issue clearances or provide separation. CAR Part 91 requires pilots intending to operate at an AFIS aerodrome to coordinate with the AFIS before entering the circuit or manoeuvring area. In practice this means calling the AFIS unit, receiving the runway in use, traffic information, and QNH, and making your intentions known. Because there's no separation service, the pilot remains responsible for see-and-avoid and traffic conflict avoidance.
exam_tip: AFIS provides information, NOT separation. Pilot is responsible for their own separation at AFIS aerodromes. Coordination (not clearance) is required before entering the circuit.
key_facts: AFIS requirement in CAR Part 91
AFIS provides information service, not separation service
Pilot must coordinate with AFIS before entering circuit or manoeuvring area
Pilot remains responsible for see-and-avoid — no ATC separation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for receiving an ATC clearance prior to entering various types of airspace, and ground manoeuvring area. CAR Part 91 & AIP ENR
concept: CAR rule 91.245 and AIP ENR 1.01 require an ATC clearance before operating IFR in controlled airspace (Classes A, C, D) and before operating on the manoeuvring area at a controlled aerodrome. In Class A airspace, all IFR and VFR flights require ATC clearance. In Class C, IFR flights require clearance and VFR flights receive a separation service from IFR. In Class D, ATC is provided but VFR pilots are responsible for separation from other VFR. For the manoeuvring area (runways, taxiways at controlled aerodromes), a clearance is required for any movement. You request clearance via radio before entering the boundary.
exam_tip: Classes A, C, D all require ATC clearance for IFR. The manoeuvring area at a controlled aerodrome also requires clearance. Know the airspace classes in NZ — this maps to the Part 71 structure.
key_facts: ATC clearance required for IFR in Class A, C, D airspace per CAR rule 91.245
Clearance also required for manoeuvring area at controlled aerodromes
Request before entering airspace boundary or manoeuvring area
Class A: all aircraft require clearance; Class C/D: IFR requires clearance
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91 rule 91.245, AIP ENR 1.01

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for receiving an ATC clearance prior to re-entering controlled airspace. CAR Part 91 & AIP ENR
concept: If you leave controlled airspace (e.g. on a diversion, weather avoidance, or planned departure) and then need to re-enter, you must obtain an ATC clearance before re-entering. You can't simply re-enter on your original clearance if you've left the airspace boundary. AIP ENR 1.01 and CAR Part 91 are clear on this. The practical procedure is to call ATC before you reach the boundary, advise that you've left and wish to re-enter, and request a new clearance. This prevents ATC from being surprised by unexpected targets appearing in the controlled airspace picture.
exam_tip: Clearance required to RE-ENTER controlled airspace — you can't automatically revert to your old clearance. Must contact ATC and request re-entry clearance before the boundary.
key_facts: ATC clearance required before re-entering controlled airspace
Requirement in CAR Part 91 and AIP ENR 1.01
Contact ATC before reaching boundary to request re-entry clearance
Cannot assume original clearance still applies after leaving controlled airspace
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91, AIP ENR 1.01

syllabus_desc: Describe the situations where Air Traffic Control is responsible for the provision of separation between VFR, SVFR and IFR traffic. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.01 sets out which traffic ATC is responsible for separating. ATC provides separation between IFR flights in controlled airspace (Classes A, C, D), between IFR and SVFR flights in a CTR, and between SVFR flights in a CTR when weather is below VMC. In Class C airspace, ATC also separates IFR from VFR. In Class D, ATC provides traffic information about VFR flights to IFR aircraft, but the IFR pilot is responsible for maintaining their own separation from VFR in some scenarios. The key rule: in Class A, everything is separated by ATC. Below Class A, the extent of ATC-provided separation depends on the airspace class and flight type.
exam_tip: ATC separates IFR from IFR in all controlled airspace. ATC separates IFR from VFR in Class A and C. IFR from VFR in Class D is more nuanced. Always read the airspace classification carefully.
key_facts: ATC separation responsibilities in AIP ENR 1.01
Class A: ATC separates all flights (IFR and VFR)
Class C: ATC separates IFR from IFR and IFR from VFR
Class D: ATC separates IFR from IFR; provides info on VFR
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.01

syllabus_desc: Describe the situations where the PIC of an IFR flight is responsible for maintaining separation from other traffic. AIP ENR
concept: The PIC of an IFR flight is responsible for maintaining separation from other traffic when: operating outside controlled airspace (Class G) — there's no ATC separation service there; operating in Class D airspace from VFR traffic (ATC only provides traffic information); operating in VMC under an own separation clearance when ATC has transferred that responsibility; or when TCAS issues a Resolution Advisory (RA) — the pilot follows the RA, overriding the ATC clearance if necessary. The basic principle in uncontrolled airspace is see-and-avoid — exactly as for VFR, except you're in IMC and can't see anything, which is why IFR pilots in Class G need to be flying published procedures where practical.
exam_tip: PIC is responsible for separation in Class G airspace and in some Class D scenarios. TCAS RA overrides ATC — pilot follows the RA and then advises ATC.
key_facts: PIC responsible for separation in Class G (uncontrolled) airspace
PIC responsible for separation from VFR in some Class D situations
TCAS RA must be followed — overrides ATC clearance
Own separation clearance from ATC transfers responsibility to PIC
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.01

syllabus_desc: Describe the normal separation standards applied by ATC. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.01 specifies normal separation standards applied by ATC. Vertical separation is 1000 ft below FL290, and 2000 ft above FL290 (RVSM airspace reduces this to 1000 ft between FL290 and FL410 for approved aircraft). Horizontal separation includes lateral and longitudinal components. Longitudinal separation in the domestic NZ FIR is typically time-based (e.g. 10 minutes between same-track same-level aircraft) or distance-based using DME or RNAV. Lateral separation is achieved by assigning different tracks, airways, or navigation aids. ATC will always use the applicable standard for the airspace and equipment available.
exam_tip: Vertical: 1000 ft below FL290, 2000 ft above FL290, 1000 ft in RVSM. These three numbers are exam staples. RVSM applies between FL290 and FL410 for approved aircraft.
key_facts: Normal separation standards in AIP ENR 1.01
Vertical: 1000 ft below FL290; 2000 ft at and above FL290
RVSM reduces vertical to 1000 ft between FL290 and FL410
Horizontal separation by time, distance, or track difference
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.01

syllabus_desc: Describe the situations where the normal separation may be reduced. AIP ENR
concept: Normal separation can be reduced in specific circumstances listed in AIP ENR 1.01. For vertical separation: can be reduced to 500 ft within controlled airspace if both aircraft are medium or light category AND the lower is VFR/SVFR at 4500 ft or below. In RVSM airspace, approved aircraft can operate at 1000 ft vertical separation above FL290. Longitudinal separation can be reduced when aircraft will soon report passing each other, or when the controller knows they are laterally separated. The principles are that separation can only be reduced when the controller has sufficient information to confirm the safety of the reduced standard.
exam_tip: 500 ft reduced vertical is available for VFR/SVFR light/medium aircraft at 4500 ft and below. RVSM reduces vertical above FL290 to 1000 ft for approved aircraft only. Both are tested.
key_facts: Reduced separation standards in AIP ENR 1.01
500 ft vertical: light/medium aircraft, lower is VFR/SVFR, at 4500 ft or below in controlled airspace
RVSM 1000 ft vertical: between FL290 and FL410 for RVSM-approved aircraft
Longitudinal reduction permitted when aircraft passing or known laterally separated
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.01

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the term "Essential Traffic". AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.01 defines Essential Traffic as: 'that controlled traffic to which the provision of separation is applicable, but which, in relation to a particular controlled flight, is not separated by the prescribed minima.' In plain English: essential traffic is traffic you should be separated from, but aren't — because separation has temporarily broken down or hasn't been established yet. When ATC declares another aircraft as 'essential traffic' to you, they're telling you that normal separation doesn't currently exist, and they're giving you the information you need to take your own action to avoid the conflict.
exam_tip: Essential Traffic = traffic you should be separated from, but currently aren't. ATC uses the phrase 'essential traffic' to warn you that normal separation doesn't apply, and gives you traffic information so you can take avoiding action.
key_facts: Essential Traffic defined in AIP ENR 1.01
Means: controlled traffic to which separation applies but prescribed minima are not currently met
ATC declares essential traffic to warn of a separation breakdown
Pilot must take action to avoid the conflict when essential traffic is declared
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.01

syllabus_desc: State the conditions under which longitudinal separation between reciprocal track aircraft may be reduced. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.01 allows longitudinal separation between aircraft on reciprocal tracks to be reduced when pilots are willing to report sighting and passing each other, in either day or night conditions. This is a pilot-responsibility arrangement — ATC tells both pilots about each other (essential traffic), the pilots look for each other, and once they've passed they report to ATC. This is only safe in VMC or near VMC conditions where pilots can actually make visual contact. In solid IMC, you'd rely on other separation methods. The reduction is a practical tool used when traffic is dense and standard longitudinal separation would cause excessive delays.
exam_tip: Reciprocal track longitudinal separation can be reduced if BOTH pilots agree to report sighting and passing each other. Works day or night. Not limited to day VMC — but only makes sense when visual contact is possible.
key_facts: Reciprocal track longitudinal separation reduction in AIP ENR 1.01
Permitted when pilots willing to report sighting and passing each other
Applicable in day or night conditions
Pilots must positively identify and report passing each other to ATC
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.01

syllabus_desc: State the minimum lateral and longitudinal separation between RNP10 aircraft, as permitted by ICAO Regional Supplementary procedures (Doc 7030). AIP ENR
concept: RNP10 is a navigation specification used for oceanic operations where aircraft must have a 95% containment within 10 NM of centreline. ICAO Regional Supplementary Procedures (Doc 7030) set the minimum separation standards for RNP10 operations in the South Pacific. These specify lateral separation of 50 NM (for lateral separation) and longitudinal separation of 50 NM at Mach 0.85 or above, or 80 NM at speeds below Mach 0.85, between same-track RNP10 aircraft. For New Zealand operators, this applies to oceanic tracks in the Auckland Oceanic FIR.
exam_tip: RNP10 separation: 50 NM lateral between parallel tracks; longitudinal 50 NM (above M0.85) or 80 NM (below M0.85). These are Auckland Oceanic FIR figures per ICAO Doc 7030.
key_facts: RNP10 separation based on ICAO Doc 7030 and AIP ENR
Lateral separation between parallel RNP10 tracks: 50 NM
Longitudinal separation: 50 NM at Mach 0.85+, or 80 NM below Mach 0.85
Applies in Auckland Oceanic FIR for oceanic operations
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR, ICAO Doc 7030

syllabus_desc: State the deviation from an assigned indicated airspeed or Mach number and ETA outside of which pilots are required to notify ATC. CAR Part 91
concept: CAR Part 91 and AIP ENR 1.01 require pilots to notify ATC when their average true airspeed between reporting points deviates by 5% or more from the filed/assigned speed, or when the Mach number deviates by 0.02 or more. For ETA: if your estimate for the next significant point or destination is in error by more than 2 minutes, you must immediately notify ATC with a corrected estimate. These thresholds exist because ATC uses speed and time information to maintain longitudinal separation — if your speed or timing changes significantly, their separation calculations may no longer be valid.
exam_tip: Three numbers: ±5% TAS, ±0.02 Mach, >2 minutes ETA error. All three trigger mandatory notification to ATC. These appear together and separately in exam questions.
key_facts: ATC notification required for speed/ETA deviations per CAR Part 91 and AIP ENR 1.01
Speed: notify if average TAS varies by ≥5% from filed/assigned speed
Mach: notify if Mach number varies by ≥0.02
ETA: notify immediately if estimate error exceeds 2 minutes
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91 rule 91.411, AIP ENR 1.01

syllabus_desc: State the wake turbulence separation requirements for light and medium aircraft. AIP AD
concept: Wake turbulence separation is published in AIP AD 1.07. For light aircraft following a medium aircraft: minimum 5 NM distance-based separation when directly behind or crossing within 1000 ft below. Time-based separation for arrivals on the same runway: medium behind heavy = 2 minutes minimum; light behind heavy = 3 minutes minimum; light behind medium = 3 minutes minimum. ATC applies these automatically in the approach sequence — you don't need to calculate them, but you need to know what they are and be able to spot if ATC is sequencing you too close. Wake turbulence is particularly serious on final approach when you're slow and low.
exam_tip: Light behind medium: 5 NM or 3 minutes. Light behind heavy: 6 NM or 3 minutes. Medium behind heavy: 3 NM or 2 minutes. These are the most commonly tested wake turbulence separation values.
key_facts: Wake turbulence separation in AIP AD 1.07
Light behind medium: 5 NM distance-based; 3 minutes time-based
Light behind heavy: 6 NM distance-based; 3 minutes time-based
Medium behind heavy: 3 NM distance-based; 2 minutes time-based
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP AD 1.07

syllabus_desc: State the maximum airspeed below 10,000 feet. CAR Part 91
concept: CAR Part 91 imposes a 250 KIAS speed limit below 10,000 ft AMSL for all aircraft, with an additional limit of 185 KIAS within 4 NM of a CTR aerodrome below 2500 ft AGL. These limits are in the rules themselves. ATC may grant exceptions above 250 kt below 10,000 ft when operationally necessary (e.g. for military or performance requirements), but pilots must request this specifically. The limits exist to reduce closing speeds between aircraft and improve ATC's ability to sequence traffic efficiently at lower altitudes.
exam_tip: 250 KIAS below 10,000 ft is the headline number. 185 KIAS within 4 NM of CTR below 2500 ft AGL is the secondary restriction. Both come from CAR Part 91.
key_facts: Speed limit 250 KIAS below 10,000 ft AMSL per CAR Part 91
Speed limit 185 KIAS below 2500 ft AGL within 4 NM of CTR aerodrome
ATC may grant exceptions on request for operational necessity
Limits apply to all aircraft categories
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the minimum descent height in IMC at an unattended aerodrome where traffic conflict may exist. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.05 specifies that at unattended aerodromes, when an IFR approach is being flown, pilots must not descend in IMC below 1200 ft above aerodrome elevation where traffic conflict may exist. This is a minimum height designed to ensure that you're above the traffic circuit height (which is typically 1000 ft above aerodrome elevation) until you can either see and avoid circuit traffic, or confirm there's no conflict. The rule is particularly important because at unattended aerodromes there's no ATC providing separation — you could be mixing with VFR aircraft in the circuit.
exam_tip: 1200 ft above aerodrome elevation is the minimum descent height in IMC at unattended aerodrome where traffic conflict may exist. It's 200 ft above the typical circuit height of 1000 ft — the buffer allows you to be established and see before descending into the circuit.
key_facts: Minimum 1200 ft above aerodrome elevation for descent in IMC at unattended aerodrome
Applies where traffic conflict may exist
Specified in AIP ENR 1.05
Keeps you above VFR circuit traffic before you can establish visual contact
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: Describe the determination of the minimum safe altitude for IFR flight. AIP GEN
concept: The minimum safe altitude (MSA) for IFR flight is determined from whichever source gives the lowest usable altitude for the route sector: the Minimum Flight Altitude (MFA) published on NZ enroute charts for each route segment; the altitudes shown on VORSEC charts after each descent step; or the 25 NM Minimum Sector Altitude diagrams around nav aids. All of these provide at least 1000 ft clearance above the highest obstacle within the defined area (or 2000 ft in mountainous zones), and they assume no nav aid errors — you're expected to be tracking the route with at least the normal navigation tolerance.
exam_tip: Three sources for MSA: MFA on enroute charts, VORSEC chart steps, 25nm MSA diagrams. All provide ≥1000 ft clearance (≥2000 ft in mountainous zones). Use whichever gives the LOWEST altitude for the sector.
key_facts: MSA for IFR determined from AIP GEN and published charts
Sources: MFA on enroute charts, VORSEC chart steps, 25 NM MSA diagrams
All guarantee ≥1000 ft obstacle clearance (≥2000 ft mountainous)
Use the lowest applicable figure for the route sector
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP GEN, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: Explain the coverage and use of VORSEC charts. AIP GEN
concept: VORSEC charts are published for New Zealand and show a series of altitude steps based on DME distance from a VOR/DME. The chart is used during the descent from enroute altitude to the approach commencement altitude when flying via a VOR. As you track inbound on the VOR radial and descend through each step, the VORSEC chart tells you the minimum altitude you must be at before passing a specific DME distance — and that minimum guarantees obstacle clearance for the terrain around the VOR. The steps get progressively lower as you get closer. If you can't maintain the VORSEC step because of performance or other factors, you must maintain the previous (higher) step.
exam_tip: VORSEC = VOR Sector Altitude Chart. It's DME-step-based: at X NM from VOR you may descend to Y ft. The steps guarantee terrain clearance. Must meet each step BEFORE descending further.
key_facts: VORSEC charts in AIP GEN and on approach charts
Provide DME-based altitude steps for descent via VOR
Each step guarantees obstacle clearance for that sector
Must be at or above the step altitude before descending through it
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP GEN

syllabus_desc: Explain the coverage and use of 25nm Minimum Sector Altitude diagrams. AIP GEN
concept: 25 NM Minimum Sector Altitude (MSA 25M) diagrams are published on NZ enroute and area charts. Each diagram shows the MSA for sectors within 25 NM of a designated nav aid or fix. The diagram is divided into sectors (typically compass quadrants or octants), and each sector has its own MSA that guarantees at least 1000 ft clearance above all obstacles within that sector within 25 NM. These diagrams are used when you need to determine the lowest safe altitude for manoeuvring or transitioning in the vicinity of a nav aid before beginning a published approach.
exam_tip: 25nm MSA diagrams are sector-based — different sectors can have different MSAs. They're the first thing you check on an approach chart to determine the minimum safe holding or manoeuvring altitude before the procedure begins.
key_facts: 25 NM MSA diagrams on NZ enroute and approach charts
Show minimum safe altitude for sectors within 25 NM of a nav aid
Each sector guarantees ≥1000 ft clearance above all obstacles
Used for manoeuvring/holding before commencing a published procedure
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP GEN

syllabus_desc: State when the radar control service is responsible for the provision of terrain clearance. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.05 and 1.06 explain that when an aircraft is being radar controlled (i.e. receiving a radar control service), the radar controller is responsible for terrain clearance when the aircraft is established at or above the minimum radar terrain contour level published for that area. When the controller issues radar vectors, they maintain the aircraft above the Minimum Radar Vectoring Altitude (MRVA) which guarantees at least 1000 ft (2000 ft in designated mountainous areas) clearance above obstacles. The pilot is NOT responsible for terrain clearance during vectoring — that responsibility sits with the controller. If the controller loses radar contact or issues a cleared altitude lower than MRVA, the controller should advise the pilot.
exam_tip: When being radar vectored: terrain clearance is the CONTROLLER'S responsibility, not the pilot's. This shifts back to the pilot when vectoring ceases and the pilot resumes own navigation.
key_facts: Radar controller responsible for terrain clearance during radar vectoring
Applies when aircraft established at or above minimum radar terrain contour level
MRVA guarantees ≥1000 ft clearance (≥2000 ft mountainous)
Responsibility shifts back to pilot when radar service ceases
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05, AIP ENR 1.06

syllabus_desc: Explain how radar control provides terrain clearance. AIP ENR
concept: Radar control provides terrain clearance by issuing altitude clearances that keep the aircraft above the Minimum Radar Vectoring Altitude (MRVA) for the sector being flown. MRVAs are published on radar controller charts and reflect the minimum altitude above which the controller can guarantee obstacle clearance. The controller monitors the aircraft's altitude via Mode C/S transponder and issues altitude changes that keep the aircraft at or above the MRVA. When a controller vectors you into a sector, they check the MRVA for that sector before issuing the heading. If an aircraft descends below MRVA, an immediate instruction to climb is issued.
exam_tip: Radar provides terrain clearance via MRVAs — Minimum Radar Vectoring Altitudes that the controller must not vector you below. Mode C/S transponder is how the controller verifies your altitude.
key_facts: Radar terrain clearance via MRVA (Minimum Radar Vectoring Altitude)
Controller must not vector below MRVA for each sector
Aircraft altitude monitored via Mode C/S transponder
Immediate climb instruction if aircraft descends below MRVA
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: Describe the use of DME descent steps for maintaining terrain clearance during departure climb or descent for an approach. AIP GEN & ENR
concept: DME descent steps are shown on enroute charts and VORSEC charts, and on some instrument approach charts. They allow a pilot to descend progressively as they track inbound on a VOR/DME radial, with each step at a specified DME distance guaranteeing obstacle clearance within 2.5 NM either side of the track. The technique is: track the published radial inbound, note the DME reading, and descend to the next step altitude only when you reach or pass the specified DME. This provides terrain protection for the descent from the enroute structure down to approach commencement altitude, without requiring radar or continuous ATC involvement.
exam_tip: DME descent steps are distance-in, altitude-down: at X DME, descend to Y ft. The terrain clearance guarantee covers 2.5 NM either side of the track. Must be at the specified DME before descending.
key_facts: DME descent steps on enroute/VORSEC/approach charts
Pilot descends to next step when reaching specified DME distance
Each step guarantees obstacle clearance within 2.5 NM either side of track
Reference: AIP GEN and ENR
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP GEN, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for deviation off track for weather avoidance. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.01 requires that any deviation off the cleared track for weather avoidance in controlled airspace must be done with an ATC clearance. In controlled airspace you can't just turn to avoid weather — you request an amendment to your clearance first (e.g. 'request 20 degrees left for weather'). In uncontrolled airspace (Class G) you're free to deviate without ATC, but you must still comply with CAR Part 91 minimum altitudes and separation requirements, and you must report the deviation to ATS as soon as practicable. For oceanic operations, specific weather deviation procedures apply including maintaining SELCAL or HF watch and transmitting on TIBA frequencies during the deviation.
exam_tip: Controlled airspace: need ATC clearance to deviate for weather. Uncontrolled: you can deviate, but report it. Oceanic: specific ICAO procedures for weather deviation apply.
key_facts: Weather deviation in controlled airspace requires ATC clearance
Request amended clearance before deviating (e.g. 'request left for weather')
In Class G, may deviate and report to ATS as soon as practicable
Oceanic deviation procedures per ICAO/AIP ENR
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.01

syllabus_desc: Describe the radar services available to IFR flights. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.06 describes the radar and surveillance services available to IFR flights. The systems in use in NZ include: Primary Surveillance Radar (PSR), which detects aircraft as skin-paint returns; Secondary Surveillance Radar (SSR/transponder-based, Modes A, C, S), which gives callsign and altitude; ADS-B (Automatic Dependent Surveillance - Broadcast), where the aircraft broadcasts its own GNSS position via extended squitter; and Multilateration (MLAT), which calculates position from transponder signal timing. Services provided to IFR flights using these systems include radar identification, traffic information, radar control, radar approach (PAR and ASR), and surveillance-based terrain clearance.
exam_tip: Four systems: PSR, SSR, ADS-B, MLAT. Know what each provides: PSR = primary echo (no callsign/alt); SSR = callsign and altitude; ADS-B = GNSS position from aircraft; MLAT = time-difference positioning.
key_facts: NZ surveillance services in AIP ENR 1.06: PSR, SSR, ADS-B, MLAT
PSR: primary skin-paint radar return, no identification
SSR (Modes A/C/S): transponder-based, callsign and altitude
ADS-B: aircraft-broadcast GNSS position; MLAT: time-difference calculation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.06

syllabus_desc: Describe the responsibility of the radar controller to keep an aircraft within controlled airspace. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.06 section 3.7.5 states that when an IFR aircraft is being vectored, the radar controller is responsible for ensuring the aircraft remains within controlled airspace, except in an emergency. This means if the controller issues a vector that would take you to or near the controlled airspace boundary, they must keep track of that and either issue a new vector before you reach the boundary, or advise you that you're leaving controlled airspace. A pilot being vectored should not fly outside of controlled airspace unless the controller has specifically stated they are leaving controlled airspace or an emergency dictates it.
exam_tip: Radar controller is responsible for keeping you WITHIN controlled airspace during vectoring. You don't need to monitor the boundary independently when being vectored — that's the controller's job.
key_facts: Controller responsible for keeping vectored aircraft within controlled airspace
Reference: AIP ENR 1.06 section 3.7.5
Exception: emergency requiring departure from controlled airspace
Pilot should not leave controlled airspace unless controller directs or emergency
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.06

syllabus_desc: State the accuracy limits required when under radar speed control. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.06 section 3.9.2 specifies that when ATC issues a radar speed control instruction, pilots are expected to maintain that speed within ±10 kt of the assigned speed. So if ATC says 'maintain 180 kt', acceptable range is 170-190 kt. This tolerance exists because there's no expectation of pinpoint airspeed accuracy, but there is an expectation of reasonably close compliance. If you can't maintain a speed within tolerance (e.g. performance or turbulence), you must advise ATC so they can adjust the sequence.
exam_tip: Radar speed control accuracy: ±10 kt. This is the figure from AIP ENR 1.06. If you can't maintain within tolerance, tell ATC.
key_facts: Radar speed control accuracy ±10 kt per AIP ENR 1.06
Maintain assigned speed within 10 kt of ATC instruction
Cannot maintain? Advise ATC immediately
Speed control used for sequencing and separation on approach
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.06

syllabus_desc: State the distance from touchdown that radar speed control can be maintained on an instrument and a visual approach. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.06 section 3.9.10 specifies the distance limits for radar speed control on approaches. On an instrument approach: speed control instructions cannot be applied after you have passed a point 5 NM from touchdown. On a visual approach: speed control cannot be applied after you have passed a point 4 NM from touchdown. Inside these distances, you're expected to manage your own speed appropriate to the approach configuration. The limit exists because close to touchdown, speed management is critical for safety and the pilot needs full authority over their own aircraft state.
exam_tip: Instrument approach: speed control stops at 5 NM from touchdown. Visual approach: stops at 4 NM from touchdown. Instrument = 5, Visual = 4. Don't mix them up.
key_facts: Radar speed control limits per AIP ENR 1.06
Instrument approach: speed control cannot be applied inside 5 NM from touchdown
Visual approach: speed control cannot be applied inside 4 NM from touchdown
Inside these distances, pilot manages own speed
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.06

syllabus_desc: State the meteorological and other conditions which allow a radar controller to vector an aircraft for a visual approach. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.06 section 3.16.9 sets out when a radar controller may vector an aircraft for a visual approach. Conditions: by day, the aerodrome visibility must be at least 16 km, and the reported ceiling must be at least 1000 ft above the applicable minimum radar vectoring altitude. At night, visual approach vectoring is permitted only at specific aerodromes (e.g. Christchurch on runways 11/29) with visibility at least 16 km and no cloud below 5000 ft. The pilot must be able to maintain visual reference to terrain throughout, and must specifically request a visual approach. These restrictions ensure that when ATC vectors you off the standard procedure, there's genuinely enough visibility to make the approach safely.
exam_tip: Visual approach vectoring: day = vis ≥16 km AND ceiling ≥1000 ft above MRVA. Night = specific aerodromes only, vis ≥16 km, no cloud below 5000 ft. The 16 km figure is key.
key_facts: Visual approach vectoring conditions in AIP ENR 1.06
Day: aerodrome visibility ≥16 km AND ceiling ≥1000 ft above minimum radar vectoring altitude
Night: specific approved aerodromes only, vis ≥16 km, no cloud below 5000 ft
Pilot must request visual approach and maintain visual reference to terrain
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.06

syllabus_desc: State the criteria for a radar controller to consider an unknown aircraft to be on a conflicting path with another aircraft. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.06 section 3.17 defines when a radar controller considers an unknown aircraft to be on a conflicting path with a known IFR flight. An unknown aircraft is one the controller cannot associate with any controlled flight in the system. That unknown aircraft is considered to be on a conflicting path when the projected tracks would reduce separation to less than 3 NM. When this occurs, the controller warns the identified IFR pilot and, if considered necessary, suggests a course of action. This is a safety net function — it protects against unidentified targets (e.g. transponder failure, non-participating aircraft) that could create conflicts in controlled airspace.
exam_tip: Conflicting path threshold: projected separation reducing to less than 3 NM. The controller warns the IFR pilot and may suggest avoiding action. The unknown aircraft is one that can't be associated with any known controlled flight.
key_facts: Unknown aircraft on conflicting path criteria in AIP ENR 1.06
Unknown aircraft: cannot be associated with any known controlled flight
Conflicting path: projected tracks would reduce separation to less than 3 NM
Controller warns IFR pilot and may suggest avoiding action
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.06

syllabus_desc: Describe the requirements which a Part 91 operator must meet to conduct a PBN operation. AC91-21, Operational Approvals - Performance-Based Navigation
concept: A Part 91 operator must meet the following requirements to conduct a PBN operation: (a) aircraft eligibility (AFM/AFMS compliance with the navigation specification); (b) continued airworthiness procedures, including MEL provisions and database management; (c) documented operator procedures (SOPs or operations manual per rule 91.261); (d) pilot training and qualification per the applicable navigation specification; and (e) obtain a Letter of Operational Approval (LOA) from the CAA Director.
exam_tip: Key rule: 91.261. Part 91 gets a Letter of Operational Approval (LOA); Part 119 gets Ops Specs. The LOA is aircraft-specific and lists further compliance requirements for pilots.
key_facts: Rule basis: 91.261 and 91.263 (Notices)
Part 91 operators receive a Letter of Operational Approval (LOA) from the Director
LOA is aircraft-specific — stipulates pilot compliance requirements
Must demonstrate: aircraft eligibility, continued airworthiness, operator procedures, pilot training
source: AC91-21 Rev 1, s 6 and s 9

syllabus_desc: Describe the PBN Operational Approval Process. AC91-21
concept: The PBN Operational Approval Process has five phases: (1) Pre-application — CAA meets with the operator to explain requirements; (2) Application — operator submits Form CAA 24091/07 and supporting documentation (aircraft eligibility, continued airworthiness, procedures, training); (3) Assessment — CAA assesses documentation and determines approval method; (4) Demonstration — operator provides crew training and conducts validation flights if required; (5) Approval — CAA issues LOA (Part 91) or amends Ops Specs (Part 119).
exam_tip: Five phases: Pre-application → Application → Assessment → Demonstration → Approval. Part 91 = LOA; Part 119 = Ops Specs. Application form is CAA 24091/07.
key_facts: Phase 1: Pre-application meeting (recommended for Part 119 and first-time applicants)
Phase 2: Application — Form CAA 24091/07 + supporting docs
Phase 3: Assessment — CAA reviews documentation
Phase 4: Demonstration — training and validation flights if required
Phase 5: Approval — LOA (Part 91) or Ops Specs (Part 119)
source: AC91-21 Rev 1, s 6

syllabus_desc: Identify who is responsible for ensuring that electronic navigation data and equipment software is valid and updated for the equipment installation the PBN approval is based on. AC91-21
concept: The operator is responsible for ensuring that electronic navigation data (database) and equipment software is valid and updated for the equipment installation the PBN approval is based on. The operator must have procedures to control the navigation database and software configuration, ensuring they remain valid and compatible with the PBN approval.
exam_tip: The OPERATOR is responsible for database and software currency — not the pilot or the regulator. Database must come from a supplier complying with RTCA DO-200A/EUROCAE ED-76. Discrepancies that invalidate procedures must be reported.
key_facts: The OPERATOR is responsible for navigation database and software validity
Database must be from a supplier complying with RTCA DO-200A / EUROCAE ED-76
Operator must control software configuration and any design changes
Discrepancies invalidating a procedure: report to supplier and prohibit affected procedure
source: AC91-21 Rev 1, s 8 and s 9

syllabus_desc: State the requirements which must be met before a pilot of an aircraft operating within the New Zealand flight information region, under IFR, using GPS equipment as a primary means navigation system, is permitted random flight routing. CAR Part 91
concept: Before a pilot may conduct random flight routing (i.e. GPS IFR en route without published airways) in the NZ FIR under IFR using GPS as the primary means of navigation, the operator must hold a GNSS-Based Navigation Approval (GBNA) and the pilot must hold a logbook endorsement for the relevant PBN operation and equipment type, and have been trained per Appendix III of AC 61-17.
exam_tip: Random routing = off-airway IFR. Requires: (1) operator GBNA (LOA under rule 91.261); (2) pilot logbook endorsement for the specific navigation specification and aircraft equipment type. Training per AC 61-17 Appendix III.
key_facts: Operator must hold GNSS-Based Navigation Approval (GBNA / LOA under rule 91.261)
Pilot must hold a logbook endorsement for the navigation specification and equipment type
Training must comply with AC 61-17, Appendix III (PBN knowledge requirements)
Part 119 pilots: competency achieved through operator training programme
source: AC91-21 Rev 1, s 6 and s 10; CAR Part 91, rule 91.261

syllabus_desc: Describe the contingency procedures required by aircraft within the New Zealand flight information region, in the event of loss of Primary Means of Navigation. CAR Part 91 and AC91-21, Appendix I
concept: Contingency procedures are required under rule 91.261(2)(iii) — the PIC must plan before departure for the loss of Primary Means of Navigation (PMoN). The plan must address: phase of flight, route (terrain, MSA, AMoN range), aircraft equipment, weather, ATC availability, and fuel. The aircraft must carry sufficient fuel to implement the contingency plan at any point in the flight. The AMoN must allow the aircraft to navigate and land safely without GNSS.
exam_tip: PMoN loss triggers: GNSS RAIM warning, LOI alert, DR mode >1 min, equipment failure, GNSS signal outage. AMoN acceptable to Director = navigation and landing without GNSS. Fuel must cover loss of PMoN at any point. Part 121/125/135: must be documented in exposition.
key_facts: Rule basis: rule 91.261(2)(iii) — PIC must plan for PMoN loss before departure
AMoN must allow navigation and landing without GNSS
Aircraft must carry fuel to implement contingency at any point in the flight
PMoN loss triggers: RAIM warning, LOI, equipment failure, DR mode >1 minute
Part 121/125/135 operators: contingency procedures must be in CAA-approved exposition
source: AC91-21 Rev 1, s 9 and Appendix I; CAR Part 91, rule 91.261(2)(iii)

syllabus_desc: State the ICAO PBN specifications implemented in the NZ FIR, in each of the following phases of flight: (a) En route Continental/Domestic; (b) Terminal; (c) Initial, Intermediate and Missed Approach; and (d) Final Approach. AC91-21, Table 1
concept: ICAO PBN specifications implemented in the NZ FIR (from AC91-21 Table 1): (a) En route Continental/Domestic: RNAV 2, RNP 2; (b) Terminal (SID/STAR): RNAV 1, RNP 1; (c) Initial, Intermediate, and Missed Approach: RNAV 1, RNP 1, RNP APCH (1 NM), RNP AR APCH (variable); (d) Final Approach: RNP APCH (0.3 NM), RNP AR APCH (0.3–0.1 NM). Note: LP and LPV are NOT implemented in NZ as there is no SBAS coverage.
exam_tip: Key NZ facts: No SBAS → no LPV or LP in NZ FIR. RNAV 2 (en route), RNAV 1/RNP 1 (terminal), RNP APCH (approaches). RNP AR APCH requires specific operator authorisation. RNAV specs likely replaced by RNP specs by 2023.
key_facts: En route Continental: RNAV 2, RNP 2
Terminal (SID/STAR): RNAV 1, RNP 1
Initial/Intermediate/Missed: RNAV 1, RNP 1, RNP APCH (1 NM), RNP AR APCH
Final approach: RNP APCH (0.3 NM), RNP AR APCH (0.3–0.1 NM)
LP and LPV NOT implemented in NZ (no SBAS coverage)
source: AC91-21 Rev 1, Table 1 (s 5)

syllabus_desc: State the surveillance and communications requirements expected to apply in RNAV 2 in the enroute phase in the NZFIR. AC91-21, Table 2
concept: Surveillance and communications requirements for RNAV 2 en route in the NZ FIR: Surveillance — RNAV 2 is expected to be conducted in a surveillance environment; operation outside surveillance or below Minimum Vectoring Altitude (MVA) requires a state safety case. RNP 2 can be conducted outside a surveillance environment (it has onboard monitoring and alerting). Communications — both RNAV 2 and RNP 2 (continental) are conducted in direct controller–pilot VHF communication.
exam_tip: RNAV 2 = surveillance required (radar environment). RNP 2 = can operate outside surveillance (has onboard monitoring). Both use direct VHF comms. The key distinction: RNP specs can go outside radar coverage because they have onboard performance monitoring.
key_facts: RNAV 2: must be in a surveillance (radar) environment
RNP 2: can operate outside surveillance — has onboard performance monitoring and alerting
Both RNAV 2 and RNP 2 (continental): direct controller–pilot VHF communication
RNP 2 remote/oceanic: may require data link (ADS-C) and HF comms
source: AC91-21 Rev 1, Table 2 (s 5)

syllabus_desc: State the navigation infrastructure required to support RNAV/RNP 2 in the enroute phase in the NZFIR. AC91-21, Table 2
concept: Navigation infrastructure supporting RNAV 2 and RNP 2 en route in the NZ FIR: based on GNSS, specifically GPS. The RNAV 2 specification can theoretically be supported by DME/DME or DME/DME/Inertial, but this has limited application in NZ — refer to the AIP for details. RNP 2 is also GNSS (GPS) based.
exam_tip: Primary nav infrastructure = GNSS (GPS). DME/DME is theoretically possible for RNAV 2 but not implemented in NZ. Know that NZ relies on GPS — no DME/DME infrastructure for en-route PBN.
key_facts: RNAV 2 and RNP 2 en route: based on GNSS (specifically GPS)
DME/DME or DME/DME/Inertial theoretically supports RNAV 2 but has limited NZ application
No DME/DME en-route navigation infrastructure implemented for PBN in NZ
Refer to AIP for specific limitations on DME-based navigation
source: AC91-21 Rev 1, Table 2 (s 5)

syllabus_desc: State the CNS equipment requirements for operations in RNAV 2 enroute continental/domestic airspace in the NZFIR. AC91-21, Table 5
concept: CNS equipment requirements for RNAV 2 en route continental/domestic in the NZ FIR: Navigation — 1x GNSS (Part 91); alternate non-GNSS means of navigation external to the GNSS LRU when only 1 GNSS fitted; 2x GNSS for Part 119. Communications — 1x VHF (Part 91); 2x VHF (Part 119). Surveillance — 1x transponder (Part 91); 1+ transponder recommended for Part 119 for redundancy.
exam_tip: Part 91 RNAV 2: 1x GNSS + alternate non-GNSS nav + 1x VHF + 1x transponder. Part 119: 2x GNSS + alternate non-GNSS nav + 2x VHF + transponder. Note: if GNSS and VHF are in the same LRU, that unit cannot count as the alternate means.
key_facts: Part 91: 1x GNSS; alternate non-GNSS nav external to GNSS LRU; 1x VHF; 1x transponder
Part 119: 2x GNSS; alternate non-GNSS nav; 2x VHF; transponder
GNSS + VHF combined LRU cannot serve as both — alternate must be external to GNSS LRU
RNAV 2 requires transponder (surveillance environment)
source: AC91-21 Rev 1, Tables 5 and 6 (s 7 and 8)

syllabus_desc: State the Total System Error (TSE) permitted in the RNAV/RNP 2 PBN specification in the enroute phase in the NZFIR. AC91-21, Table 2
concept: Total System Error (TSE) for RNAV 2 and RNP 2 en route in the NZ FIR: lateral and along-track TSE must be within ±2 NM for at least 95% of total flight time. Flight Technical Error (FTE) is not to exceed half the lateral TSE — for RNAV 2 and RNP 2 the lateral FTE alert threshold is 1 NM.
exam_tip: RNAV 2 / RNP 2 TSE = ±2 NM (95% of time). FTE ≤ half of TSE = ≤1 NM. The TSE number matches the specification number: RNAV 2 = ±2 NM, RNAV 1 = ±1 NM, RNP APCH = ±0.3 NM on final. Easy pattern to remember.
key_facts: TSE: within ±2 NM for ≥95% of flight time
FTE (lateral cross-track): must not exceed half the lateral TSE
Lateral FTE alert threshold for RNAV 2 / RNP 2: 1 NM
TSE = Navigation System Error (NSE) + Flight Technical Error (FTE) + Path Definition Error (PDE)
source: AC91-21 Rev 1, Table 2 (s 5)

syllabus_desc: State the surveillance and communications requirements expected to apply in RNAV/RNP 1 in the terminal and approach phases in the NZFIR. AC91-21, Table 3
concept: Surveillance and communications requirements for RNAV 1 and RNP 1 terminal and approach phases in the NZ FIR: Surveillance — RNAV 1 is expected to be conducted in a surveillance environment; operation outside surveillance or below MVA requires a state safety case. RNP 1 can be conducted outside a surveillance environment (has onboard monitoring and alerting). Communications — both RNAV 1 and RNP 1 use direct controller–pilot VHF communication.
exam_tip: Same pattern as en route: RNAV 1 = surveillance (radar) required; RNP 1 = can operate outside radar. Both use VHF direct comms. NZ SIDs/STARs extend beyond 30 NM — navigation system must set lateral indication scaling and GNSS integrity alert to 1 NM beyond 30 NM.
key_facts: RNAV 1: must be in surveillance environment (radar-based ATC)
RNP 1: can be outside surveillance — has onboard monitoring and alerting
Both RNAV 1 and RNP 1: direct controller–pilot VHF communication
NZ SIDs/STARs exceed 30 NM — system must set 1 NM scaling beyond 30 NM ARP
source: AC91-21 Rev 1, Table 3 (s 5)

syllabus_desc: State the navigation infrastructure required to support RNAV/RNP 1 in the terminal and approach phases in the NZFIR. AC91-21, Table 3
concept: Navigation infrastructure supporting RNAV 1 and RNP 1 terminal and approach phases in the NZ FIR: based on GNSS, specifically GPS. The RNAV 1 specification can theoretically be supported by DME/DME or DME/DME/Inertial, but this has NOT been implemented in New Zealand.
exam_tip: RNAV 1/RNP 1 terminal = GNSS (GPS). DME/DME is in the ICAO spec for RNAV 1 but not used in NZ. Know that all NZ terminal PBN operations rely on GPS.
key_facts: RNAV 1 and RNP 1: based on GNSS (GPS)
DME/DME or DME/DME/Inertial theoretically supports RNAV 1 but NOT implemented in NZ
All NZ terminal PBN relies on GNSS (GPS) as the navigation sensor
Database currency is critical for terminal procedures
source: AC91-21 Rev 1, Table 3 (s 5)

syllabus_desc: State the CNS equipment requirements for operations in RNAV/RNP 1 in the terminal and approach phases in the NZFIR. AC91-21, Tables 3 and 5
concept: CNS equipment requirements for RNAV 1 and RNP 1 terminal and approach phases in the NZ FIR: Navigation — 1x GNSS (Part 91); alternate non-GNSS means external to GNSS LRU; 2x GNSS (Part 119). Communications — 1x VHF (Part 91); 2x VHF (Part 119). Surveillance — RNAV 1 requires transponder (surveillance environment); RNP 1 does not have a specific transponder requirement (can operate outside surveillance).
exam_tip: RNAV 1 Part 91: 1x GNSS + alternate non-GNSS nav + 1x VHF + transponder. RNP 1 Part 91: 1x GNSS + alternate + 1x VHF (no transponder requirement as it can operate outside surveillance). Part 119: 2x GNSS + alternate + 2x VHF for both.
key_facts: RNAV 1: 1x GNSS + alternate non-GNSS + 1x VHF + transponder (Part 91)
RNP 1: 1x GNSS + alternate non-GNSS + 1x VHF (no transponder requirement)
Part 119 for both: 2x GNSS + alternate non-GNSS + 2x VHF
GNSS + VHF in same LRU cannot count as alternate — must be external
source: AC91-21 Rev 1, Tables 3, 5, and 6

syllabus_desc: State the Total System Error (TSE) permitted in the RNAV/RNP 1 PBN specification in the terminal and approach phases in the NZFIR. AC91-21, Table 3
concept: Total System Error (TSE) for RNAV 1 and RNP 1 terminal and approach phases in the NZ FIR: lateral and along-track TSE must be within ±1 NM for at least 95% of total flight time. Flight Technical Error (FTE) is not to exceed half the lateral TSE — for RNAV 1 and RNP 1 the lateral FTE alert threshold is 0.5 NM.
exam_tip: RNAV 1 / RNP 1 TSE = ±1 NM (95% of time). FTE ≤ 0.5 NM. The spec number tells you the TSE: RNAV 2 = ±2 NM, RNAV 1 = ±1 NM. This is the key examiner trick — TSE matches the spec number.
key_facts: TSE: within ±1 NM for ≥95% of flight time
FTE (lateral cross-track): must not exceed half the lateral TSE
Lateral FTE alert threshold for RNAV 1 / RNP 1: 0.5 NM
Spec number = TSE value: RNAV 1 → ±1 NM, RNAV 2 → ±2 NM
source: AC91-21 Rev 1, Table 3 (s 5)

syllabus_desc: State the limitation, during approach operations, on aircraft with advisory vertical navigation systems only. AC91-21
concept: Aircraft with advisory vertical navigation systems only may NOT conduct LNAV/VNAV approaches to LNAV/VNAV minima. Advisory VNAV may only be used to conduct approaches to LNAV minima. The pilot remains responsible for adherence to minimum procedure altitudes.
exam_tip: Advisory VNAV = not a certified Baro-VNAV system. If you don't have a certified Baro-VNAV, you can use LNAV minima only — not LNAV/VNAV. The vertical needle in advisory mode is for guidance only, not for precision descent to LNAV/VNAV minima.
key_facts: Advisory vertical navigation ≠ certified Baro-VNAV
Advisory VNAV: approach to LNAV minima ONLY — not LNAV/VNAV
Certified Baro-VNAV required to use LNAV/VNAV minima
Pilot remains responsible for adherence to minimum procedure altitudes in all cases
source: AC91-21 Rev 1, s 5 (Approach Operations note)

syllabus_desc: Describe the authorisation requirements applicable to RNP AR APCH procedures. AC91-21
concept: RNP AR APCH (Authorisation Required) procedures require specific operator and aircraft authorisation granted by the CAA. Procedures are published as 'RNAV (RNP) RWY xx' charts and include the statement 'for operators with CAANZ RNP-AR approvals only' or 'for approved operators only'. Authorisation is specific to the operator, aircraft type, and may include specific operating procedures, special crew training, and approach minima.
exam_tip: RNP AR ≠ just having the equipment. You need an explicit CAA authorisation for each operator and aircraft combination. The chart will say 'CAANZ RNP-AR approval only'. Pre-application meeting required. Used at constrained aerodromes (e.g. Queenstown).
key_facts: Requires specific CAA authorisation — not just equipment eligibility
Procedure charts: 'RNAV (RNP) RWY xx' with statement 'CAANZ RNP-AR approvals only'
Authorisation is operator- and aircraft-specific
May include specific operating procedures, special crew training, and approach minima
Complex assessment — pre-application meeting required
source: AC91-21 Rev 1, s 5 and s 6

syllabus_desc: State the surveillance and communications requirements expected to apply in RNP APCH in the approach phase in the NZFIR. AC91-21, Tables 4 and 5
concept: Surveillance and communications requirements for RNP APCH in the NZ FIR: Surveillance — RNP APCH does NOT have specific surveillance requirements; adequate obstacle clearance is achieved through aircraft performance and operating procedures. Communications — no specific communications requirements; determined by complexity and density of the operating environment. VHF is required as part of the CNS equipment but is not a navigation specification requirement per se.
exam_tip: Key point: RNP APCH has NO specific radar surveillance requirement — it's designed for use at aerodromes without radar (that's the whole point of a standalone instrument approach). Comms requirements are operationally determined, not prescribed by the specification.
key_facts: Surveillance: NO specific requirement — obstacle clearance via aircraft performance and procedures
This allows RNP APCH at non-radar aerodromes (the key advantage over RNAV approaches)
Communications: no specific requirement — determined by operational environment
VHF communication required as standard IFR equipment, not as a nav specification requirement
source: AC91-21 Rev 1, Table 4 (s 5)

syllabus_desc: State the navigation infrastructure required to support RNP APCH in the approach and missed approach phases in the NZFIR. AC91-21 Tables 4 and 5
concept: Navigation infrastructure supporting RNP APCH approach and missed approach in the NZ FIR: LNAV approaches — GNSS (GPS) with RAIM (Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring). LNAV/VNAV approaches — aircraft certified Baro-VNAV system in addition to GNSS for lateral guidance. Missed approach — can be based on GNSS (GPS) OR a conventional navigation aid. Note: LP and LPV (which require SBAS) are NOT implemented in NZ — no SBAS coverage in NZFIR.
exam_tip: LNAV = GPS + RAIM. LNAV/VNAV = GPS + certified Baro-VNAV. No SBAS in NZ → no LP or LPV. Missed approach can revert to a conventional navaid (NDB, VOR). This is important for contingency planning — if GPS fails on missed approach, a VOR/NDB-based missed approach may be needed.
key_facts: LNAV: GNSS (GPS) with RAIM for integrity monitoring
LNAV/VNAV: GNSS (GPS) + certified Baro-VNAV system
Missed approach: GNSS (GPS) OR conventional navaid (VOR/NDB)
LP and LPV: NOT implemented in NZ (no SBAS coverage in NZFIR)
source: AC91-21 Rev 1, Tables 4 and 5 (s 5)

syllabus_desc: State the CNS equipment requirements for operations in RNP APCH in the approach and missed approach phases in the NZFIR. AC91-21, Tables 4 and 5
concept: CNS equipment requirements for RNP APCH in the NZ FIR: Navigation — 1x GNSS with RAIM (Part 91); alternate non-GNSS means of navigation external to the GNSS LRU when only 1 GNSS fitted; 2x GNSS (Part 119). For LNAV/VNAV: certified Baro-VNAV in addition to GNSS. Communications — 1x VHF (Part 91); 2x VHF (Part 119). Surveillance — no specific transponder requirement from the nav specification.
exam_tip: RNP APCH Part 91: 1x GNSS (RAIM) + alternate non-GNSS nav + 1x VHF. No transponder required by the spec (no radar surveillance requirement). LNAV/VNAV adds certified Baro-VNAV. RAIM must be predicted available for the entire approach.
key_facts: Navigation: 1x GNSS with RAIM (Part 91); 2x GNSS (Part 119); alternate non-GNSS nav
LNAV/VNAV addition: certified Baro-VNAV system
Communications: 1x VHF (Part 91); 2x VHF (Part 119)
Surveillance: no specific transponder requirement from the nav specification
RAIM must be predicted available for approach ETA
source: AC91-21 Rev 1, Tables 4, 5, and 6

syllabus_desc: State the Total System Error (TSE) permitted in the RNP APCH PBN specification in the approach and missed approach phases in the NZFIR. AC91-21, Table 4
concept: Total System Error (TSE) for RNP APCH in the NZ FIR: Initial, intermediate, and missed approach segments — lateral and along-track TSE within ±1 NM for ≥95% of flight time (lateral FTE alert: 0.5 NM). Final approach segment (to LNAV or LNAV/VNAV minima) — lateral and along-track TSE within ±0.3 NM for ≥95% of flight time (lateral FTE alert: 0.25 NM). For LNAV/VNAV on Baro-VNAV: vertical deviation must not exceed ±75 ft (±22 m) from the defined vertical path in the final approach segment.
exam_tip: RNP APCH TSE: ±1 NM (initial/intermediate/missed) → ±0.3 NM (final). The 0.3 value is what makes RNP APCH precise enough for instrument approaches. Lateral FTE halves too: 0.5 NM → 0.25 NM. Baro-VNAV adds: ±75 ft vertical deviation limit on final.
key_facts: Initial/intermediate/missed approach: TSE ±1 NM (95%), FTE alert 0.5 NM
Final approach segment (LNAV/LNAV/VNAV): TSE ±0.3 NM (95%), FTE alert 0.25 NM
Baro-VNAV on final: vertical deviation must not exceed ±75 ft (±22 m) from defined path
The 0.3 NM containment on final enables lower approach minima than RNAV 1
source: AC91-21 Rev 1, Table 4 (s 5)

syllabus_desc: Explain the altimeter setting requirements for flight under IFR. CAR Part 91 & AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.07 specifies the altimeter setting requirements for IFR flight in NZ. Below the transition altitude (13,000 ft AMSL): set and use QNH (the local sea-level pressure corrected to MSL) to maintain altitude in feet. Above the transition level (FL150): set and use standard pressure (1013.2 hPa) to maintain flight level. In the transition layer (between 13,000 ft and FL150): use the altimeter setting as directed by ATC, and don't cruise — you're either climbing through to FL150 or descending to below 13,000 ft. The transition altitude and level are fixed in NZ, unlike some countries where they vary with QNH.
exam_tip: NZ transition altitude = 13,000 ft (set QNH below). NZ transition level = FL150 (set 1013.2 hPa above). Transition layer = 13,000 ft to FL150. These are fixed NZ figures — memorise them.
key_facts: Below 13,000 ft: use QNH (local sea-level pressure)
Above FL150: use standard pressure (1013.2 hPa / flight levels)
NZ transition altitude = 13,000 ft AMSL (fixed)
NZ transition level = FL150 (fixed)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.07

syllabus_desc: State the procedure to use to obtain an altimeter setting when QNH is not available prior to take-off and the requirement to obtain a QNH once in flight. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.07 section 2.2.10 covers what to do when QNH isn't available before take-off. Set the aerodrome elevation on the altimeter sub-scale before departing. Once airborne, you must obtain the appropriate QNH from an ATS unit as soon as possible — and you must have obtained it before entering IMC. You can't depart and fly into cloud with your altimeter set to aerodrome elevation — that's only valid as a stop-gap until you get a proper QNH. When you contact the area control centre or approach after departure, getting the QNH should be one of your first requests.
exam_tip: No QNH before departure? Set aerodrome elevation and go. But you MUST obtain QNH from ATS before entering IMC. This is a firm requirement — not optional.
key_facts: If QNH unavailable before take-off: set aerodrome elevation on altimeter
Must obtain QNH from ATS as soon as possible after departure
Must obtain QNH before entering IMC
Reference: AIP ENR 1.07 section 2.2.10
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.07

syllabus_desc: Describe QNH zones and state when zone QNH should be used. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.07 section 1.3.2 defines NZ Area QNH Zones. There are 12 defined zones within the NZ FIR, each covering a geographic region. Within a zone, a single area QNH value applies to all aircraft operating in Class G airspace. Zone QNH is used in Class G airspace (uncontrolled) — it replaces individual aerodrome QNH for terrain clearance and separation calculations across the zone. When the zone QNH is 980 hPa or less, the minimum usable flight level for that zone rises to FL160 (rather than the normal FL150 transition level) to account for the lower pressure reducing indicated altitudes.
exam_tip: 12 QNH zones in NZ FIR. Zone QNH used in Class G airspace. When zone QNH is ≤980 hPa, minimum usable FL rises to FL160. This pressure-based FL adjustment is a commonly tested detail.
key_facts: 12 Area QNH Zones in NZ FIR per AIP ENR 1.07
Zone QNH applies in Class G airspace across the zone
When zone QNH ≤980 hPa: minimum usable flight level rises to FL160
Reference: AIP ENR 1.07 section 1.3.2
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.07

syllabus_desc: Describe the transition altitude, layer and level. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.07 section 2.2 and AIP GEN define the three terms. Transition Altitude (TA): the altitude below which vertical position is expressed in altitude (QNH) — in NZ this is 13,000 ft AMSL. Transition Level (TL): the lowest flight level available for use above the transition altitude — in NZ this is FL150. Transition Layer: the airspace between the transition altitude and the transition level — in NZ this is the band from 13,000 ft to FL150. The transition layer exists as a buffer to prevent two aircraft both at 13,000 ft (one using QNH, one using 1013.2) from conflicting. You don't cruise in the layer — you're either climbing to FL150 or descending to below 13,000 ft.
exam_tip: Three definitions: TA (altitude where you switch from FL to QNH on descent) = 13,000 ft; TL (lowest available FL above TA) = FL150; Transition Layer (between them) = don't cruise here.
key_facts: Transition Altitude = 13,000 ft AMSL (NZ) — below this, use QNH
Transition Level = FL150 (NZ) — lowest FL above TA
Transition Layer = 13,000 ft to FL150 — climbing or descending only, no cruising
Reference: AIP ENR 1.07
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.07

syllabus_desc: State the altitude/flight level requirements when cruising IFR within the New Zealand FIR. CAR Part 91 & AIP ENR
concept: CAR rule 91.425 and AIP ENR 1.01 set the IFR cruising level table for NZ. At or below 13,000 ft AMSL: magnetic track 270° to 089° = odd thousands (3000, 5000, 7000, 9000, 11000, 13000 ft); magnetic track 090° to 269° = even thousands (2000, 4000, 6000, 8000, 10000, 12000 ft). At FL150 and above: magnetic track 270° to 089° = odd flight levels (FL150, FL170, FL190...); track 090° to 269° = even flight levels (FL160, FL180, FL200...). Above FL410 a different table applies. ATC assignment overrides the table.
exam_tip: Eastbound (090-269 magnetic) = even. Westbound (270-089 magnetic) = odd. The ranges look backwards to some students — remember 090-269 covers east and south, 270-089 covers west and north.
key_facts: IFR cruising levels in CAR rule 91.425 and AIP ENR
Magnetic track 090°-269° (eastbound/southbound): even thousands / even FLs
Magnetic track 270°-089° (westbound/northbound): odd thousands / odd FLs
ATC assignment overrides the semi-circular table
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91 rule 91.425, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: Determine from charts and publications the minimum flight altitude (MFA) for a route sector
concept: Minimum Flight Altitudes (MFAs) are published on NZ enroute charts for each route segment. To determine the MFA for a sector: locate the segment on the enroute chart, read the published MFA (usually shown as a boxed figure). The MFA is the lowest IFR altitude for that segment that guarantees obstacle clearance (1000 ft non-mountainous, 2000 ft mountainous) over the terrain within the protected area of the route. You must be at or above the MFA when operating along that segment. Multiple segments may apply across a cross-country route and you plan the highest applicable MFA for each.
exam_tip: MFA is read directly from the enroute chart for each segment. This is a chart-reading skill. Exam questions often give you a chart excerpt and ask for the MFA or the minimum cruise altitude for a route.
key_facts: MFAs published on NZ enroute charts for each segment
Guarantees ≥1000 ft clearance non-mountainous, ≥2000 ft mountainous
Read the boxed figure on the enroute chart for the applicable segment
Must be at or above MFA when operating the segment under IFR
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR charts

syllabus_desc: Describe situations where ATC may assign cruising altitudes not in accordance with the IFR table of cruising altitudes. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR specifies when ATC may assign IFR cruising altitudes outside the standard semi-circular table. ATC can assign a non-standard level when required for traffic management — for example, to separate traffic by giving them levels that don't follow the directional rule. This is common in terminal areas where the volume of traffic makes the semi-circular table unworkable. ATC can also issue block clearances (e.g. 'maintain between FL150 and FL170') or assign specific levels for short portions of a route. When ATC assigns a level, that assignment satisfies the requirement regardless of the semi-circular rules.
exam_tip: ATC can override the semi-circular rule for traffic management. When they assign you a non-standard level, you fly it — that's your clearance.
key_facts: ATC may assign levels outside semi-circular table for traffic management
Reference: AIP ENR
Common in terminal areas with high traffic volume
ATC-assigned level overrides semi-circular requirements
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State the position by which an aircraft must be at a higher MFA if changing to a track with a higher MFA. AIP GEN
concept: AIP GEN specifies that if you're cruising along a track and then changing to a track that requires a higher MFA, you must be at the higher MFA by the point where the new track segment begins — i.e. the track change point. You can't wait until you're already on the new track to start climbing. This is because the obstacle protection for the new segment starts from that waypoint, not from some point further along. In practice: if you're tracking to a waypoint where the MFA increases, start the climb early enough to be at the new MFA level before you cross the waypoint.
exam_tip: Must be AT the higher MFA BY the track change point — not some time after you've started the new track. Climb early to meet the requirement at the waypoint.
key_facts: Must be at the higher MFA by the track change waypoint
Reference: AIP GEN
Cannot wait until established on the new track to begin climbing
Obstacle protection for new segment starts from the track change point
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP GEN

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for the operation of transponders within the New Zealand FIR. CAR Part 91 & AIP ENR
