concept: CAR rule 91.261 and AIP ENR require transponders to operate in Mode A/C (or Mode S) within the NZ FIR as specified. In controlled airspace (all classes), a serviceable transponder with Mode C (altitude reporting) is mandatory. In uncontrolled airspace where transponder mandatory airspace has been declared, the same applies. ADS-B Out is now mandatory in certain airspace per the NZ ADS-B mandate, which requires GNSS-linked ADS-B Out equipment in addition to the transponder. Transponders must be operational before entering transponder-mandatory airspace and remain so throughout flight in that airspace.
exam_tip: Transponders required in controlled airspace and declared transponder-mandatory airspace. Mode C (altitude reporting) is the minimum standard. ADS-B Out adds a GPS position requirement.
key_facts: Transponder requirements in CAR rule 91.261 and AIP ENR
Mandatory in all controlled airspace with Mode C altitude reporting
Mandatory in designated transponder-mandatory uncontrolled airspace
ADS-B Out also required in ADS-B mandated airspace
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91 rule 91.261, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: Describe the procedures required of pilots operating transponders. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR sets out the procedures for pilots operating transponders. The transponder should be on and set to the assigned code (or 1200 for VFR if no code assigned) at all times in flight. When entering controlled airspace, ensure the code is set before crossing the boundary. When ATC assigns a new code, select it promptly. On final approach, squawk the assigned code unless ATC directs otherwise. When parking or at a gate, transponder should be set to standby (not OFF) to prevent accidental activation. Emergency codes are 7700 (emergency), 7600 (comms failure), 7500 (unlawful interference) — these override any assigned code in the respective emergencies.
exam_tip: Key codes: 7700 emergency, 7600 comms failure, 7500 unlawful interference. These three are almost certainly on every IFR law paper. Standby (not OFF) when parked.
key_facts: Transponder operating procedures in AIP ENR
Squawk assigned code at all times; 1200 for VFR if no code assigned
Emergency squawks: 7700 emergency, 7600 comms failure, 7500 unlawful interference
Set standby when parked, not OFF
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: Describe the procedure whereby ATC can verify the accuracy of the Mode C function of a transponder. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR describes the procedure for ATC to verify Mode C accuracy. When ATC has reason to doubt the accuracy of an aircraft's Mode C altitude readout, they will ask the pilot to verify their altitude. The pilot reads off their altimeter (QNH set correctly) and the controller compares that against the Mode C readout on their display. If there's a discrepancy, the controller will advise the pilot and may ask them to select Mode C off, switch to Mode A only, or take other action until the issue is resolved. Maintaining an accurate Mode C is important because ATC uses it for TCAS resolution advisory coordination and RVSM separation.
exam_tip: ATC verifies Mode C by asking for your altitude and comparing against their display. A discrepancy may result in Mode C being turned off until rectified.
key_facts: Mode C verification procedure in AIP ENR
ATC asks pilot to report altitude; compares against Mode C display
Discrepancy may require Mode C deselection until corrected
Important for RVSM accuracy and TCAS coordination
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State the requirements and limitations on an aircraft operating in transponder mandatory airspace without an operating transponder. CAR Part 91 & AIP ENR
concept: CAR Part 91 and AIP ENR cover what happens if your transponder fails in transponder-mandatory airspace. If it fails before departure, you may not depart without ATC approval. If it fails in flight in transponder-mandatory airspace, you must immediately notify ATC and may be required to leave the transponder-mandatory airspace as soon as practicable, or ATC may provide special handling. ATC has discretion to allow continuation of flight in some circumstances — for example, if diverting would be unsafe or impractical. An aircraft without a serviceable transponder generally cannot enter controlled airspace unless authorised by ATC, and cannot enter RVSM airspace under any circumstances.
exam_tip: Transponder fails in flight in transponder-mandatory airspace: notify ATC immediately. ATC decides if you can continue or must leave. Cannot enter RVSM airspace without transponder under any circumstances.
key_facts: Transponder failure procedures in CAR Part 91 and AIP ENR
Notify ATC immediately on transponder failure in mandatory airspace
ATC may authorise continued flight or require leaving mandatory airspace
RVSM airspace: no entry without serviceable transponder — no exceptions
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State the rules pertaining to operating IFR in the various classes of airspace. CAR Part 91 & AIP ENR
concept: CAR Part 91 and AIP ENR specify the rules for IFR operations in each class of NZ airspace. Class A: IFR only, ATC clearance required, ATC separates all traffic. Class C: IFR and VFR allowed, IFR requires clearance and ATC separation, VFR requires clearance and gets separation from IFR. Class D: IFR and VFR allowed, IFR requires clearance, ATC separates IFR from IFR and provides traffic information on VFR. Class E (NZ does not use this class extensively). Class G: IFR and VFR allowed, no ATC clearance required for IFR but pilot responsible for own separation. For IFR in Class G, you're essentially flying without ATC protection — you must maintain terrain clearance independently and see-and-avoid applies.
exam_tip: Key distinction: Class A = IFR only, full ATC separation. Class C = IFR from IFR AND IFR from VFR separated. Class D = IFR from IFR separated; traffic info on VFR. Class G = no ATC separation at all.
key_facts: IFR airspace rules in CAR Part 91 and AIP ENR
Class A: IFR only, full ATC separation of all flights
Class C: ATC separates IFR/IFR and IFR/VFR; VFR needs clearance
Class G: no ATC clearance or separation for IFR; pilot responsible
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: Describe the vertical limits and purpose of control zones (CTR). CAR Part 71
concept: CAR Part 71 rule 71.55 defines Control Zones (CTR). A CTR is controlled airspace of defined dimensions extending from the surface of the earth upward to a specified altitude or flight level. CTRs are established around aerodromes where aerodrome control service or aerodrome and approach control service is required. The lateral limits must encompass IFR arrival and departure flight paths and extend at least 5 NM from the aerodrome in directions from which instrument approaches are made. The purpose is to protect arriving and departing IFR traffic from the surface upward, where the aircraft is most vulnerable.
exam_tip: CTR starts at the SURFACE and extends up to a defined altitude/FL. Extends at least 5 NM from the aerodrome in instrument approach directions. Purpose: protect IFR traffic from the surface up.
key_facts: CTR definition in CAR Part 71 rule 71.55
Extends from the surface upward to a specified altitude or flight level
Must extend at least 5 NM in directions of instrument approaches
Purpose: protect arriving/departing IFR traffic from the surface
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 71 rule 71.55

syllabus_desc: Describe the vertical limits and purpose of control areas (CTA). CAR Part 71
concept: CAR Part 71 rule 71.53 defines Control Areas (CTA). A CTA is controlled airspace of defined dimensions extending from a specified altitude or flight level above the surface upward (i.e. it has a floor, unlike a CTR which starts at the surface). Types of CTA in NZ include terminal control areas (around busy aerodromes, above the CTR), and upper control areas (enroute). The lower limit of a CTA must be at least 700 ft above the earth surface, and where the lower limit is above 3000 ft AMSL it must coincide with a VFR cruising level. The purpose of CTAs is to protect IFR enroute traffic at the altitudes where they fly.
exam_tip: CTA has a FLOOR above the surface — it doesn't start at the ground (unlike CTR). The floor must be at least 700 ft above terrain. Purpose: protect enroute IFR traffic above a specified altitude.
key_facts: CTA definition in CAR Part 71 rule 71.53
Has a defined floor above the surface (minimum 700 ft above terrain)
Extends from the floor upward to specified altitude/FL
Includes terminal control areas and upper control areas
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 71 rule 71.53

syllabus_desc: State the status and conditions relating to flight in VFR transit lanes. AIP ENR
concept: VFR Transit Lanes (rule 71.57) are designated corridors within controlled airspace that allow VFR traffic to transit without requiring an ATC clearance. They are positioned clear of IFR arrival and departure procedures, with a buffer zone between VFR lane centrelines and IFR aircraft paths. Transit lanes are active only during daylight hours, and the airspace is Class G while the lane is active. They're identified in the AIP by a specific code and have a designated altitude band and frequency. For IFR pilots, VFR transit lanes mean you may have uncontrolled VFR traffic in the lane area — ATC won't be separating you from those aircraft.
exam_tip: VFR transit lanes: VFR can transit without clearance. Day only. Class G while active. IFR pilots: be aware that uncontrolled VFR traffic may be in the lane without ATC coordination.
key_facts: VFR transit lanes defined in CAR Part 71 rule 71.57
Allow VFR transit through controlled airspace without ATC clearance
Active daylight hours only; Class G airspace while active
IFR aircraft may encounter uncontrolled VFR traffic in lane area
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 71 rule 71.57, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: Describe the status and purpose of a general aviation area (GAA), and state the process for IFR flights to transit through GAA airspace. CAR Part 91 & AIP ENR
concept: General Aviation Areas (GAA, rule 71.59) are areas of controlled airspace designated to allow VFR flights access without ATC clearance during specific periods. GAAs are active during daylight hours only and are Class G while active. For IFR flights, CAR Part 91 and AIP ENR specify a procedure for transiting through GAA airspace: the IFR flight must contact ATC before entering the GAA, advise of the transit, and ATC may provide a specific clearance or routing to allow the IFR flight through the GAA while keeping it separated from the uncontrolled VFR traffic in the area.
exam_tip: GAA = VFR access without clearance during day. For IFR, must contact ATC and get specific clearance to transit through GAA. Don't just fly through as if it were normal controlled airspace.
key_facts: GAA defined in CAR Part 71 rule 71.59
VFR access without ATC clearance; daylight hours only; Class G while active
IFR transiting GAA must contact ATC for specific clearance or routing
Reference: CAR Part 91 and AIP ENR
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 71 rule 71.59, CAR Part 91, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: Describe visual reporting points
concept: Visual Reporting Points (VRPs) are geographically identifiable landmarks used to define positions in the vicinity of aerodromes, particularly for arriving and departing VFR traffic in or near controlled airspace. They're typically prominent landmarks visible from the air — bridges, road intersections, lakes, headlands — and are published in the AIP aerodrome pages (AIPNZ Vol 4) with names (e.g. 'Dairy Flat', 'Clevedon') and positions. IFR pilots need to be aware of VRPs because ATC may use them when providing instructions to other traffic, and STAR procedures may reference them for VFR joining the approach path. They're not IFR fixes, but they appear in RTF and on charts.
exam_tip: VRPs are identifiable landmarks for VFR position reporting in the aerodrome environment. They're published in the AIP (Vol 4). IFR pilots encounter them in ATC RTF when ATC sequences VFR traffic.
key_facts: VRPs are geographically identifiable landmarks for VFR position reporting
Published in AIPNZ Vol 4 aerodrome pages
Used by ATC for sequencing VFR traffic in aerodrome vicinity
Not IFR fixes — but appear in RTF and on aerodrome approach charts
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP Vol 4

syllabus_desc: Describe the status of controlled airspace when ATC go off duty. AIP GEN
concept: AIP GEN specifies what happens to controlled airspace classification when ATC goes off duty. When an ATC unit closes (goes off duty), the controlled airspace it was providing a service in reverts to Class G (uncontrolled airspace) for the duration of the ATC closure. The airspace boundaries and dimensions don't change — the transponder-mandatory and other airspace requirements may still apply — but no ATC service is being provided. Published in the AIP for each aerodrome are the hours of ATC operation. IFR pilots must be aware that arriving or transiting outside those hours puts them into what was controlled airspace but is now effectively uncontrolled.
exam_tip: When ATC goes off duty: controlled airspace reverts to Class G for the ATC closure period. The airspace dimensions don't change, but no service is provided and classification becomes Class G.
key_facts: When ATC closes, controlled airspace reverts to Class G
Reference: AIP GEN
Boundaries and dimensions unchanged, but no ATC service provided
ATC operating hours published in AIPNZ Vol 4 for each aerodrome
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP GEN

syllabus_desc: State the restrictions on operating an aircraft in a restricted area. CAR Part 91 & AIP ENR
concept: CAR Part 91 and AIP ENR restrict operations in restricted areas (designated NZRXX). A restricted area is airspace of defined dimensions within which the flight of aircraft is restricted in accordance with certain specified conditions. The administering authority controls entry. Unless you've been authorised by the administering authority, you cannot enter a restricted area when it's active. NOTAMs activate restricted areas (typically 24 hours' notice). For IFR flights, you'd request entry clearance through ATC who will coordinate with the administering authority. Some restricted areas have established entry procedures for coordinated IFR traffic.
exam_tip: Restricted area = entry by permission of administering authority only. Check NOTAM for activation. For IFR: ATC coordinates with the authority. Can't enter active restricted area without authorisation.
key_facts: Restricted area restrictions in CAR Part 91 and AIP ENR
Entry restricted to authorised aircraft only
Administering authority controls access
NOTAM required 24 hours before activation; IFR entry coordinated via ATC
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 71 rule 71.153, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State the restrictions on operating an aircraft in a military operational area (MOA). CAR Part 91 & AIP ENR
concept: Military Operational Areas (MOAs, rule 71.155) are airspace where military activities that may be hazardous to civil aircraft are conducted. When a MOA is active (activated by NOTAM), civil aircraft may operate in or through the MOA but must accept the risks associated with the military activity (live ordnance, low-level fast jets, formations, etc.). For IFR flights, the practical restriction is that ATC will typically advise of MOA activity and may suggest routing around it, or you can request clearance through it accepting the stated restrictions. Some MOAs have specific procedures for civil IFR transit — check the AIP.
exam_tip: MOA = military activity that may be hazardous. Civil aircraft CAN enter but accept the risk. Check NOTAM for activation and type of activity. ATC will advise; routing around is common.
key_facts: MOA restrictions in CAR Part 71 rule 71.155 and AIP ENR
Military activity may be hazardous to civil aircraft
Civil aircraft may enter but accept responsibility for risks
NOTAM required 24 hours before activation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 71 rule 71.155, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State the restrictions and operating considerations relating to operating an aircraft in a mandatory broadcast zone (MBZ). CAR Part 91 & AIP ENR
concept: Mandatory Broadcast Zones (MBZs, rule 71.157) are designated uncontrolled airspace where pilots are required to make broadcasts of their position and intentions on the published MBZ frequency. MBZs are established at aerodromes where traffic density or operational circumstances make position broadcasting mandatory for safety. Broadcasts are required when: on departure (before taxiing, before entering runway, departing); when entering the MBZ area; at reporting points around the MBZ; and on approach and landing. No ATC clearance is needed — it's a self-announcement system. For IFR approaches into an MBZ aerodrome, you make the same position broadcasts as other traffic.
exam_tip: MBZ = mandatory broadcast zone (not clearance zone). You must broadcast on the published frequency. No ATC clearance required. Works like a CTAF/MULTICOM — everyone self-announces.
key_facts: MBZ defined in CAR Part 71 rule 71.157 and AIP ENR
Pilots must make mandatory position broadcasts on published frequency
No ATC clearance required — it's a self-announcement system
Broadcasts required: departing, entering MBZ, at reporting points, on approach
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 71 rule 71.157, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State the restrictions and operating considerations relating to operating an aircraft in a volcanic hazard area (VHA). CAR Part 91 & AIP ENR
concept: Volcanic Hazard Areas (VHAs, rule 71.159) are designated around active NZ volcanoes (White Island/Whakaari, Tongariro, Ruapehu, etc.). They are airspace where volcanic activity presents a hazard to aircraft. Operations within or through a VHA are governed by the current NOTAM status. When a VHA is activated by a volcanic eruption or elevated volcanic activity, specific restrictions apply — typically a minimum altitude and/or no-fly zone around the volcanic vent. For IFR operations, ATC will provide routing advice and current NOTAM information. The primary hazards are volcanic ash (which destroys engines), gases, and flying rocks/tephra.
exam_tip: VHAs are around active NZ volcanoes. When activated (by NOTAM), specific restrictions and minimum altitudes apply. Primary aviation hazards: volcanic ash (engine destruction), toxic gases, tephra (rocks).
key_facts: VHA defined in CAR Part 71 rule 71.159 and AIP ENR
Established around active NZ volcanoes
Restrictions activated by NOTAM based on volcanic activity level
Primary hazards: volcanic ash, toxic gases, tephra
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 71 rule 71.159, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State the restrictions and operating considerations relating to operating an aircraft in a danger area. CAR Part 91 & AIP ENR
concept: Danger Areas (rule 71.161) are airspace of defined dimensions within which activities dangerous to aircraft may exist at specified times. Unlike restricted areas, entry into a danger area is not prohibited — but you're accepting risk. Danger areas are activated by NOTAM (24 hours' notice required). Typical activities in danger areas include weapons training, artillery, aerial gunnery, or parachute operations. For IFR flights, check the NOTAM to understand what activity is occurring, the altitude range, and the activation times. If the activity is genuinely hazardous (live fire, artillery), even though entry isn't technically prohibited, ATC will advise routing around.
exam_tip: Danger area = entry NOT prohibited but activity is potentially dangerous. Different from restricted area where entry requires authorisation. Check NOTAM for type and extent of danger.
key_facts: Danger area defined in CAR Part 71 rule 71.161 and AIP ENR
Entry not prohibited — but activity is potentially dangerous
Activated by NOTAM 24 hours in advance
Typical activities: weapons training, artillery, aerial gunnery
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 71 rule 71.161, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State the restrictions and operating considerations relating to operating an aircraft in a parachute landing area (PLA). AIP ENR
concept: A Parachute Landing Area (PLA) is a designated area used for parachuting operations, as published in the AIP ENR. Aircraft operating in or near a PLA must exercise caution as there may be parachutists free-falling in the area. The AIP ENR gives the location, dimensions, altitude range, and operating hours of each PLA. For IFR flights, a PLA may restrict your routing if it's active and within your intended path. Check NOTAMs for activation. The hazard is primarily a collision risk with free-falling parachutists who may not be visible until very close range.
exam_tip: PLA = parachute landing area. Main hazard is collision with free-falling parachutists. Check AIP ENR for location and dimensions, NOTAMs for activation. This replaced the old 'PDZ' (parachute drop zone) — the term changed in 2024.
key_facts: PLA (parachute landing area) in AIP ENR
Designates area used for parachuting operations
Hazard: collision risk with free-falling parachutists
Term changed from PDZ to PLA in 2024 per AC61-17 Rev 16
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State the operating considerations relating to operating an aircraft in a common frequency zone (CFZ). AIP ENR
concept: Common Frequency Zones (CFZ) are areas of airspace where a common radio frequency is designated for all traffic. They're typically established in areas of mixed VFR/IFR traffic without a dedicated ATC facility, where self-announcing on a common frequency improves traffic awareness. Pilots transiting a CFZ should monitor and broadcast on the published CFZ frequency. Unlike a MBZ, a CFZ doesn't necessarily require mandatory broadcasts — it's a 'common awareness' frequency rather than a mandatory announcement requirement. Check the AIP for the specific requirements of each CFZ.
exam_tip: CFZ = Common Frequency Zone. Designated frequency for all traffic in the area. Similar to CTAF in concept — everyone monitoring and broadcasting on the same frequency improves situational awareness.
key_facts: CFZ designates common radio frequency for all traffic in the area
Reference: AIP ENR
Improves traffic awareness in areas without dedicated ATC
Check AIP for specific CFZ requirements and frequency
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State the operating considerations relating to operating an aircraft over or close to temporary hazards/airspace. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR provides information on temporary hazards and temporary airspace restrictions, published via NOTAM. Temporary hazards include things like air displays, fireworks, mass parachute jumps, military exercises, and temporary restricted areas. When operating IFR in areas with temporary hazards, you should: check current NOTAMs before and during flight; advise ATC of your routing and get their input; if required, coordinate with the administering authority. For IFR operations, ATC will normally have NOTAM information and can advise you of restrictions. Temporary restricted airspace (NOTAM R) follows the same rules as permanent restricted areas — entry by authorisation only.
exam_tip: Temporary hazards published via NOTAM. For IFR, ATC is your first port of call for current NOTAM status on temporary restrictions. Brief NOTAMs thoroughly pre-flight.
key_facts: Temporary hazards published via NOTAM and in AIP ENR
Types: air displays, fireworks, military exercises, temporary restrictions
ATC has NOTAM info and will advise routing around active temporary hazards
Temporary restricted airspace follows same rules as permanent restricted areas
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: Explain the requirements for the operation of an aircraft in RNP airspace. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR covers the requirements for operating in RNP (Required Navigation Performance) airspace in NZ. To fly in designated RNP airspace, the aircraft must hold the navigation specification approval for that airspace (e.g. RNAV 2 for enroute, RNP 1 for terminal), the avionics must be serviceable and the GNSS database current, and the operator must hold a PBN operational approval per AC91-21. The pilot must verify RAIM availability (for GPS-dependent RNP) before departure for the planned route and alternates. In flight, any navigation degradation that means the aircraft can no longer meet the RNP specification must be reported to ATC immediately.
exam_tip: RNP airspace requirements: aircraft approval, database currency, RAIM availability check pre-flight, and report any degradation in flight. These are the four key points for RNP airspace operations.
key_facts: RNP airspace requirements in AIP ENR and AC91-21
Aircraft must hold PBN operational approval per AC91-21
Avionics serviceable and GNSS database current required
RAIM check before flight; report degradation to ATC in flight
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR, AC91-21

syllabus_desc: Interpret airspace information on aeronautical charts
concept: NZ aeronautical charts used for IFR include: Enroute Charts (ENRC) — showing airways, MEAs, VORs, NDBs, compulsory and on-request reporting points, airspace boundaries, and altitude data; Area Charts (ARC) — more detailed for terminal areas; Instrument Approach Charts (IAC) — for each published approach procedure showing plan view, profile, and minima; SID and STAR charts — for published departure and arrival procedures. Airspace boundaries are depicted with different line styles and colours — controlled airspace boundaries, restricted areas, danger areas, MBZs etc. all have specific symbology defined in the AIP chart legend.
exam_tip: This is a practical/visual item — expect to be given a chart and asked to interpret airspace boundaries, altitude values, or identify airspace types. Know the standard symbology: controlled airspace = solid blue/grey line, restricted = hatching, etc.
key_facts: IFR charts: ENRCs, ARCs, IACs, SID/STAR charts
Airspace depicted with standardised symbology per AIP legend
Controlled airspace, restricted areas, danger areas all have distinct depiction
Chart legend in AIP GEN explains all symbols
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the limitations on the use of a place as an aerodrome or heliport. CAR Part 91
concept: CAR Part 91 restricts use of a place as an aerodrome. An aerodrome must be certified or registered under Part 139 if it's used for regular public transport or certain commercial operations. For private and general aviation, a landowner can designate land as a private aerodrome (not requiring Part 139 certification) but must ensure it's safe for operations. The IFR implications: IFR operations into an uncertified private aerodrome may not have published approach procedures, lighting requirements, or aerodrome information in the AIP. For any regular IFR operation, you'd want a certified aerodrome with published approaches and proper equipment.
exam_tip: Certified aerodromes (Part 139) required for RPT. Private aerodromes can be used for general aviation but may lack IFR infrastructure. Check whether the aerodrome has published procedures and required equipment before planning an IFR flight there.
key_facts: Aerodrome use restrictions in CAR Part 91
Part 139 certification required for RPT and specified commercial operations
Private aerodromes can be used for GA without Part 139 certification
IFR operations require aerodrome with suitable procedures and infrastructure
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Describe the method of runway designation. AIP AD
concept: Runway designation is described in AIP AD 1.02. Runways are designated by their magnetic heading rounded to the nearest 10 degrees and then divided by 10. So a runway with a magnetic heading of 278° becomes Runway 28. For parallel runways the suffixes L (left), C (centre), and R (right) are added. A runway's reciprocal designation is 18 apart — Runway 28 has a reciprocal of Runway 10. In NZ, because magnetic variation changes over time, runway designations are occasionally updated as variation shifts enough to change the nearest 10-degree rounding. Some airports have a promulgated effective date for designation changes.
exam_tip: Runway number = magnetic heading ÷ 10, rounded to nearest. Reciprocal = add or subtract 18. Parallel runways add L/C/R. Magnetic variation can require runway renaming over time.
key_facts: Runway designation method in AIP AD 1.02
Runway number = magnetic heading ÷ 10 rounded to nearest whole number
Reciprocal runway = runway number ± 18
Parallel runways: L (left), C (centre), R (right) suffix added
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP AD 1.02

syllabus_desc: Describe the movement area of an aerodrome. CAR Part 1
concept: CAR Part 1 defines movement area as the part of an aerodrome to be used for the take-off, landing, and taxiing of aircraft, consisting of the manoeuvring area (runways and taxiways) and the apron(s). In controlled airspace, clearances are required before entering the manoeuvring area — you need tower clearance to enter a runway and specific clearance to cross a runway. The apron is usually managed separately by the airport company with its own procedures. Understanding the movement area distinction matters for IFR ground operations because different rules and clearance requirements apply depending on whether you're on the apron, taxiway, or runway.
exam_tip: Movement area = manoeuvring area (runways + taxiways) + apron. ATC clearance required to enter manoeuvring area at controlled aerodrome. The apron is typically airport company-managed.
key_facts: Movement area defined in CAR Part 1
Consists of manoeuvring area (runways and taxiways) and apron(s)
ATC clearance required to enter manoeuvring area at controlled aerodrome
Apron managed separately by airport company
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 1

syllabus_desc: Interpret information on aerodrome/heliport charts. AIP GEN & AIP Volume 4
concept: Aerodrome and heliport charts in the AIP NZ contain: an aerodrome plan view (showing runways, taxiways, aprons, terminal buildings, nav aids); a profile view showing approach angles and lighting; declared distances (TORA, TODA, ASDA, LDA) for each runway direction; elevation of runway thresholds; touchdown zone elevation; gradient; surface type; lighting types and availability; and operational hours. Additional information includes ground frequency, ATIS, approach frequencies, fuel types, and emergency services. Heliport charts show the same general structure adapted for helicopter operations including FATO and TLOF dimensions.
exam_tip: Aerodrome charts are in AIPNZ Vol 4. Know the five declared distances (TORA, TODA, ASDA, LDA, EMDA). Know that threshold elevation appears on approach charts — not always on the aerodrome plan view.
key_facts: Aerodrome charts in AIPNZ Vol 4 and AIP GEN
Contains: plan view, declared distances, threshold elevations, lighting, frequencies
Declared distances: TORA, TODA, ASDA, LDA (EMDA where applicable)
Heliport charts show FATO and TLOF dimensions
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP GEN, AIPNZ Vol 4

syllabus_desc: Describe the lighting intensity classifications
concept: Aerodrome lighting systems are classified into intensity levels. The standard classifications are High Intensity (HI), Medium Intensity (MI), and Low Intensity (LI). These classifications appear in the AIP AD section for each aerodrome. High intensity systems are used on major IFR-capable runways — they're bright enough to be seen in poor visibility and in daylight. Medium intensity is typical at regional airports. Low intensity is common at smaller aerodromes with predominantly day/VFR operations. For IFR approach planning, you need to verify that the lighting at destination and alternate meets the requirements of your planned procedure.
exam_tip: Three intensity classifications: High Intensity (HI), Medium Intensity (MI), Low Intensity (LI). Published in the aerodrome section of the AIP. HI is required for most published instrument approaches.
key_facts: Lighting intensity classifications: High Intensity (HI), Medium Intensity (MI), Low Intensity (LI)
Published in AIP AD section for each aerodrome
HI required for most instrument approaches in poor visibility
Intensity affects whether lighting is visible at published procedure minima
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP AD

syllabus_desc: Describe the following lighting system: Runway edge lighting (REDL)
concept: Runway Edge Lighting (REDL) consists of white lights along the edge of the runway (last 600 m or one-third of runway, whichever is less, may be yellow to indicate the end zone). REDL defines the lateral limits of the usable runway for landing and take-off. The lights are visible from all angles above the horizontal. REDL is essential for IFR night operations and for any approach where visibility is reduced, as it defines the runway boundary during the approach and roll-out phases.
exam_tip: REDL = white lights along runway edges; last 600 m/one-third may be yellow. Defines the lateral runway limits. Key for IFR night operations.
key_facts: REDL: white lights along runway edges
Last 600 m or one-third of runway (whichever less) may be yellow
Defines lateral runway limits
Essential for IFR night approach identification
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP AD

syllabus_desc: Describe the following lighting system: Runway landing threshold lighting (RTHL)
concept: Runway Landing Threshold Lighting (RTHL) marks the landing threshold — the beginning of the runway available for landing. RTHL consists of a row of green lights (visible to approaching aircraft) across the threshold, often combined with red lights on the opposite side visible to aircraft on the runway looking back toward the threshold (indicating the displaced portion if applicable). Green = start of landing area. If there's a displaced threshold, the pre-threshold area may have red lights marking the non-landing zone and the green threshold lights are positioned at the actual landing point.
exam_tip: RTHL: green lights across the threshold visible to approaching aircraft. Green = start of the landing area. Displaced thresholds have the green moved to the actual landing point.
key_facts: RTHL: green lights across the runway threshold
Visible to approaching aircraft — marks start of landing area
Displaced thresholds: green at actual landing threshold point
Often combined with red lights visible in the opposite direction
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP AD

syllabus_desc: Describe the following lighting system: Runway end lighting (RENL)
concept: Runway End Lighting (RENL) marks the end of the runway. RENL consists of a row of red lights across the end of the runway, visible to aircraft on the runway and on approach. Red = stop, end of usable surface. The purpose is to clearly identify the runway end during landing roll-out, rejected take-offs, and go-arounds at low altitude where the end of the runway may not be visually apparent until close range. Combined with REDL (edge lighting), RENL defines all four corners of the usable runway.
exam_tip: RENL: red lights across the runway END. Red means stop — end of usable surface. Combined with RTHL green (start) and REDL white (sides) to fully define the runway.
key_facts: RENL: red lights across the runway end
Visible to aircraft on runway and on approach
Marks end of usable runway surface
Combined with RTHL and REDL to define full runway boundary
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP AD

syllabus_desc: Describe the following lighting system: Runway centreline lighting system (RCLL)
concept: Runway Centreline Lighting System (RCLL) is a series of lights embedded in the runway surface along the centreline. The lights are white from the threshold to within 900 m of the end, alternating red and white from 900 m to 300 m from the end, then all red for the last 300 m. RCLL enables pilots to track the centreline during low visibility take-off and landing operations, particularly in Category II and Category III instrument approaches where the visual segment is extremely short. RCLL is typically found at major international airports (Auckland, Wellington, Christchurch) and is required for CAT II/III operations.
exam_tip: RCLL: centreline lights in the runway surface. White to 900 m from end, alternating red/white 900 m-300 m, all red last 300 m. Required for CAT II/III operations.
key_facts: RCLL: in-pavement centreline lights
White from threshold to 900 m from end
Alternating red/white from 900 m to 300 m from end
All red for last 300 m; required for CAT II/III operations
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP AD

syllabus_desc: Describe the following lighting system: Runway end identifier lighting (REIL)
concept: Runway End Identifier Lights (REIL) are two flashing lights positioned on either side of the runway threshold. They flash synchronously (or sequentially) to positively identify the threshold, particularly when the approach lighting is minimal or when the aerodrome background lighting makes the threshold difficult to identify. REILs are particularly useful at aerodromes without full approach lighting systems, providing an easily identifiable flashing reference for the threshold location during the visual segment of an approach.
exam_tip: REIL: two flashing lights at the threshold sides. Purpose: positively identify the threshold. Particularly useful where approach lighting is minimal.
key_facts: REIL: two flashing lights on each side of the runway threshold
Flash synchronously or sequentially
Purpose: positively identify runway threshold
Useful where approach lighting is limited
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP AD

syllabus_desc: Describe the following lighting system: Approach lighting systems (ALS)
concept: Approach Lighting Systems (ALS) are lighting arrays installed before the runway threshold to assist pilots in transitioning from instrument flight to visual flight during the final approach. NZ uses several ALS types including ALSF (Approach Lighting System with sequenced Flashers), MALSR (Medium intensity Approach Lighting System with Runway alignment indicator lights), and simpler bar systems. The ALS provides a visual reference corridor aligned with the runway during the approach in reduced visibility. At CAT I minima, you're expected to see the ALS before you can commence descent from DA — it counts as the required visual reference.
exam_tip: ALS guides pilots from IFR to visual. The approach lighting system counts as a visual reference for descent from DA/MDA. Know that ALS is one of the items you can see to justify descent below minima.
key_facts: ALS: lighting arrays before the runway threshold
Provides visual corridor aligned with runway during approach
Multiple types: ALSF, MALSR, and simpler bar systems
ALS sighting counts as visual reference for descent below DA
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP AD

syllabus_desc: Describe the following lighting system: Circling guidance lighting (CGL)
concept: Circling Guidance Lighting (CGL) assists pilots executing a circling approach — an instrument approach where the final approach track is not aligned with the runway being used for landing. CGL provides additional lighting to help the pilot maintain orientation to the aerodrome while manoeuvring visually from the approach track to the landing runway. It may consist of additional perimeter lights, enhanced REDL, or other visual aids on the landing runway that help the pilot identify the runway they intend to land on during the circling manoeuvre.
exam_tip: CGL assists with circling approaches — when you fly an ILS to runway 12 but need to land on runway 30, CGL helps you stay oriented to the landing runway during the visual manoeuvre.
key_facts: CGL: Circling Guidance Lighting for circling approaches
Assists pilot in maintaining aerodrome orientation during circling manoeuvre
Provides visual reference to the landing runway
Used when landing runway differs from instrument approach runway
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP AD

syllabus_desc: Describe the following lighting system: Runway lead in lighting (RLLS)
concept: Runway Lead-In Lighting System (RLLS) is a series of sequenced flashing lights installed along a desired approach path to a runway, used to guide aircraft along a specific approach route (often to avoid terrain or noise-sensitive areas). The RLLS 'leads' the pilot in on the required approach path before the ALS takes over. They flash in sequence away from the aerodrome (pointing toward the aircraft on approach) and are visible at significant distance, helping aircraft navigate the initial approach segment in reduced visibility.
exam_tip: RLLS: sequenced flashing lights that guide you along a specific approach path to the ALS/runway. Think of it as an extension of the approach lighting further out on a specific approach track.
key_facts: RLLS: Runway Lead-In Lighting System
Sequenced flashing lights along a specific approach path
Guides aircraft to the ALS from the initial approach segment
Used where specific routing is required to avoid terrain or noise
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP AD

syllabus_desc: Describe the following lighting system: Pilot activated lighting (PAL)
concept: Pilot Activated Lighting (PAL) allows pilots to switch on aerodrome lighting by transmitting on the published CTAF/MBZ frequency. Typically, five or seven clicks within 5 seconds on the published frequency activates all aerodrome lighting for a set period (usually 15 minutes). Some systems have selectable intensity levels — more clicks gives higher intensity. PAL is used at aerodromes that don't have continuously staffed operations, allowing arriving aircraft to illuminate the runway and approach lighting before landing. For IFR, PAL is important because the lighting may be off when you start your approach — you need to activate it before you need it (on initial approach, not on final).
exam_tip: PAL: pilot activates aerodrome lighting by transmitting on CTAF/MBZ. Do it on INITIAL approach, not final — gives the lights time to warm up and you time to verify they're on.
key_facts: PAL: Pilot Activated Lighting by radio transmission
Typically 5-7 clicks on CTAF/MBZ frequency activates lighting
Lighting stays on for approximately 15 minutes after activation
Activate on initial approach, not final, to ensure lights are on when needed
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP AD

syllabus_desc: Describe the following lighting system: T-Visual approach slope indicators (T-VASIS)
concept: T-Visual Approach Slope Indicators (T-VASIS) provide visual slope guidance to pilots on final approach using a T-shaped array of lights. The system uses two rows of lights — a wing bar (horizontal) and a fly-to bar (vertical). When on the correct glide slope, the pilot sees the full T configuration. When above the slope, only the wing bar is visible (all white, fly higher). When below the slope, additional lights below the wing bar are visible (indicating fly higher). T-VASIS is the older system, now largely replaced by PAPI in most installations.
exam_tip: T-VASIS uses a T-shaped array. On slope = full T visible. Typically being replaced by PAPI. The memory aid: T for 'on Track' (slope).
key_facts: T-VASIS: T-shaped array providing visual slope guidance
On slope: full T configuration visible
Above slope: only wing bar visible
Being replaced by PAPI in most modern installations
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP AD

syllabus_desc: Describe the following lighting system: Visual approach slope indicators (VASIS)
concept: Visual Approach Slope Indicators (VASIS) provide visual glide slope guidance using two bars of lights (upwind and downwind bars), each displaying either red (below the approach line) or white (above the approach line). When on slope: both bars white (or the standard coding is far bar white, near bar red — check installation). When above slope: both bars white. When below slope: both bars red. The common three-light-bar VASIS uses different colour combinations for different slope positions. VASIS is mainly found at older aerodromes; PAPI has largely replaced it.
exam_tip: VASIS uses red (low) and white (high) bars. On slope: upwind (far) bar white, downwind (near) bar red — 'white over red, you're dead in bed' (on the slope). 'Red over red, you're dead' (too low).
key_facts: VASIS: two-bar system using red (low) and white (high) lights
On slope: far bar white, near bar red ('white over red, on the glide')
Below slope: both bars red
Above slope: both bars white
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP AD

syllabus_desc: Describe the following lighting system: Precision approach path indicators (PAPI)
concept: Precision Approach Path Indicators (PAPI) are the standard modern visual approach slope indicator in NZ. Four light units are mounted beside the runway (usually on the left side). The lights can appear white (above slope) or red (below slope). Reading from left to right (runway side to outside): when all four are white = well above slope; three white one red = slightly above; two white two red = on slope; one white three red = slightly below; all four red = well below slope. The standard approach slope is 3°, but some PAPI installations are set for other angles. PAPI is used for all categories of IFR approach that have a visual segment.
exam_tip: PAPI: four lights, read left to right (left = inside nearest runway). 4 white = too high; 2 white 2 red = on slope; 4 red = too low. The transition from 2/2 to 3 red should trigger immediate action.
key_facts: PAPI: four lights on runway side
Read left to right: 4 white = high; 2 white/2 red = on slope; 4 red = low
Standard slope 3° (may vary by installation)
Used for all IFR approaches with a visual segment
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP AD

syllabus_desc: Describe aerodrome beacons
concept: Aerodrome beacons are rotating lights installed at controlled aerodromes to make the aerodrome visually identifiable from the air, particularly at night and in reduced visibility. NZ land aerodrome beacons display alternating white and green flashes. The beacon rotates continuously and is visible at long range. Heliport beacons flash alternating white and yellow. Water aerodrome beacons flash alternating white and yellow (same as heliport but at different flash rates in some installations). The beacon helps pilots locate the aerodrome when flying towards it before they have the runway lighting in sight.
exam_tip: Aerodrome beacon: alternating white and green = land aerodrome. Alternating white and yellow = heliport. The beacon helps you find the aerodrome at night from distance.
key_facts: Aerodrome beacons aid identification at night and in reduced visibility
Land aerodrome: alternating white and green flashes
Heliport: alternating white and yellow flashes
Visible at long range to assist aerodrome location
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP AD

syllabus_desc: State the transponder code a pilot should set to indicate an emergency condition. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR specifies that when an aircraft is in an emergency, the pilot should squawk Mode A code 7700. This is the universal ICAO emergency squawk. When 7700 is set, it triggers an alarm on ATC radar displays, highlighting the aircraft and alerting controllers immediately. The pilot should also declare MAYDAY on the appropriate frequency if radio is available. Setting 7700 doesn't replace a voice call — it supplements it. Controllers will immediately attempt to contact the aircraft. If already in communication with ATC on an assigned code, you can squawk 7700 in addition to the MAYDAY call.
exam_tip: Emergency squawk = 7700. Three emergency codes: 7700 (emergency), 7600 (comms failure), 7500 (unlawful interference). 7700 triggers an ATC alarm immediately.
key_facts: Emergency transponder code: 7700
Triggers immediate alarm on ATC radar displays
Used for any aircraft emergency (engine failure, medical, fire, etc.)
Combined with MAYDAY call on appropriate frequency when radio available
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State the transponder code a pilot should set to indicate a loss of communications. AIP ENR
concept: When radio communications are lost, AIP ENR specifies the pilot should squawk Mode A code 7600. Setting 7600 tells ATC controllers that the aircraft has lost communications. ATC will then take steps to establish contact by trying various frequencies and may use light signals if the aircraft is within visual range of the tower. The pilot should continue to squawk 7600 throughout the comms failure, fly the standard comms failure procedure (hold, then proceed to destination at expected approach time), and keep the transponder on for ATC tracking. Don't turn the transponder off — it's ATC's only way to track you once comms are lost.
exam_tip: Comms failure = squawk 7600. ATC tries to re-establish via frequencies and light signals. Keep transponder on — it's the only way ATC can track you. Fly the standard comms failure procedure.
key_facts: Communications failure transponder code: 7600
Signals to ATC that radio communications have been lost
ATC will try alternate frequencies and light signals
Keep transponder on throughout comms failure for ATC tracking
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State the transponder code a pilot should set to indicate that the aircraft is being subjected to unlawful interference. AIP ENR
concept: If an aircraft is subjected to unlawful interference (hijacking), the pilot squawks Mode A code 7500. This is the universal ICAO hijack squawk code. ATC controllers who see 7500 on their display will immediately initiate the appropriate authority response — police, military, and security services will be alerted. The pilot should squawk 7500 without alerting the person causing the interference if possible. On some transponders there is a 'HIJACK' or 'SPI' button that directly activates 7500. The pilot should not acknowledge to the hijacker that an emergency transponder code is being set.
exam_tip: Unlawful interference (hijack) = squawk 7500. Set it discreetly. ATC will alert security services. Don't let the person causing interference know you've set it.
key_facts: Unlawful interference (hijacking) transponder code: 7500
Set discreetly without alerting the person causing interference
ATC alerts police, military, and security services on seeing 7500
Some transponders have dedicated HIJACK button for 7500
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: Describe the means by which ATC will verify the transmission of an emergency SSR transponder code. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR describes how ATC verifies that an aircraft has indeed transmitted an emergency SSR code. The controller will observe the code on their radar display and then call the aircraft on the assigned frequency asking them to confirm the squawk. If the pilot confirms (or the aircraft is the only one with that code in the display), the controller accepts the code is intentional. If the pilot says it was accidental (e.g. selected 7700 by mistake while changing codes), the controller will ask them to revert to the previous code and the alarm is cancelled. If the pilot doesn't respond to the radio check, the controller treats the code as genuine and takes appropriate action.
exam_tip: ATC verifies by radio call asking pilot to confirm the squawk. Accidental activation: tell ATC immediately and revert to your assigned code. No radio response = ATC treats it as genuine emergency.
key_facts: ATC verification of emergency SSR code by radio call to aircraft
Pilot confirms or denies the squawk on frequency
Accidental activation: advise ATC and revert to assigned code
No response from pilot: controller treats code as genuine
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: Describe the use of the speechless technique using un-modulated transmissions. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR describes the speechless technique for aircraft that have lost the ability to transmit but can still receive radio (one-way communications). ATC calls the aircraft and asks the pilot to respond by transmitting un-modulated (silent) carrier wave — by pressing the PTT without speaking. A response of 'carrier only' (a silent transmission) confirms the pilot is receiving. ATC can then conduct one-sided communication: 'if you understand, squawk 7600 and transmit twice' or 'if you are proceeding to [destination], transmit once.' This allows ATC to establish that the pilot is safe and aware, and to get approximate information about intentions.
exam_tip: Speechless technique: ATC calls, pilot responds with silent carrier (press PTT, don't speak). ATC uses yes/no questions answered by transmit count or transponder changes. Confirms one-way comms.
key_facts: Speechless technique for one-way communications in AIP ENR
Pilot presses PTT without speaking — un-modulated carrier confirms receiving
ATC asks yes/no questions; pilot responds with number of transmissions
Also uses transponder squawk changes to confirm understanding
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State the procedures for the emergency activation of an ELT. AIP GEN
concept: AIP GEN specifies the procedure for emergency ELT activation. 406 MHz ELTs can be activated manually (typically by moving an arming switch) or automatically (via the g-switch on impact). In a survival situation (forced landing, ditching, etc.) where rescue is needed, the pilot activates the ELT manually as soon as possible. The ELT should be activated AFTER securing the aircraft and survivors — not during the emergency itself. For water ditching, the ELT should be removed from the aircraft if possible and taken to the life raft, as the aircraft may sink. The ELT signal is detected by COSPAS-SARSAT satellites and relayed to RCCNZ within minutes.
exam_tip: Manual ELT activation: arm the switch after landing/ditching. 406 MHz sends location data via satellite to RCCNZ. Take the ELT with you if abandoning the aircraft — it's no good if the aircraft sinks with it.
key_facts: ELT emergency activation procedures in AIP GEN
Manual activation: arm the switch after forced landing or ditching
Automatic activation via g-switch on impact
Remove ELT from aircraft if abandoning — take to life raft or clear of wreckage
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP GEN

syllabus_desc: State the pilot action required following the inadvertent transmission of an ELT. AIP GEN
concept: AIP GEN specifies what to do if an ELT is inadvertently activated. If you realise an ELT is transmitting unintentionally (e.g. after a hard landing, or someone knocked the arming switch), you must: immediately deactivate the ELT; contact ATC or the nearest ATS unit by the fastest means available and advise of the inadvertent activation, your call sign, the type of ELT, the time of activation, and the time of deactivation; and if you are able, monitor 121.5 MHz for any response to the ELT signal. A quick notification prevents RCCNZ from initiating a search and wastes scarce SAR resources.
exam_tip: Inadvertent ELT: deactivate immediately, then notify ATS as fast as possible with all the details (callsign, ELT type, activation time, deactivation time). Monitor 121.5 MHz for any response.
key_facts: Inadvertent ELT procedure in AIP GEN
Immediately deactivate the ELT
Notify ATS as soon as possible: callsign, ELT type, activation time, deactivation time
Monitor 121.5 MHz to check for any response to the signal
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP GEN

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for the operational testing of an ELT. AIP GEN
concept: AIP GEN sets requirements for operational testing of ELTs. Testing of 406 MHz ELTs must be done according to the manufacturer's instructions — typically using the built-in self-test function that does NOT transmit a live signal. Do NOT test by actually activating the ELT and transmitting on 406 MHz or 121.5 MHz — this triggers COSPAS-SARSAT satellite detection and may result in a false SAR activation. If a test transmission on 121.5 MHz is necessary, it must be done in the first 5 minutes of each hour, for no more than 3 seconds. This narrow window exists because satellites monitor 121.5 MHz and a test outside this window can trigger unnecessary alerts.
exam_tip: ELT test: use built-in self-test function — do NOT actually transmit a live signal. If 121.5 MHz test required: first 5 minutes of the hour, no more than 3 seconds. Tests generate false SAR if done incorrectly.
key_facts: ELT testing procedures in AIP GEN
Use manufacturer self-test function — no live 406 MHz transmission
If 121.5 MHz test required: first 5 minutes of each hour only
121.5 MHz test duration: no more than 3 seconds
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP GEN

syllabus_desc: State the procedures to be followed on receiving an ELT signal. AIP GEN
concept: AIP GEN specifies what pilots should do if they receive an ELT signal in flight. On receiving the characteristic 'warbling' tone of an ELT on 121.5 MHz: note the time, your position, heading, altitude, and the bearing of the signal if you have direction-finding capability. Contact ATC or the nearest ATS unit and report the signal. Continue monitoring on 121.5 MHz to see if the signal continues. If you have DF capability, you may be asked to home toward the signal and give ATC position updates. RCCNZ will determine whether to initiate a SAR response. Don't ignore ELT signals — even if they're often inadvertent activations, the cost of ignoring a genuine distress is too high.
exam_tip: ELT signal received: note position/time/bearing, contact ATC immediately, continue monitoring. Report everything. Don't ignore it — it could be a genuine distress.
key_facts: On receiving ELT signal: note time, position, heading, altitude, bearing
Contact ATC/ATS immediately and report the signal
Continue monitoring 121.5 MHz; provide position updates if DF available
ATC/RCCNZ determines whether to initiate SAR
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP GEN

syllabus_desc: Interpret information on SID and Departure Procedure charts
concept: SID (Standard Instrument Departure) and departure procedure charts contain: the aerodrome plan view with the departure route depicted; altitude/height restrictions at specified fixes or distances; the climb gradient required (or if the standard 3.3% applies); navigation aid frequencies; transition fixes where the SID terminates and you join the enroute structure; and any notes about special procedures (e.g. noise abatement, operational restrictions). To interpret a SID chart: trace the departure track from the runway, note each constraint (cross at or above, cross at or below, speed restrictions), and identify the termination point and its altitude before joining your cleared route.
exam_tip: SID charts are read track-following from runway through to the en-route fix. Key items: climb constraints (at or above vs at or below), gradient requirements, and where the SID terminates.
key_facts: SID/Departure charts in AIPNZ Vol 2/3
Show departure track, altitude/speed constraints, climb gradients
Include nav aid frequencies and SID termination point
Read track-following from runway to enroute junction
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05, AIPNZ Vol 2

syllabus_desc: Determine the IFR take-off minima for a departure off a given runway. AIP ENR
concept: IFR take-off minima for each runway are published in the AIP AD 2 (Operational Data) pages for that aerodrome. To determine the applicable minima: locate the aerodrome's AD 2 pages, find the runway in use, and read the published ceiling and visibility minima for that runway direction. Take-off minima may differ between runway directions (e.g. one direction has terrain close in, requiring higher minima). If the aerodrome has reduced minima approved (under CAR rule 91.413(g)), these will also be noted on the chart.
exam_tip: IFR take-off minima come from the AIP AD 2 pages for the specific runway. Different runway directions may have different minima. If nothing is published: default is ceiling 300 ft, visibility 1500 m.
key_facts: IFR take-off minima in AIP AD 2 for each aerodrome/runway
May differ by runway direction due to terrain differences
Default if not published: ceiling 300 ft, visibility >1500 m (AIP ENR)
Reduced minima (0 ceiling/800 m vis) published if approved
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05, AIP AD

syllabus_desc: State the IFR take-off minima if it is not prescribed in the AIPNZ, VOL 2 & 3. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.05 specifies that if IFR take-off minima are not published in the AIP AD 2 for the runway being used, the default minima apply: ceiling at least 300 ft and visibility greater than 1500 m. These defaults are a reasonable minimum that allows the pilot to maintain visual terrain clearance during the departure climb until reaching the first applicable MSA or procedure minimum altitude. If conditions are below these defaults and no published minima are available, the departure cannot legally proceed under IFR.
exam_tip: No published minima: default is ceiling 300 ft, visibility >1500 m. This is the AIP ENR fallback — know this number cold.
key_facts: Default IFR take-off minima if not published: ceiling 300 ft, visibility >1500 m
Reference: AIP ENR 1.05
Applies when no minima are in the AIP AD 2 for the runway
Cannot depart IFR below these defaults when no published alternative exists
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: State the CAR Part 91 requirements and limitations of IFR reduced take-off minima. CAR Part 91 & AIP ENR
concept: CAR rule 91.413(g) and AIP ENR 1.05 allow reduced take-off minima: zero cloud ceiling with visibility at or above 800 m, provided ALL of the following conditions apply: the runway has centreline marking or centreline lighting; the pilot confirms take-off weather visibility by observing the centreline marking or lighting; the AD 2 specifically allows reduced minima for that runway; any obstacles in the departure path have been accounted for; and for twin-engine propeller aeroplanes specifically, the aircraft must have operative auto-feather and auto-coarse systems. If any of these conditions are not met, reduced minima cannot be used.
exam_tip: Reduced take-off minima = 0 ceiling, 800 m vis. Five conditions must ALL be met: centreline marking/lighting, pilot visual confirmation, AD 2 authorisation, obstacle consideration, and twin-props need auto-feather/coarse.
key_facts: Reduced take-off minima conditions in CAR rule 91.413(g) and AIP ENR
Reduced: zero ceiling, visibility ≥800 m
Requires: centreline marking/lighting, AD 2 authorisation, pilot visual confirmation
Twin-engine propeller aircraft: auto-feather and auto-coarse must be operative
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91 rule 91.413(g), AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: State the minimum height for a turn after take-off on departure. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.05 states the minimum height for a turn after take-off on departure: 400 ft above the departure end of runway (DER) before commencing a turn. This applies unless the SID or departure procedure specifies a higher altitude for the turn, or unless ATC assigns a different altitude. The 400 ft AGL figure exists to ensure the aircraft has adequate performance margin and terrain clearance before beginning a turning manoeuvre. For helicopters, the minimum is 295 ft above DER.
exam_tip: Minimum turn height after take-off: 400 ft above DER (runway end). 295 ft for helicopters. This is the default — SID or ATC can specify higher.
key_facts: Minimum turn height after take-off: 400 ft above departure end of runway
Reference: AIP ENR 1.05
Helicopter minimum: 295 ft above DER
SID or ATC assignment may specify a higher turn altitude
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: State the minimum climb gradient on a SID unless otherwise specified. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.05 specifies that the default (standard) minimum climb gradient on a SID is 3.3% (200 ft per NM). If the SID requires a higher climb gradient due to terrain or procedure design, it will be published on the chart. For helicopters, the standard is 5.0% (300 ft per NM). The gradient is net gradient — meaning it's already reduced from gross performance by a safety margin that accounts for normal performance variations. If your aircraft can't meet the published gradient (e.g. because of temperature, weight, or a failed engine), you cannot fly that SID.
exam_tip: Default SID climb gradient: 3.3% = 200 ft/NM for aeroplanes. 5.0% = 300 ft/NM for helicopters. If SID publishes a higher gradient, you must meet it. Can't meet it? Don't fly that SID.
key_facts: Standard SID minimum climb gradient: 3.3% (200 ft/NM) per AIP ENR 1.05
Helicopter standard: 5.0% (300 ft/NM)
Higher gradients published on chart if required by procedure
Cannot fly SID if aircraft cannot meet published gradient
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: Calculate the rate of climb required to meet the net climb gradient specified on instrument departures. AIP ENR
concept: To calculate the rate of climb required to meet a specified net climb gradient, you multiply the gradient (expressed as ft/NM) by the aircraft's groundspeed and divide by 60. Formula: Rate of Climb (ft/min) = Gradient (ft/NM) × Groundspeed (knots) / 60. Example: a 200 ft/NM gradient at 120 kt groundspeed = 200 × 120 / 60 = 400 ft/min. If the gradient is expressed as a percentage, convert first: 3.3% ≈ 200 ft/NM (multiply percent by 60 to get approximate ft/NM). Check this against your aircraft's performance charts to verify compliance.
exam_tip: ROC (ft/min) = gradient (ft/NM) × groundspeed (kt) ÷ 60. At 120 kt and 200 ft/NM gradient: 200 × 120 ÷ 60 = 400 ft/min. Exam questions often give you the gradient and speed and ask for the required ROC.
key_facts: Rate of climb calculation: gradient (ft/NM) × groundspeed (kt) ÷ 60
3.3% gradient ≈ 200 ft/NM
Example: 200 ft/NM at 120 kt = 400 ft/min required
Reference: AIP ENR 1.05
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: State when a departure procedure terminates. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.05 specifies when a departure procedure terminates. A SID terminates when the aircraft reaches the terminal fix specified in the procedure — typically a designated waypoint or fix at the boundary of the terminal area where the aircraft joins the enroute structure. Where the SID specifies a climb to a minimum altitude before proceeding, that altitude must be achieved before the procedure terminates. In some cases, the SID terminates when the aircraft reaches a specified track and altitude combination. ATC may also state 'SID cancelled' and issue direct routing to a fix, which terminates the SID at that point.
exam_tip: SID terminates at the specified terminal fix (waypoint), or when ATC cancels it and issues direct routing. Must meet any altitude requirements first.
key_facts: SID terminates at the published terminal fix/waypoint per AIP ENR 1.05
Minimum altitudes must be achieved before the SID is considered complete
ATC may cancel SID and issue direct routing at any point
On reaching the terminal fix, join cleared enroute route
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: State the limitation on the termination of radar vectoring for a departing IFR aircraft. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.05 specifies the limitation on termination of radar vectoring for departing IFR aircraft. Radar vectoring of a departing IFR aircraft may not be terminated until the aircraft is established on: the evaluated route in the climb sector; or climbing above the VORSEC chart steps; or under radar control at or above the minimum radar terrain contour level; or at or above the approved area MSA. The reason is that radar vectoring provides terrain clearance assurance — you can't simply cancel vectoring and tell the pilot to navigate themselves if they're still below the MSA in terrain.
exam_tip: Radar vectoring of departing IFR can't end until aircraft is established on: evaluated route, VORSEC steps, minimum radar terrain contour level, or area MSA. All four are options — just need one.
key_facts: Radar vectoring termination limitation in AIP ENR 1.05
Vectoring cannot cease until aircraft is: on evaluated route, above VORSEC steps, at min radar terrain contour, or at area MSA
Ensures terrain clearance before pilot resumes own navigation
Controller must confirm one of the four conditions before cancelling vectors
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for broadcasting intentions when departing from an unattended aerodrome. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.01 and 1.05 specify that pilots departing from unattended aerodromes under IFR must broadcast their intentions on the published frequency before taxiing and before entering the runway. The broadcasts allow any VFR traffic in the circuit or in the vicinity of the aerodrome to hear your intentions and take avoiding action. The standard format is: 'AERODROME TRAFFIC [callsign] [position] [intentions] [aerodrome name]'. This is the same as the standard unattended aerodrome broadcast format. For IFR departures, it's particularly important because you may be entering IMC shortly after take-off when VFR traffic still in the circuit can't see you.
exam_tip: IFR departure from unattended aerodrome: broadcast intentions before taxiing and before entering runway. Same format as standard unattended aerodrome broadcasts. Keeps VFR traffic aware.
key_facts: Broadcast requirements in AIP ENR 1.01 for unattended aerodrome departures
Broadcast before taxiing and before entering runway
Standard format: callsign, position, intentions, aerodrome name
Ensures VFR circuit traffic awareness before you enter IMC
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.01, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for and limitations on a visual departure. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.05 specifies that a visual departure is permitted by day only, where the pilot maintains visual reference to terrain throughout and can see and avoid obstacles. The pilot must maintain terrain clearance visually to the applicable MSA (enroute descent steps, VORSEC chart steps, or PBN SID segment MSA) or to a specified upper limit. The minimum net climb gradient still applies (3.3% / 200 ft per NM). A visual departure is used when a published IFR departure procedure isn't available but conditions are VMC — the pilot departs visually and transitions to IFR once at or above the applicable MSA.
exam_tip: Visual departure: day only, pilot maintains terrain clearance visually to applicable MSA. 3.3% climb gradient still required. Used when no published IFR procedure is available but conditions are VMC.
key_facts: Visual departure requirements in AIP ENR 1.05
Day only; pilot maintains visual reference to terrain throughout
Must maintain terrain clearance visually to applicable MSA
Standard 3.3% climb gradient still applies
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: Describe the operating restrictions where an IFR departure procedure is not promulgated. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.05 states that where no IFR departure procedure is published for an aerodrome, the pilot must ensure that the aircraft's climb performance is adequate to provide obstacle clearance before reaching the applicable minimum safe altitude. In practice, this means: calculate the aircraft's actual net climb gradient from the available performance charts, determine the obstacle environment from the appropriate charts, and confirm that the aircraft will be above the MSA before reaching any obstacle. If performance is insufficient (heavy aircraft, high density altitude, obstacles close in), the departure must not be made under IFR unless conditions allow a visual departure.
exam_tip: No published IFR departure procedure: PIC must verify climb performance is sufficient for obstacle clearance to MSA. This is a pilot responsibility check — not ATC's problem.
key_facts: No published IFR departure: pilot must verify adequate climb performance
Reference: AIP ENR 1.05
Calculate net climb gradient vs obstacle environment
Must reach MSA before encountering any obstacles
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: State the maximum speed in enroute holding patterns. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.05 sets maximum holding speeds for enroute holding patterns. At 14,000 ft and below: 230 kt IAS (or 170 kt for Cat A and B aircraft only). Between 14,000 ft and 20,000 ft: 240 kt IAS. Between 20,000 ft and 34,000 ft: 265 kt IAS. Above 34,000 ft: Mach 0.83. In turbulent conditions, all limits below 34,000 ft increase to the lesser of 280 kt or Mach 0.8. Helicopters: 100 kt IAS at or below 6,000 ft, 170 kt above 6,000 ft.
exam_tip: Enroute holding speeds: ≤14,000 ft = 230 kt; 14-20,000 ft = 240 kt; 20-34,000 ft = 265 kt; >34,000 ft = M0.83. Turbulence: 280 kt or M0.8, whichever is less. These numbers are essential exam material.
key_facts: Holding speeds in AIP ENR 1.05
≤14,000 ft: 230 kt IAS maximum
Turbulence: lesser of 280 kt or M0.8 at all altitudes up to 34,000 ft
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: State the maximum entry and holding pattern speeds. AIP ENR
concept: Entry into holding patterns must also be flown within the published holding speeds. The same speed limits that apply during the hold also apply during the entry phase. At 14,000 ft and below: 230 kt (170 kt Cat A/B). The outbound timing depends on altitude: 1 minute outbound at 14,000 ft and below; 1.5 minutes outbound above 14,000 ft. The purpose is that both the entry and holding segments are designed with these speed limits in mind — if you exceed them, the protected airspace may be exceeded and obstacle clearance may not be assured.
exam_tip: Entry speeds = same as holding speeds. Outbound timing: 1 minute at ≤14,000 ft; 1.5 minutes above 14,000 ft. Both are needed to fly the protected holding pattern.
key_facts: Holding entry speeds same as holding pattern speeds per AIP ENR 1.05
Outbound leg timing: 1 minute at ≤14,000 ft; 1.5 minutes above 14,000 ft
Speed limits ensure aircraft stays within the designed protected airspace
If speed limits exceeded, obstacle clearance may not be assured
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: Identify and describe appropriate holding pattern entry procedures. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.05 specifies three holding pattern entry procedures based on the aircraft's heading relative to the holding fix. The holding fix has three entry sectors. Sector 1 (parallel entry): aircraft heading within 70° of the outbound holding track (on the non-holding side) — turn to the reciprocal of the inbound track, fly for the outbound time, then turn back to intercept the inbound track. Sector 2 (offset/teardrop entry): aircraft heading within 110° on the holding side — turn 30° from the reciprocal of inbound track toward the holding side, fly for appropriate time, then turn to intercept inbound. Sector 3 (direct entry): remaining headings — turn directly in the holding direction to follow the holding pattern.
exam_tip: Three entries: parallel (Sector 1, non-holding side), teardrop/offset (Sector 2, holding side, 30° outbound), direct (Sector 3, everything else). The sectors are defined by the aircraft's heading relative to the inbound track.
key_facts: Three holding entries per AIP ENR 1.05
Sector 1 (parallel): fly reciprocal of inbound for 1/1.5 min, turn to intercept
Sector 2 (teardrop): fly 30° offset on holding side, turn to intercept inbound
Sector 3 (direct): turn in holding direction and follow the pattern
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: State when an onwards clearance time will be passed to the pilots of an aircraft instructed to hold enroute. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR specifies when ATC passes an onwards clearance time to aircraft holding enroute (not at an initial approach fix, but at an enroute holding fix). An onwards clearance time (OCT) is passed when the aircraft's delay in holding would exceed 5 minutes — essentially giving the pilot a specific time at which they should leave the holding pattern and continue. This allows the pilot to plan holding fuel requirements and to time their departure from the hold to arrive at the next fix at the right time.
exam_tip: Onwards clearance time (for enroute holding): passed when delay exceeds 5 minutes. This is the 'you can leave at HH:MM' time. Don't confuse with Expected Approach Time which is for holding at an IAF.
key_facts: Onwards clearance time passed when enroute holding delay exceeds 5 minutes
Reference: AIP ENR
Allows pilot to plan fuel and timing for departure from hold
Distinct from expected approach time (which applies at an IAF)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR

syllabus_desc: State when an expected approach time will be passed to the pilots of an aircraft instructed to hold at an initial approach fix. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.05 specifies that an expected approach time (EAT) will be passed to the pilot of an aircraft instructed to hold at an Initial Approach Fix (IAF) when the delay will exceed 5 minutes. The EAT is the time at which the holding aircraft can expect to leave the IAF and commence the approach. This allows the pilot to configure for the approach in advance, plan fuel, and advise ATC if the EAT is not operationally acceptable. If the delay is less than 5 minutes, ATC may simply sequence you in without issuing a formal EAT.
exam_tip: Expected approach time (EAT): passed when IAF delay exceeds 5 minutes. Tells pilot when they can expect to start the approach. Must advise ATC if unable to accept the EAT.
key_facts: Expected approach time passed when IAF holding delay exceeds 5 minutes
Reference: AIP ENR 1.05
Allows pilot to plan approach configuration and fuel in advance
Pilot must advise ATC if unable to accept the EAT
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: State the angle of bank required during turns in a holding pattern. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.05 specifies the angle of bank required in holding patterns. The pilot must use whichever requires the LESSER bank angle: 25° bank, OR a 3° per second rate of turn. For most aircraft at typical holding speeds, 3°/second requires more than 25° bank, so 25° is the operative limit. At slow speed (light Cat A aircraft at 90 kt), 3°/second may require less than 25° bank, in which case 3°/sec applies. The intent is to ensure turns stay within the protected airspace for the holding pattern.
exam_tip: Holding bank: 25° OR 3°/sec, whichever requires LESS bank. Not 'whichever is lower' — it's whichever requires the smaller bank angle based on your speed. For most IFR aircraft, 25° is the operative limit.
key_facts: Holding bank angle: 25° OR 3°/second — whichever requires the lesser bank
Reference: AIP ENR 1.05
At high speeds 25° applies; at very low speeds 3°/sec may apply
Ensures turns stay within the protected holding airspace
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: Describe the descent limitations from cruise to approach commencement. AIP GEN
concept: AIP ENR 1.05 specifies descent limitations from cruise to approach commencement. A STAR level requirement does NOT authorise descent to meet that requirement — ATC will assign descent to permit compliance, or advise otherwise. You must not descend below your cruise level to comply with STAR altitude requirements without an ATC clearance to descend.
