In practice, ATC manages the sequencing and descent clearances to ensure you arrive at approach commencement altitude at the right time and position. If you cannot meet a level requirement on the STAR even with ATC clearance, advise ATC immediately.
exam_tip: STAR altitude requirement does NOT authorise descent — you still need an explicit ATC descent clearance. This is a frequently tested point: STAR does not equal clearance to descend.
key_facts: STAR level requirements do not authorise descent per AIP ENR 1.05
ATC must issue explicit descent clearance
Advise ATC if STAR level requirement cannot be met
ATC manages sequencing and descent clearances
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: Interpret information on STAR charts
concept: STAR (Standard Arrival Route) charts contain: the arrival route from the enroute structure to the IAF; altitude/speed constraints at specified fixes; the transitions available (which STARs connect which enroute fixes to which approaches); and the terminal routing. To interpret a STAR chart: trace the route from the enroute entry fix, note each altitude constraint (at or above, at or below, or specific altitude), note speed constraints (e.g. 250 kt at or below FL100), and identify the IAF where the STAR terminates and the instrument approach procedure begins.
exam_tip: STAR charts connect the enroute structure to the IAF. Read them track-following, noting altitude and speed constraints at each fix. The STAR ends at the IAF where the approach procedure starts.
key_facts: STAR charts in AIPNZ Vol 2/3
Connect enroute structure to IAF with altitude and speed constraints
Read track-following from enroute entry fix to IAF
STAR terminates at IAF — approach procedure starts there
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIPNZ Vol 2

syllabus_desc: State the limitation on a clearance to fly a STAR. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.05 specifies the limitation on clearances to fly a STAR: receiving a STAR clearance does NOT constitute clearance for the instrument approach. You must receive a separate approach clearance before commencing the approach procedure. Additionally, if you cannot fly the STAR as published because a required navigation aid is unserviceable or unavailable, you must advise ATC. You cannot fly a STAR that requires a navigation aid you don't have, without ATC providing an alternative routing.
exam_tip: STAR clearance ≠ approach clearance. You need both, separately. Also: if a required nav aid is unserviceable, tell ATC — you can't fly that STAR.
key_facts: STAR clearance does NOT constitute approach clearance per AIP ENR 1.05
Separate approach clearance required before commencing approach
Advise ATC if required nav aid is unserviceable
ATC must provide alternative routing if STAR nav aid unavailable
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: Define the minimum initial approach altitude. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.05 defines the minimum initial approach altitude as the HIGHER of: the minimum procedure commencement altitude shown on the instrument approach chart (the altitude above which the approach procedure begins); OR the MSA for the route sector from which the approach is being commenced (MFA, VORSEC step, or 25nm MSA diagram figure). Using the higher of the two ensures that you're above the applicable terrain clearance altitude before starting the approach, regardless of which approach is being flown.
exam_tip: Minimum initial approach altitude = higher of (procedure commencement altitude on chart) OR (MSA for route sector). Always use the greater of the two. This is the lowest you can be when the approach procedure starts.
key_facts: Minimum initial approach altitude: higher of chart procedure altitude or route sector MSA
Reference: AIP ENR 1.05
Ensures terrain clearance before approach procedure begins
MSA source: MFA, VORSEC step, or 25nm MSA diagram
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: Interpret information on instrument approach charts
concept: Instrument approach charts contain three main sections: the plan view (overhead view showing the approach track, IAF, FAF, turns, navigation aids, and terrain/obstructions); the profile view (side view showing altitude at each segment — initial approach altitude, intermediate altitude, step-down fixes if applicable, and the approach slope gradient); and the minima box (the DA/DH or MDA/MDH and visibility minimums for each approach category and procedure type). Additional information: missed approach procedure, altimeter setting source, communications frequencies, chart notes, and date of issue.
exam_tip: Three sections: plan view (overhead), profile view (altitudes), minima box (DA/MDA and visibility). The minima box has different rows for different aircraft categories and approach types.
key_facts: Approach chart structure: plan view, profile view, minima box
Plan view: approach track, IAF, FAF, nav aids
Profile view: segment altitudes, approach gradient
Minima box: DA/MDA, visibility by aircraft category and approach type
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIPNZ Vol 2

syllabus_desc: Determine the IFR meteorological minima for an instrument approach to a given runway
concept: IFR meteorological minima for an instrument approach are determined from the approach chart minima box. The minima depend on: the approach type (precision = DA/DH, non-precision = MDA/MDH, circling = different MDA); the aircraft category (A, B, C, D, based on Vs); and any special conditions (night, wet runway, visual aids not available). To determine the applicable minima: find the aircraft category (based on Vs0 × 1.3), find the appropriate approach type row in the minima box, and read off the DA/MDA and visibility. For NZ, the minima reflect the approach aid available and any local variations.
exam_tip: Minima come from the approach chart minima box. Three inputs: aircraft category, approach type (precision/non-precision/circling), and visual aid availability. Know aircraft category boundaries: A <91 kt, B 91-120, C 121-140, D 141-165.
key_facts: IFR approach minima from approach chart minima box
Depends on: aircraft category, approach type, visual aid availability
Precision: DA/DH; Non-precision: MDA/MDH; Circling: separate MDA
Category based on Vs × 1.3 — A <91 kt, B 91-120, C 121-140, D 141-165 kt
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: State the meteorological minima which must exist prior to a landing off an instrument approach. CAR Part 91 & AIP ENR
concept: CAR rule 91.413 and AIP ENR 1.05 specify that before landing off an instrument approach, at the decision point (DA or MAP), ALL of the following must be met: (1) the aircraft must be continuously in a position from which descent to landing can be made at a normal rate of descent; (2) the flight visibility must not be less than the published visibility for the procedure; and (3) at least one of the specified visual references must be distinctly visible and identifiable. If any of these conditions are not met, a missed approach must be initiated immediately.
exam_tip: Three conditions ALL required before landing: (1) in position to descend normally, (2) flight visibility ≥ published minimum, (3) at least one specific visual reference visible. All three, not just one.
key_facts: Landing conditions per CAR rule 91.413 and AIP ENR 1.05
Must be in position to make normal descent to landing
Flight visibility not less than published procedure minimum
At least one specified visual reference distinctly visible and identifiable
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91 rule 91.413, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: Describe the procedures for joining overhead a navigation aid for an instrument approach. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.05 describes the procedure for joining overhead a nav aid for an instrument approach. The overhead join involves flying over the nav aid (typically a VOR or NDB) at or above the initial approach altitude, then turning outbound on the appropriate track, timing the outbound leg, turning inbound, and tracking to the fix for final approach. This procedure is used when no STAR is published and direct vectoring isn't available. The pilot must confirm the nav aid identification before commencing the procedure, track accurately, and maintain the published altitudes for each segment.
exam_tip: Overhead join: fly over the nav aid at initial approach altitude, outbound on procedure track, turn inbound. Confirm nav aid ID before starting. This is the classic full procedural approach used at remote aerodromes.
key_facts: Overhead join procedure in AIP ENR 1.05
Fly over nav aid at or above initial approach altitude
Outbound on procedure track for prescribed time
Turn inbound and track to nav aid/FAF for final approach
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: State the minimum meteorological conditions which must exist before ATC may clear an aircraft for an instrument approach with a descent restriction. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.05 section specifies that before ATC may clear an aircraft for an instrument approach with a descent restriction (i.e. an approach where ATC is telling you 'do not descend below X until established'), the reported or known cloud base must be at least 1000 ft above the altitude specified in the descent restriction, and visibility must be at least 8 km. This protects the situation where ATC is restricting descent for traffic reasons — the aircraft needs to have enough VMC to safely hold the restriction and transition to the approach.
exam_tip: Descent restriction approach clearance: cloud base ≥1000 ft above restriction altitude AND vis ≥8 km. Both required. This is the ATC standard before issuing that type of clearance.
key_facts: Conditions for approach with descent restriction in AIP ENR 1.05
Cloud base at least 1000 ft above the descent restriction altitude
Visibility at least 8 km
Both conditions must be met before ATC may issue the clearance
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: State the meteorological and other conditions which will allow a pilot to request a visual approach in controlled airspace. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.05 specifies conditions under which a pilot may REQUEST a visual approach in controlled airspace. The pilot can request a visual approach when: the aerodrome is in sight, OR the pilot can maintain visual reference to terrain; AND visibility is at least 16 km; AND the reported ceiling is not below the approved initial approach level for the aerodrome. ATC will also need the separation picture to allow a visual approach. The pilot must specifically request it — ATC won't offer it unprompted unless conditions allow and traffic permits.
exam_tip: To REQUEST visual approach in controlled airspace: vis ≥16 km, ceiling not below initial approach level, aerodrome/terrain in sight. Must specifically request it.
key_facts: Pilot request conditions for visual approach in controlled airspace: AIP ENR 1.05
Visibility at least 16 km
Reported ceiling not below approved initial approach level
Aerodrome in sight or visual reference to terrain maintained
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: State the meteorological and other conditions which allow ATC to advise that conditions are suitable for a visual approach. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.05 specifies conditions under which ATC may advise that conditions are suitable for a visual approach (i.e. ATC initiates the offer). This is the same as the pilot request conditions: visibility at least 16 km (by day) and ceiling at least 1000 ft above the applicable minimum radar vectoring altitude. At night at specific approved aerodromes, the conditions are visibility ≥16 km and no cloud below 5000 ft. ATC won't make this offer unless they're confident the pilot will have a safe visual segment for the approach.
exam_tip: ATC advising conditions suitable for visual approach: same conditions as pilot request. Day: vis ≥16 km, ceiling ≥1000 ft above MRVA. Night: approved aerodromes only, vis ≥16 km, no cloud below 5000 ft.
key_facts: ATC visual approach offer conditions: AIP ENR 1.05
Day: visibility ≥16 km, ceiling ≥1000 ft above minimum radar vectoring altitude
Night: approved aerodromes only; vis ≥16 km; no cloud below 5000 ft
ATC only offers when confident pilot will have safe visual segment
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: State the meteorological and other conditions which will allow a pilot to carry out a visual approach in uncontrolled airspace. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.05 specifies conditions for a visual approach in uncontrolled airspace. For an IFR flight in Class G airspace, the pilot may carry out a visual approach when: the aerodrome is in sight; the pilot can maintain visual reference to terrain throughout; flight visibility is sufficient for safe navigation; and conditions allow safe integration with any circuit traffic. The pilot maintains full responsibility for terrain clearance and see-and-avoid. There's no ATC service providing separation, so the pilot must remain vigilant for other traffic, particularly VFR aircraft which may be in the circuit.
exam_tip: Visual approach in uncontrolled airspace: pilot's own judgement on visibility, terrain in sight, and integration with circuit traffic. No ATC — full pilot responsibility.
key_facts: Visual approach in uncontrolled airspace conditions: AIP ENR 1.05
Aerodrome in sight; visual reference to terrain maintained throughout
Sufficient visibility for safe navigation
Pilot responsible for terrain clearance and separation from circuit traffic
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: Describe the provision of separation and terrain clearance during a visual approach. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.05 specifies separation and terrain clearance during a visual approach. In controlled airspace, once a pilot reports 'runway in sight' or 'aerodrome in sight', ATC may transfer responsibility for terrain clearance and separation from other visual approach aircraft to the pilot ('your discretion to descend, maintain own separation from [traffic]'). ATC continues to provide separation from IFR traffic not conducting visual approaches. In uncontrolled airspace, the pilot is always responsible for own terrain clearance and see-and-avoid. A visual approach in Class G means operating under the see-and-avoid principle regardless of the IFR flight rules being followed.
exam_tip: Visual approach in controlled airspace: ATC may hand terrain clearance and separation from other visual aircraft to you. ATC keeps IFR separation. In Class G: always pilot's responsibility.
key_facts: Visual approach separation in AIP ENR 1.05
Controlled airspace: ATC may transfer terrain clearance/visual separation to pilot
ATC continues IFR separation for non-visual approach traffic
Class G: pilot always responsible for terrain clearance and see-and-avoid
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: Given an aircraft's Vs, determine its category for approach speeds and minima. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.05 specifies the aircraft categories for approach speeds and minima based on Vs (stall speed in landing configuration at maximum certified landing weight). Category A: Vs less than 91 kt IAS. Category B: Vs 91 to 120 kt. Category C: Vs 121 to 140 kt. Category D: Vs 141 to 165 kt. Category E: Vs 166 to 210 kt. To determine your aircraft's category: multiply Vs0 (or Vso at MLW) by 1.3 and use the result to find the category. The category determines approach speeds for procedure turns, initial approach, final approach, and circling, as well as the applicable IFR minima.
exam_tip: Aircraft category = Vs × 1.3. A <91, B 91-120, C 121-140, D 141-165, E 166-210. Your category determines which row of the minima box applies. If Vs ×1.3 puts you in Cat B but you're flying faster, you may need to use Cat C minima.
key_facts: Aircraft categories for approaches per AIP ENR 1.05
Based on Vs (stall speed in landing config at max landing weight)
Category determines applicable approach speeds and minima
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: State the category A and B speed limitations during an instrument approach under ICAO PANS OPS II procedures. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.05 publishes approach speed limits for Cat A and B aircraft under ICAO PANS-OPS II procedures. Category A: initial approach 90-150 kt; procedure turn/reversal max 110 kt; final approach 70-100 kt; visual manoeuvring (circling) 100 kt max. Category B: initial approach 120-180 kt; procedure turn/reversal max 150 kt; final approach 85-130 kt; visual manoeuvring max 135 kt. These speeds ensure the aircraft stays within the protected airspace designed for each segment. If you exceed them, you may fly outside the protected area and lose obstacle clearance.
exam_tip: Cat A final approach: 70-100 kt. Cat B final approach: 85-130 kt. Cat A max for reversal: 110 kt. Cat B max for reversal: 150 kt. Circling: Cat A 100 kt, Cat B 135 kt.
key_facts: Cat A/B approach speeds in AIP ENR 1.05 per ICAO PANS-OPS
Cat A: initial 90-150, reversal max 110, final 70-100, circling max 100 kt
Cat B: initial 120-180, reversal max 150, final 85-130, circling max 135 kt
Exceeding speed limits may take aircraft outside protected airspace
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for making position reports during an instrument approach in controlled and uncontrolled airspace. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.05 specifies position reporting during instrument approaches. In controlled airspace: unless under radar service and not requested, position reports are required at the IAF when crossing it, when established on final approach, and when commencing missed approach. In uncontrolled airspace (including unattended aerodromes): mandatory broadcast on the MBZ/CTAF frequency at the IAF, when turning inbound/base, when established on final, and when on approach. These broadcasts are the IFR equivalent of the standard unattended aerodrome position calls — they allow any listening traffic to build a mental picture of where you are.
exam_tip: Controlled airspace: report at IAF, established on final, commencing missed approach (if not under radar). Uncontrolled/unattended: broadcast same points on MBZ/CTAF frequency.
key_facts: Approach position reports required at: IAF crossing, established on final, commencing missed approach
In controlled airspace: unless under radar and not required by ATC
Unattended aerodrome: mandatory broadcasts on MBZ/CTAF at same positions
Reference: AIP ENR 1.05
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: Describe the procedures for carrying out an instrument approach at an unattended aerodrome. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.05 specifies the procedure for instrument approaches at unattended aerodromes. The pilot must: broadcast intentions on the published MBZ/CTAF frequency before commencing the approach; ensure the PAL is on if the approach is at night; restrict descent in IMC to 1200 ft above aerodrome elevation if traffic conflict may exist; follow the published approach procedure exactly; monitor the circuit frequency for any conflicting VFR traffic; and make position broadcasts at the IAF, on final, and on missed approach. The pilot bears full responsibility for separation from circuit traffic — there's no ATC.
exam_tip: Unattended aerodrome IFR approach: broadcast on MBZ/CTAF, activate PAL at night, don't descend below 1200 ft AGL in IMC if traffic conflict possible, monitor circuit frequency throughout.
key_facts: Unattended aerodrome approach procedure in AIP ENR 1.05
Broadcast intentions on MBZ/CTAF before commencing approach
Restrict IMC descent to 1200 ft above aerodrome if traffic conflict may exist
Pilot responsible for separation from circuit traffic — no ATC
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: Determine the minimum descent altitude using a QNH from a remote location. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.05 describes how to calculate minimum descent altitude when using a QNH from a remote location (i.e. not from the destination aerodrome). When the destination aerodrome has no weather reporting, you may use the area QNH from the nearest ATS unit or weather station. The adjustment is: take the published approach MDA (referenced to aerodrome elevation), add the aerodrome elevation to get the figure in feet AMSL, then apply the remote QNH to get the altimeter reading. The remote QNH may be slightly different from aerodrome QNH, so the MDA needs to be adjusted accordingly. The AIP specifies the maximum distance from which a remote QNH may be used.
exam_tip: Remote QNH for MDA: convert MDA from AGL to AMSL using aerodrome elevation, then apply the remote QNH correction. Maximum allowable remote QNH distance is published in the AIP.
key_facts: Remote QNH MDA calculation in AIP ENR 1.05
Convert published MDA (AGL) to AMSL using aerodrome elevation
Apply remote QNH correction for the pressure difference
Maximum allowable distance for remote QNH use specified in AIP
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: State when descent below decision altitude or minimum descent altitude may be made on an instrument approach. AIP ENR
concept: CAR rule 91.413 and AIP ENR 1.05 specify exactly when descent below DA or MDA is permitted during an instrument approach. At the decision point, you may descend below DA/MDA ONLY if: (1) the aircraft is continuously in a position from which descent to landing can be made at a normal rate of descent; (2) the flight visibility is not less than the visibility published for the approach procedure; AND (3) at least ONE of the following visual references is distinctly visible and identifiable: approach lighting system, threshold markings or lights, runway-end identifier lights, visual approach slope indicator, touchdown zone markings or lights, runway or runway markings or lights. All three conditions must be met simultaneously.
exam_tip: Descent below DA/MDA: three conditions ALL met simultaneously. (1) In position for normal descent, (2) flight visibility not less than published, (3) at least one listed visual reference. Missing any one = missed approach.
key_facts: Descent below DA/MDA conditions in CAR rule 91.413 and AIP ENR 1.05
Must be in position for a normal descent to landing
Flight visibility not less than published procedure minimum
At least one visual reference visible: ALS, threshold lights/markings, REIL, VASIS/PAPI, TDZ, runway
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91 rule 91.413, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: Describe the missed approach procedures and limitations. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.05 specifies missed approach procedures and limitations. The published missed approach procedure must be executed when: at the missed approach point (MAP) the required visual reference is not established; during a circling approach, the identifiable part of the aerodrome is lost; or ATC directs a missed approach. The missed approach begins at the MAP and must be followed exactly unless ATC issues different instructions. Key limitations: minimum bank angle of 15° during turns; assumed missed approach climb gradient is 2.5% (150 ft/NM) for the procedure design. If you can't achieve 2.5%, continue climbing in the holding pattern to the published altitude. ATC may amend missed approach for training purposes under specific day/VMC conditions.
exam_tip: Execute missed approach when: visual reference lost at MAP, identifiable aerodrome lost during circling, or ATC directs. Minimum 15° bank in turns. Assumed gradient 2.5% = 150 ft/NM.
key_facts: Missed approach procedure in AIP ENR 1.05
Execute at MAP if visual reference not established, or on ATC instruction
Minimum 15° bank angle during turns
Assumed climb gradient 2.5% (150 ft/NM) for procedure design
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05

syllabus_desc: Describe the procedures required following a communications failure enroute. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.15 and AIP ENR 1.05 specify comms failure procedures enroute. On losing communications: squawk 7600; try all available frequencies (primary ATC frequency, guard 121.5 MHz, company freq, mobile phone if in range); try alternative equipment (second radio, satellite); transmit 'TRANSMITTING BLIND' on the last assigned frequency with your position and intentions — ATC may still be able to receive even if not transmit. If in controlled airspace, proceed by: maintaining last assigned altitude; following last assigned route; if in holding, continue to hold until expected approach time, then commence approach. ATC will clear airspace for your expected routing.
exam_tip: Comms failure enroute: 7600, try all frequencies, transmit blind, maintain last clearance. ATC will predict your routing and clear airspace. The key is: fly what you last planned, squawk 7600.
key_facts: Comms failure enroute: squawk 7600 immediately
Try all frequencies including 121.5 MHz guard; transmit BLIND on last ATC frequency
Maintain last assigned altitude and route
ATC clears airspace based on your expected routing
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.15

syllabus_desc: Describe the procedures required following a communications failure during an instrument approach. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.15 specifies comms failure procedures during an instrument approach. After losing comms on approach: continue the approach using the last clearance received; squawk 7600; track via the cleared routing to the destination nav aid or fix for the approach; commence descent to the initial approach altitude per the last acknowledged clearance; if given an EAT, time the approach to commence at the EAT; if being radar vectored at the time of failure, maintain the last assigned heading for 2 minutes then proceed to the IAF for the approach. Land at the destination — don't return to the departure aerodrome. ATC will protect the airspace for your expected approach time.
exam_tip: Comms failure during approach: squawk 7600, fly the last clearance, time to your EAT, and land. If being vectored when comms fail: hold last heading for 2 minutes, then proceed to IAF.
key_facts: Comms failure during approach: squawk 7600
Continue approach per last clearance received
If vectored at time of failure: last heading for 2 minutes, then proceed to IAF
Time approach to commence at EAT; land at destination
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.15

syllabus_desc: Describe the procedure to be carried out in the event of a radio navigation aid failure during an approach. AIP ENR
concept: AIP ENR 1.05 specifies the procedure if a radio navigation aid fails during an approach. If the approach can be continued using a suitable alternative aid or procedure, it may be continued provided the prevailing weather conditions are within the minima for the alternative procedure. In controlled airspace, advise ATC of your position and intentions immediately. If no alternative procedure is available, advise ATC so they can continue providing separation from other traffic while you sort out your options. If the approach cannot be continued safely, initiate a missed approach and advise ATC.
exam_tip: Nav aid fails on approach: alternative procedure if available and wx within minima. Notify ATC immediately in controlled airspace. No alternative? Missed approach and advise ATC.
key_facts: Nav aid failure during approach procedure in AIP ENR 1.05
May continue using alternative aid/procedure if wx meets alternative minima
Notify ATC of position and intentions immediately in controlled airspace
No alternative: initiate missed approach and advise ATC
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP ENR 1.05
